responses.answer: Multidiciplinary but mainly metal, textiles, silicone, but also painting and photographing

My artistic practice explores the body as a site of identity, desire, and social construction. Through jewellery, sculpture, and metalwork, I investigate how bodies are represented, controlled, celebrated, and remembered. I am interested in the tension between personal experience and the cultural structures that shape how we understand ourselves and others. Working with traditional craft techniques alongside contemporary approaches, I use materials such as metal, textiles, and silicone to create objects that explore the space between the beautiful and the grotesque. Where desire, vulnerability, and bodily transformation meet. My work often examines overlooked narratives and questions whose bodies, desires, and histories are given space within visual culture.

During this residency, I want to explore and develop the idea/series ” Women athletes are fucking hot!”, that I have carried with me for a long time. I want to use chasing to create a series of erotic lesbian scenes, capturing intimacy, desire and emotional connection. Through this technique, I want to make moments visible and freeze them in time creating images of what I feel and what I want to see.
Chasing is based on shaping metal in the most expressive way through its interaction with light. The surface is raised and lowered depending on where shadows are meant to appear, allowing the material to hold both softness and intensity. I primarily want to work with copper sheet, as the oxidation of copper creates a strong contrast against the highly polished surface. Through these works, I want to explore the intimate and the exposed, creating a physical archive of queer female desire and tenderness.

Graphite and charcoal drawing are my primary mediums, with a practice rooted in figurative and homoerotic portraiture. I also work with ink, colored pencil, acrylic paint, and mixed media, and I enjoy experimenting across materials while maintaining a strong emphasis on drawing.

I am a Sri Lankan artist whose practice explores queer identity, masculinity, vulnerability, and desire through homoerotic figurative art. Working primarily in graphite and charcoal, I create intimate portraits that challenge cultural taboos and celebrate the beauty of the male form. Growing up in a conservative environment has deeply shaped my perspective, inspiring work that reclaims visibility and tenderness for LGBTQ+ experiences. Alongside drawing, I continue to expand my practice through painting and mixed media while remaining rooted in strong draftsmanship. My work has been exhibited in Sri Lanka and Bali, where it has sparked conversations around sexuality, identity, and representation. Through my practice, I aim to create honest, emotionally resonant images that encourage connection, empathy, and a greater sense of belonging within queer communities.

Growing up in Sri Lanka, where queer lives have long existed under legal and social stigma, I had little access to LGBTQ+ history, art, or representation. The Tom of Finland Foundation archives offer something I never had: a living record of queer creativity, resilience, and desire. I want to study the original drawings, photographs, letters, and personal papers to better understand how artists before me documented queer life with honesty and pride. That research will deepen my own practice while helping me place my work within a broader history that I was never able to access growing up.

I first learned about the residency through photographer Richard Funston after modeling for Erotico magazine. During that experience, I learned about his connection to Tony Ward and the Tom of Finland Foundation. As someone whose work centers queer figurative drawing, I began exploring the Foundation’s mission and quickly realized how closely it aligned with my own artistic practice and the stories I hope to tell through my work.

Working Title: Soft Men: Brown Bodies, Tender Histories

During my residency, I plan to develop a new body of large-scale figurative drawings and mixed-media works exploring intimacy, tenderness, and queer identity among South Asian men. As a Sri Lankan artist, I rarely saw my own culture represented within queer art. Conversations around masculinity in South Asia are often shaped by silence, shame, and rigid expectations, leaving little room for vulnerability or affection between men.

My work seeks to reclaim those moments. Through graphite, painting, and mixed media, I want to portray brown male bodies not only as objects of desire but as people capable of softness, emotional openness, and genuine connection. By centering South Asian queer experiences, I hope to challenge stereotypes that often reduce our stories to secrecy or trauma while acknowledging the social and cultural realities many of us continue to navigate.

The Tom of Finland Foundation offers a unique environment to expand this project. Tom’s work radically affirmed queer sexuality and visibility at a time when both were heavily marginalized. My goal is not to replicate that visual language, but to continue its spirit through my own cultural perspective. I want to contribute a narrative that broadens the conversation around queer masculinity by introducing stories rooted in South Asian identity, migration, memory, and resilience.

During the residency, I plan to research the Foundation’s archives while experimenting with new materials, larger formats, and more ambitious compositions. The resulting body of work will form the basis of a future exhibition that invites viewers to see tenderness as an act of resistance and visibility as a form of liberation.
No special accessibility requirements. I would simply appreciate access to a well-lit studio space suitable for drawing and painting, along with basic art-making facilities.

As a queer artist from Sri Lanka, this residency would be a rare opportunity to develop work in an environment where queer expression is celebrated rather than constrained. I’m excited to contribute a South Asian perspective to the Foundation’s community while learning from its history, archives, and fellow artists.

Proposed Residency Project

During the residency, I will focus on developing and completing my original comic book series, Super Andy and Jay. This long-term independent project combines superhero storytelling, science fiction, humor, action, and personal mythology into a unique visual narrative that I have been building through years of writing and illustration.

Super Andy and Jay follows two extraordinary brothers living in a strange and often chaotic world. One embodies immense spiritual power, while the other relies on technology, invention, and determination. Through their adventures, the story explores themes of transformation, friendship, identity, family, resilience, and the relationship between humanity and technology. The residency would provide dedicated time and space to advance the project through writing, penciling, inking, page design, and production preparation.

Alongside the comic book, I create figurative and erotic artwork that celebrates sexuality, imagination, and the human form. This work examines themes of freedom, desire, vulnerability, and self-expression while challenging conventional ideas about how intimacy and the body are represented in art. During the residency, I plan to expand this body of work through new drawings, illustrations, and finished pieces that further develop my artistic voice.

I am also actively building a career as a tattoo artist in Los Angeles. Tattooing allows me to bring my skills in drawing, design, and storytelling into a collaborative and deeply personal art form. During the residency, I intend to create original tattoo flash designs, refine my illustrative techniques, and continue developing a portfolio that reflects both my comic book and fine art influences.

My work is driven by a belief that storytelling, sexuality, and personal transformation are powerful creative forces. Whether through comics, figurative art, or tattoo design, I am interested in creating work that is imaginative, expressive, and unapologetically authentic.

By the end of the residency, I aim to complete significant new material for Super Andy and Jay, expand my portfolio of figurative artwork, and strengthen the foundation of my tattoo practice. The residency would provide an invaluable opportunity to focus intensely on my creative work while contributing to and learning from a community of fellow artists.,

I plan to engage with the Tom of Finland Foundation archives as a source of research, artistic inspiration, and historical context for my creative practice. Studying original drawings, publications, and archival materials will help inform my comic book Super Andy and Jay, my figurative and erotic artwork, and my tattoo designs. I am particularly interested in learning from the strong draftsmanship, storytelling, and celebration of sexuality found in the collection. Access to the archives will deepen my understanding of queer art history and help me create new work that builds upon that legacy while expressing my own artistic voice.

I first heard about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program through a conversation with another artist and member of the queer community at a local bathhouse. The recommendation led me to learn more about the Foundation, its archives, and its commitment to supporting artists whose work explores sexuality, identity, and self-expression. After researching the program, I felt it aligned strongly with my own creative practice in comics, figurative art, erotic art, and tattoo design.

My current focus is developing original comic and graphic narrative projects that explore queer identity, transformation, friendship, resilience, and self-invention. The Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence program represents an opportunity to create new work in an environment that values artistic freedom and unapologetic self-expression. I am especially interested in learning from the Foundation’s archives and the artists connected to its community. As a creator, I want to continue building stories and imagery that celebrate queer imagination while expanding my technical skills and professional practice. I would be honored to contribute my perspective, energy, and artwork to the residency community.

Jakob Dziorek is a queer artist based in Malmö-Sweden who works with classical realism and queer motifs. By exploring the human body and the relationship between objects and sexuality, Jakob creates artworks that navigate between traditional still life paintings and anatomical studies. Using traditional materials such as charcoal and oil paint, Jakob combines classical techniques with a modern touch. Through careful studies of old objects, Jakob creates pieces that are not only beautiful and inspiring but also thought-provoking and engaging.

My proposed project for the residency is to continue developing my work around queer identity, intimacy, and the relationship between objects and the body through painting and drawing. I work mainly with oil paint and charcoal, using classical techniques and observational studies as a foundation, but I’m interested in how these traditional methods can be used to speak about contemporary queer experiences.

During the residency, I want to create a new series of paintings and drawings focused on queer still lifes and figurative works. I’m especially interested in the emotional weight objects can carry,  clothing, flowers, leather, furniture, fabrics, or personal items that hold traces of a person or a relationship. I want to explore how desire, vulnerability, and memory can exist within these objects and spaces, sometimes more strongly than through explicit narratives.
A big part of my process is studying light, material, and atmosphere through direct observation. I often work slowly, building paintings over time, and I want to use the residency period to deepen this process and challenge myself with more ambitious compositions and larger works. I also hope to experiment more freely between still life and figurative painting, allowing the works to become more personal and emotionally open.
The Tom of Finland Foundation feels especially meaningful to me because of its connection to queer art history and its support of queer artists working across different forms of expression. As a young queer painter working within a very traditional medium, I often think about how queer stories and bodies have historically been excluded from classical painting traditions. Through my work, I want to create paintings where queer intimacy and sexuality can exist naturally within these spaces.
More than anything, I see this residency as an opportunity to immerse myself fully in my practice, connect with other queer artists, and continue shaping a visual language that feels both personal and timeless.

I would like to engage with the archives both visually and historically by studying how queer desire, intimacy, masculinity, and identity have been represented across generations of artists. In my own practice, I often use symbolic still lifes where objects such as old magazines, books, drawings and personal items become emotionally loaded and function almost like portraits or traces of a person. Because of this, I feel deeply connected to the idea of the archive. I hope to use the archives through observation, sketching, research, and reflection to inform new paintings and drawings created during the residency.

ASH COOK [HOYLE] spent almost a decade as a features Programmer at the Sundance Film Festival, working across fiction, nonfiction, and Midnight. Ash is currently serving as the Festival Director of the San Francisco Jewish Film Festival, the first and largest Jewish film festival in the world and the Associate Director of Programming & Operations + the Advisory Board of the Los Angeles Festival of Movies. Ash is a 2026 Queer|Art Mentorship Fellow & a 2021 Project Involve Fellow with Film Independent. He has also worked as the Director of Programming of Damn These Heels Film Festival by the Utah Film Center, a Senior Programmer at Outfest Los Angeles, a Programmer at Palm Springs Shorts Fest and in various curatorial capacities at NewFest NY, Sun Valley Film Festival, Overlook Film Festival, and AFI Fest. Ash has experience in production at ABC Studios, Mssng Peces, and The Annoyance Theater. Ash is originally from Philadelphia, PA and holds a dual degree in Film & English from Vassar College in Poughkeepsie, NY.

Last year, I wrote a short script based on a scene from legendary underground novelist Dennis Cooper's new book "I Wished." This book is an end cap of sorts to his cycle of works about his late friend George Miles, which were a cluster of fictionalized novellas about adjacent & resonant characters. Markedly different than the rest, "I Wished" is constituted more of direct authorial address than fictionalized vignette. In a mode of great clarity and vulnerability, Dennis unpacks his narrative fixation and opens more of a transparent biographical window than he does the other related works in such a way that felt arresting. The urgent honesty and intimacy felt like a precious offering from this writer capable of such intricate and engaging story engineering, but his signature matter of factness was a through line - familiar and stunning as ever.

Dennis is another hero of mine I've been able to be in relationship to through my curatorial work (through, for example world premiering his latest filmic work "Room Temperature" at the Los Angeles Festival of Movies). It was been so gratifying to have him read and support the script his book inspired me to write. Taking a piece of Dennis's biography - a devastating injury he sustained as a child (described briefly and frankly in "I Wished") - I wrote something that grapples with many of the things Dennis does in general in is work (death, indifference, mischief, teen boys), but in a way I found particularly revelatory in this text. This book encouraged me to imagine death not as foreboding nor necessarily violent, but rather as a loving force of consumption. This shift in perspective opened up a deep, resonant space in my heart and mind that I wanted to inhabit in a piece of cinematic homage. I believe that my knowledge of Dennis's body of work and contemporary collaborations on his filmic endeavors put me in an excellent position to make this adaptation. What I bring to it, as a trans man, is a slightly more feminine lens looking at the potent ingredients always present on Dennis's table. I believe the resulting project will be alchemically exciting, fresh, and beautiful.

I have a 2 page script I would love to work on and shoot at the house. Dennis is such an undersung Angeleno / creative hero - his work has been so provocative he mostly works in France now. But working on this homage piece in a place so crucial to the cultural landscape of his hometown would be a really meaningful way to acknowledge the artistic legacy Dennis is a part of creating for our city.

Working in these archives would drive the aesthetic of the project. The work I am doing on this film sits really squarely in the boldly erotic, dark inky masculinity that connects the works in the Tom of Finland Foundation archive. I would be excited to use the visual materials to inform and create some of the visual language of the film.
I have been to tour and visit the house as well as attended several openings for Artists In Residence.
The work ToFF does is invaluable and indispensable! I am so grateful to be able to considered for this opportunity and really appreciate your attention on my proposal.

I’m Islam Nashef, an artist and designer from Taybeh, occupied Palestine. I work with fashion, jewelry, and shoemaking, but I see myself as a creator of art through fashion. Materials and experimentation are my tools to explore identity, emotion, and existence.

Living under occupation deeply shapes my work, my daily life, movements, and relationship with objects inform my designs. Using fabric, leather, and metal, I create pieces that archive emotion and reflect a layered, charged identity.

In Palestine, fashion is rarely recognized as contemporary art. I challenge this by presenting fashion as a medium that carries political narratives, human experience, and aesthetic vision. For me, fashion is not ornament or commodity, it is a visual language, a presence, and a subtle act of resistance.
Mixed-media sculpture and wearable art using leather and textiles

I first knew the name Tom of Finland from their collaboration with Diesel. While shopping one day from one of their shared collections, I became curious and decided to research the artist. During my search, I discovered the Foundation’s website and learned about the Artist-in-Residence Program. I had been considering this opportunity for some time, and finally decided to apply, inspired by the chance to engage deeply with a space dedicated to creativity, artistic exploration, and the freedom to experiment.
I do not have any special requirements or needs during the residency. I am flexible and able to fully engage with all aspects of the program as offered.

responses.answer: I was unable to upload a PDF directly, so I have included an image of my main project here. Additional work samples, including process videos and more images, are provided via the links below. These materials showcase my creative practice, experimentation with leather and fashion design, and the concepts behind my work.

plan to engage deeply with the Tom of Finland Foundation archives by selecting historical images that conceptually resonate with my work. These images will be adapted and printed onto leather garments, allowing me to reinterpret archival material in a contemporary context. By studying the archives, I aim to understand the visual language, composition, and emotional tone of the works, guiding how I position and manipulate the images on the body. The archives will inspire and serve as a direct resource for creating layered, kinetic visuals that explore movement, identity, and narrative, bridging historical imagery with wearable art.

During the residency, I plan to create Kinetic Leather Prints, a series exploring the relationship between image, body, and leather. I will design and stitch large-scale leather garments and experiment with the placement of images on the body to create the illusion of a third, moving image that shifts with the wearer’s movements. A major focus of the project is exploring the technical challenges of printing on leather, a material that behaves very differently from traditional fabrics. Through this process, I aim to push the boundaries of both material and form, investigating how texture, structure, and body interaction can convey motion, emotion, and narrative. The final outcome will be a collection of wearable pieces that merge conceptual experimentation with technical innovation, culminating in a public presentation.
Shields (Collection), 2025, leather sculpture / wearable garments, variable dimensions (~up to 2 meters in length)
Kinetic sculpture, sculpture, installation, photography, video

I'm a conceptual visual artist who works are mostly in kinetic sculpture, sculpture and photography. During my dual master’s in Fashion Styling, Fashion Photography, and Film in London, I worked as an art director in a fashion studio while creating my first fashion installations, sculptural pieces that could also be seen as wearable art. This work led to my first recognition at the British Photography Awards in the fashion category. After moving to Berlin, I became active in the queer techno scene as a photographer, developing documentary series while working at Munich Pride, Berlin Pride, Berghain, the National Gallery in Berlin, and the Maxim Gorki Theater. At the same time, I continued developing sculptural and kinetic installation works, exhibiting in cities such as Stockholm, Tokyo, Berlin, Venice, London and Barcelona. Evolving from my early fashion installations, these works explore movement, identity, and presence. Rather than teaching or delivering fixed messages, my practice invites audiences, especially within queer communities,to find their own truths, starting from individual empowerment to community.

The project I aim, brings together sculpture, photography, and video through four works; the Fuckmachine, th Iron Condoms: BIG BOY, the doublesided dildo figures, and The Slap Wall. Fuckmachine has already been produced, and during the residency I will develop and build the remaining three sculptures, expanding the series.
Fuckmachine is inspired by the atmosphere of 1990s Berlin gay dungeons, spaces of pleasure, resistance, survival, and community that helped shape today’s queer culture. The sculpture is activated by hand pedals, while mirrors reflect the user’s face and the mechanism. This setup invites a personal confrontation with desire: questioning whether pleasure comes from one’s own agency or from images shaped by society and beauty standards. The work does not aim to teach but to create a space where individuals can discover their own truths and vulnerabilities.
Iron Condoms: BIG BOY explores how patriarchal systems discipline masculinity. The sculptures appear as iron cage-like condoms that restrict growth, transforming social pressure into physical form. Elements such as the kanji 家 (house), a circle, and a cross reference expectations of family, religion, and colonial Christianity. Together they reflect how cultural systems impose rigid roles and punish those who do not conform.
The doublesided dildo figures explore reciprocity and fluidity in intimacy. By allowing bodies to penetrate and be penetrated at the same time, they challenge the binary roles often present in the sex-toy industry and suggest that pleasure can exist through trust, safety, and mutual exchange.
The Slap Wall is an interactive sculpture with a mechanical slapping system activated by the user. Here the participant becomes both the operator and the recipient, highlighting the paradox of power and vulnerability and reminding us that experiences of dominance and submission can be self granted.
During the residency, I will also create photographic and video works where performers interact with these sculptures. The visual language will draw from Tom of Finland’s aesthetic, especially his representation of queer masculinity through confidence, tenderness, and bodily presence. By combining this with my sculptures, shaped by references to Berlin’s underground culture and histories of queer repression, the project connects different moments of queer history while highlighting masculinity as strong, vulnerable, caring, and empowering.

I will use the Tom of Finland Foundation archives to study how he presents queer masculinity through posture, confidence, and beauty. I will not copy his drawings, but translate these elements into my own work. Through photography and video, I will show performers interacting with my sculptures, creating scenes that reflect both strength and freedom. Inspired by Berlin’s dungeon culture and queer history, my sculptures add another layer. By bringing these influences together, I want to show masculinity as strong, caring, vulnerable, and thoughtful.

Michael McFadden (Chicago, IL) explores and celebrates sexual freedom as a form of resistance to multilayered stigma and trauma, both historical and ongoing. It explores the relationship between death and photography, accentuating a longing for what is no longer present. Focusing on the overlap between internalized shame and identity affirmation, he draws from family albums and vintage gay pornography. These vernaculars, inherited from biological and chosen families, are the foundation to his visual iconography. He interweaves photographs, sculpture, text, and video to examine the inner workings and the visceral charges of sexual connection, as they manifest in the context of everyday life. Michael received his B.A. and M.S.W. from Loyola University Chicago and recently graduated from the ICP-Bard M.F.A. Program in Advanced Photographic Studies. He received 3rd Place in the ToFF’s Emerging Artist Competition in 2024. Michael currently resides in Los Angeles, CA.
I don't have any special requirements, accessibility needs or dietary restrictions.

I have been an avid admirer of both Tom's art and the work produced by the previous Artists-in-Residence for many years.  Living in Los Angeles has provided me with the opportunity to visit the House, meet many of the A-in-Rs, attend the art exhibitions and participate in programming.

While I have many ideas and intentions going into the residency, I am coming in with a level of openness and excitement that my ideas will morph and change.  I am looking forward to diving into the archives, meeting with folks connected ToFF and allowing the possibilities to flow.

During this moment of escalating political hostility and renewed attempts to repress queer lives, I find myself returning to a central question: what does community make possible? Like many others, I have been deeply impacted by the current political climate and the overt repression of queer identities. The erosion of rights, the weaponization of language, and the reshaping of public narratives do not only target policy; they attempt to destabilize belonging itself. In response, my proposed work for the ToFF AIR program centers on “community” as both a form of care and an act of resistance.

Community is often invoked as a comforting abstraction, but for queer people it has always been infrastructural—an embodied network of survival. It is where knowledge circulates when institutions fail, where grief is metabolized collectively, and where joy becomes a radical insistence on presence. I want to explore how community functions not simply as a gathering of individuals, but as a living archive: a site where histories, intimacies, and strategies for endurance are stored and transmitted.

Tom’s House stands as a monument to historical resistance and the hard-fought gains that allow us to exist as we do today. It is not only a physical space but a testament to the labor, risk, and imagination of those who came before. Within its walls, the archive operates on multiple registers. For me, it is a mirror—reflecting fragments of myself and my lineage back to me. It is a lighthouse—casting guidance across uncertain waters, illuminating paths forged through previous crises. And it is a roadmap—offering tangible evidence that resistance is not theoretical but lived, practiced, and sustained over time.
During my residency, I intend to engage the archive and the ToFF community as a collaborative partner rather than a static repository. Through research, dialogue, and creative response, I will trace the gestures of care embedded within its materials: letters, photographs, ephemera, and oral histories. These artifacts hold the residue of touch, risk, and relation. By reactivating them in new forms— through image-making, writing, or participatory encounters—I hope to surface the connective tissue between past and present struggles.

Ultimately, this project seeks to ask: how can we build communities that do not merely withstand repression, but transform it? At Tom’s House, I hope to locate models of collective resilience that can inform how we gather, protect, and imagine ourselves forward.

I will engage the archive through close research, creative response, and shared conversation. I plan to spend time with drawings, letters, photographs, and ephemera to understand how queer community was built through care, desire, and resistance. From there, I will create new photographic work that reimagines and connects these materials to the present moment. I hope to host informal study gatherings with artists and community members, allowing shared reflection to shape the project. Throughout, I will document the process, treating the archive as a collaborator in imagining, protecting, and strengthening radical queer community.

Riven Ratanavanh (b. 1996 in Bangkok, Thailand) is a multi-disciplinary artist working between New York and Bangkok. His work engages Asian (trans)masculine identities, fetish, erotic reclamation, and sociopolitical power.

Rooted in performance and ritual, his work incorporates BDSM and body modification as tools for transformation and transcendence. Working across photography and film, he documents the aftermath of ritual acts. Queering sacred gestures and sanctifying the perverse, he carves space for queer Asian belonging.

He has presented his work at venues including Performance Space New York, the Park Avenue Armory, Movement Research, The Institute of Contemporary Arts (ICA) London, and his films have been screened at the London Short Film Festival, and by CIRCA on the Piccadilly Lights. He has been featured in Butt Magazine, the Asian American Arts Alliance, and has published in Document Journal. He has a forthcoming solo exhibition in 2026 at the Bangkok Kunsthalle.

In the past few years, I’ve used leather in my work for political commentary, as a way to confront racial fetishization, reverse stereotypes of Asian masculinity as deficient, and critique historically entrenched power dynamics. I created a leather persona who exists both on and off stage: armored, skilled at refusal and rebuttal, both cutting and unflinching. But that is him at work, not who he is at rest or at play.

As a gay trans man who grew up in Thailand with no role models nor language to describe who I was — and am still becoming — much of my work has centered on protecting against and reversing racialized and bioessentialist ideas of who I am and how I should be. But I am now less interested in refuting stereotypes and more interested in stepping into a rawer eroticism that gets me closer to myself — that is just for me.

In my time at TOM House, I plan to create a body of self-portraits documenting personal rituals, working specifically with ideas of masculinity.

As I reckon with the prospect of bottom surgery, I am also daily encountering and reencountering my body anew: how it measures up to ideals of masculinity, physicality, and equippedness (very much including those found in Tom’s archive). Simultaneously holding the truths of my body and my desires while interrogating these standards and expectations, in these self-portraits I reference and pastiche in order to reimagine, give new form to, and carve a place for my belonging in this lineage.

Unlike my staged performances, these images will be captured in quieter rituals woven into the everyday. They may include acts like shaving, getting dressed, working out, exchanges of intimacy, conscious power exchange, devotion; possibly involving visitors, passers-throughs, and leather kin in LA. In creating these images I hope to both play into ideas of masculinity for my own pleasure, as well as subvert them in ways only my body can, in this moment.

I intend these portraits to be more immediate, erotic, and personal than my previous work — centering the ways I desire to see and shape myself, using Tom’s imagination as inspiration. I may potentially also incorporate a larger performative ritual to close my time at the house, and to frame and accompany the presentation of these works.

I hope to come to TOM House to land, to live, to shed armor, to settle into new skin, and to find belonging. Especially in a time where trans life is contested, I believe that living, and being seen living – with all its ordinary and extraordinary transformations — is enough.

I first visited the Tom of Finland Foundation in early 2019, and have had it in my mind that I would like to be an artist in residence here ever since. I have also heard about the deeply transformative times my friends Oat and Sweat have had during their residencies, both professionally and personally, which has largely urged me to apply now.

I am particularly interested in referencing images in the archive that hold both erotic charge and vulnerability, as well as rituals of masculinity such as bodybuilding, camaraderie, and more everyday scenes. I am also drawn to Tom’s letters and correspondence, and how these materials might inform my process. I often write alongside making images, and have been keeping a diary of my cruising encounters and the mundane and transcendental transformations of my relationship to my body — a practice I hope to continue in residence, potentially presenting excerpts in dialogue with the photographs.

Excerpt from "Orientalism Up your Ass", published in Document Journal (2024)

In 2022, an account called “Asian Guys?” messages me to ask: “how are you?”

Without having seen his Grindr profile I wonder if he’s white, but then I think to myself that doesn’t seem right—his username is too self-proclaimed.

I look at his profile. He’s Asian. We get chatting. He says he’s looking for something specific:

“Looking to train Asian guys to dominate white boys.”

I’m not sure yet if my dick is hard but my interest is piqued.

•

Dominating white boys had already been on my mind. Last year, I proposed to do a redux of Carolee Schneemann’s infamous performance Interior Scroll at the Judson Church. This very church where nearly five decades ago in 1975, she performed Interior Scroll, standing nude on a table and pulling out a long sheet of paper from her vagina, reading from it her response to a film critic’s dismissive, misogynistic remarks about her work. This piece was on my mind even though I first encountered it years ago. I was struck by how she owned her body, got at the feeling of it. And her anger—it was loud, confrontational, in-your-face.

As an East-Southeast Asian transsexual man—or let me say, transmasculine faggot—I grew up as a girl in Thailand, and I was never taught to be assertive, nor to own or be loud and public about my anger. I thought, if Schneemann, a white cis woman, had a lot to say to a white cis man in the ’70s, then I have a lot to say to what cisness and whiteness are doing in the culture around me now. I wanted to pastiche her method but also bring race to the fore, and tease out how erotic power dynamics are intertwined with colonialism. What was I angry about? I was tired of trying to make art, fuck—exist—without being stereotyped as an innately submissive bottom, sexually, psychically, culturally. Scheemann said ‘fuck the man’ by pulling her words out of her vagina, amping up whatever ‘feminine indulgence’ had been used to dismiss her work. I said Fuck the white cis man by putting his stereotypical roles up his ass.

Months into my exchange with Asian Guys?, I discover that he not only has a harem of white boys he lends out to the guys he trains, but also a “brainwasher” who makes homework for the boys—hours-long hypnosis videos. The brainwasher can’t find enough porn of Asian men dominating white men to make these videos with, so he resorts to also using footage of Asian men fucking white women instead.

I can’t believe that’s true, so I check. I look up “gay Asian man dominates white man” and its variants on Google. Everything that shows up is the reverse, my words spit out in opposite order. Among the results: “muscular white man fucks Asian bare in the kitchen”, “Asian bottoms pounded by big white cocks (13 scenes!)”, and “WHITE DADDY DOMINATES ASIAN SUB”. Less than one out of ten feature an Asian top and white bottom. Even fewer feature an Asian dom and white sub. The majority of videos center around white dominance.

I understand there is a shortage of images–and sometimes you’ve got to see, first, in order to be able to do.

•

In the past few years, I’ve used leather in my work for political commentary, as a way to confront racial fetishization, reverse stereotypes of Asian masculinity, and critique historically rooted power dynamics. I created a leather persona who exists both on and off stage, armored, skilled at refusal and rebuttal. But that is him at work, not who he is at rest or at play.

As a gay trans man who grew up in Thailand with no examples of me nor language to describe who I was – and am still becoming – much of my work has centered on protection and reversal against external ideas of who I am. But I am now less interested in refuting stereotypes and more interested in stepping into a more raw eroticism that gets closer to myself.

At TOM House, I propose to create a series of photographic works documenting personal rituals — erotic self-portraits in moments of transformation, alongside intimate encounters with queer-trans leather kin in LA. Unlike my staged performances, these images will unfold at the scale of the everyday: shaving, dressing, working out, exchanges of intimacy and devotion; and in them, quiet transformations.

As I reckon with the prospect of bottom surgery, I am also daily encountering and reencountering my body: how it measures up to ideals and ideas of masculinity, physicality, and equippedness (including those found in Tom’s archive). Moving between inhabiting future versions of myself and present embodiment, contentment and tension coexist at once.

With these photos I hope to access the more immediately erotic and intimate, and this approach may perhaps work itself into to a ritual performance that may accompany the final showing of photographic works.

In engaging with TOM’s depictions of charged encounters and exaggerated masculinities, I hope to emulate and reshape them for my own lineage. Just as I have previously reworked canonical photographs and performance gestures, I want to situate myself within — and gently transform – visual language of the leatherman in a way that can bring me home, toward joy, play, and rest – not just resistance.

Through these images, I aim to write myself into continuity: the leatherman in erotic charge, in between, and in ordinary living. In a moment where trans life in this country is contested, I hope that living, and being seen living – with all its ordinary and extraordinary transformations — is enough.

In creating my photographs, I am interested in referencing images in the archive that depict sex, charged moments, and rituals of masculinity such as bodybuilding, camaraderie, as well as more everyday and mundane scenes. I am also drawn to letters and correspondence, and how these materials may inspire my process, as I often create images in conjunction with writing. I have been keeping a diary of my cruising encounters, intimate moments, as well as the more mundane and transcendental transformations of my body, which I hope to continue developing — and potentially display alongside the images I create in residence.
I first visited the Tom of Finland Foundation in early 2019, and have had it in my mind that I would like to be and artist in residence there ever since.

Riven Ratanavanh (b. 1996 in Bangkok, Thailand) is a multi-disciplinary artist whose work focuses on Asian (trans)masculine identities, (re)shaping fetishes-and-fetishism, sociopolitical power play, and imagining trans utopias.

Rooted in performance and ritual, his work incorporates BDSM and body modification to transform the body. His work also spans photo and film, which document his rituals. Referencing, reshaping, and queering sacred gestures and sanctifying the perverse, he affirms and asserts queer Asian existence and belonging.

He has presented his work at venues including Performance Space New York, the Park Avenue Armory, Movement Research, The Institute of Contemporary Arts (ICA) London, and his films have been screened at the London Short Film Festival and by CIRCA on the Piccadilly Lights. He has been featured in Butt Magazine, the Asian American Arts Alliance, and published in Document Journal. He has a forthcoming solo exhibition at the Bangkok Kunsthalle.

I depict spaces that foster memory and kinship among queer people, such as gay bars, dance floors, and the mirrored halls of theaters and bathhouses. I use abstraction, symbols and gestures to capture the energy and visual phenomena there: moving beams of light, reflections, heavy air, joyful motion, and feeling dis/embodied.

These spaces of togetherness are captured when guests are yet to arrive, or after they have just departed. This emptiness creates a sense of mortality and belonging that is particularly queer. A sensibility that emerges when I find myself in these rooms, joining a lineage of ancestors among people with HIV, gender non-conformists, and fetishists. 

As I spend more time in California, my work has expanded to include landscape as a queer space, with communes, secret beaches, and cruising spots among the ruins. Now, bodies are starting to appear in the outdoors, telling stories of agency, pleasure, and sensuality.

I’ve heard about the residency in various ways, from being a follower of Tom of Finland’s work, from social media, from friends who have visited the house, and through the work of some previous artists-in-residence.

If you’re interested in reading in detail about how I’ve worked in queer archives in the past, feel free to check this interview from my time at the Leather Archives & Museum in Chicago: https://sixtyinchesfromcenter.org/chicago-archives-artist-project-artist-profile-on-aay-preston-myint/

At ToF House, I hope to expand the scope of my practice in bothe format and concept. In terms of my wall drawings, if permitted, I would love to develop more of my site-responsive drawings using the walls of any of the exhibition spaces available to artists for their residency projects. I would also like to bring these works off the wall with suspended and collaged fabric, tiled sheets of paper, or even cut and assembled boards. While I love working directly on the wall, those installations are often temporary, and this change would mean the art can exist and be shared beyond the exhibition period. The Pleasure Park could also provide a wonderful opportunity to figure out how to use these portable formats to display work outdoors (my club interiors are drawn on interior walls, so how might I install my cruising and public sex imagery in the outdoors)? 

As mentioned, I have been incorporating more explicitly figurative and sexual imagery in recent work. The next step in my practice is to speak more as a participant in queer life, not simply an observer. I look to the archives and house for inspiration for how to use my own experiences and desire as a fetishist, cruiser, etc as fuel for the work (as well as a greater “queer experience” or queer desire as an abstract concept). This is not necessarily to be autobiographical or confessional, but to work on being more nakedly honest, transgressive, and push the boundaries of comfort through graphic expression - much the way Tom’s life experiences informed his desire and his work around power, taboo, and even humor in sex.

The ToF House, with the deep history and community contained within the house and the Pleasure Park, is an ideal location to work on bridging the interior and exterior worlds depicted in my drawings. As my work evolves, I have shifted the frame from the vernacular architecture of clubs, back rooms and and drag stages to outdoor scenes of queer contemplation and communion. The flat, abstractly depicted passageways, gloryholes, and shells of abandoned buildings are retained as framing devices, blending with elements of figuration and landscape that celebrate the outdoors as a site for queer radical pleasure. An idyllic yet urban landmark of California queer history feels like the right place to develop a new, expansive, and more explicitly erotic expression of my work.

I’ve already spoken a bit about getting visual inspiration from the archive, but I’m also excited to engage with the materials to make deeper connections to our queer ancestors. As someone who documents not only bodies or sex acts, but also the locations that provide a space for them, I also believe erotic ephemera, publications, and art can function as safe spaces. I look forward to being inspired by these materials as a testament to how people would find each other and care for each other, in secret, across great distances and often at risk to themselves.
Oil painting, Collage, Plaster Sculpture, and Pastel Drawing

Morgan earned his Bachelor of Arts in Environmental Sciences & Policy from Northwestern University in 2014. After the passing of his father, he shifted his focus from a legal career to art and art education. He went on to graduate from Columbia University with a second Bachelor’s degree in visual arts. During his time at Columbia, he was also a researcher and graphic designer at the Columbia Center on Sustainable Investment and was a finalist for the 2020 Elie Wiesel Prize in Ethics for his writings on environmental theory and art. Currently pursuing his Master of Fine Arts in Creative Visual Arts at Cornell University, Morgan works across mediums including painting, sculpture, collage, and mixed-media installations. His work often highlights underrepresented voices, including a project honoring Christine Jorgensen, a prominent trans figure from his hometown. He lives, works, and studies in Ithaca, New York.

This past September, I curated "Magnum O-Pspsps," an exhibition devoted to cats featuring works from 47 artists. The show made a splash and I was invited by an art center in New Haven, CT to curate a show around dogs this coming summer called "Deus Ex Bark-ina." I am now interested in curating a show around literal and gay bears in LA called "Bear Season." A close friend in LA recommended I look into your foundation and realized how much I needed to learn both for my practice and as I develop a prospectus for "Bear Season."

The Tom of Finland Foundation AIR Program would support a focused expansion of erotic content within my practice; specifically, paintings and sculptures that celebrate the nude male bear body with candor, tenderness, and humor.

Many of my large-scale tableau paintings feature muscular bears who, as a way to comically subvert western sculpture tropes, are adorned with underwear. That device produces a dissonance: figures that project confidence and monumentality while signaling self-consciousness. The works hold two registers at once, social commentary and desire. Yet, these paintings are also hiding a secret in plain sight: the bodies I depict are drawn from my intimate life and community. Until now, I have often “nested” that desire inside broader narratives, as if eroticism needed justification. During the residency, I want to let the nude form stand without apology: undergarments removed, the bare bear presented as a direct subject rather than coded subtext. I see this as an ethical and aesthetic shift toward greater vulnerability, immediacy, and clarity of intention.

At the AIR Program, this would manifest in a series of paintings and plaster sculptures reminiscent of the memorial-esque figures from my tableaus. I would first develop them from models using charcoal and soft pastels. Then use the drawing to develop paintings and sculpture work. These sculptures would look similar to my recent “Dying Gaul” sculpture. Here, I modeled bears to pose as the iconic marbled sculpture. I created a plaster sculpture from models and enveloped the plaster from an archive of magazines. For the residency, I hope to extend that method using photocopies and material from the Foundation’s archives and library, allowing historical queer erotic imagery to function not only as reference alone, but also as literal skin; an accumulated surface of lineage, memory, and transmission.

Research and sculpture is married within my practice. In 2020, I used magazine archives, recorded lectures, and books to learn about the life of Christine Jorgensen. Christine was one of the first high-profile transgendered women, and had also lived in my hometown, where I struggled with my sexuality. So, I used this research to apply for an historic marker at her old home. When the proposal was rejected, I created a life-sized public sculpture of Jorgensen and installed the piece in a local park where it remained for two weeks. My hope would be to bring this energy into the sculpture I produce during the residency.

My additional hope would be to leave the residency and curate a show featuring Bears that considers the Magic Realism and Erotic Illustration of historic queer artists such as Paul Cadmus and Tom of Finland with contemporary queer artists who, among other movements, employ new queer intimism.

I plan to engage the Foundation’s archives in two direct ways: research and material incorporation. First, I’ll study works from the collection, photographs, and books to analyze compositional strategies; pose, exaggeration, tenderness, humor, and how erotic images negotiate monumentality and intimacy. Second, I’ll photocopy select archival pages or books (as permitted) and use them as collage “skin” on plaster figures; while also generating a reference library that informs a parallel series of drawings and paintings of the nude bear body.

I work primarily in sculpture and installation, often with everyday objects. Through recontextualization and material transformation, I explore how desire, power, and cultural meaning accumulate in objects, often using them as proxies for the body.

kohlman harshbarger (b. 1993) is a queer, transdisciplinary artist based in New York City. They create multivalent installations and sculptures that hijack the everyday, transforming familiar objects and spaces into sites of instability, desire, and becoming. Their work uses consumer goods, found materials, fabricated objects, and sensory elements to disrupt systems of value and reveal the poetic and transformative potential embedded in material life.

They have exhibited work at O'Flaherty's, Hesse Flatow, All Street, A Hug from the Art World, 95 Gallon, and U-Haul Gallery among others, and have had work published by Blurring Books.

I plan to use the residency to conduct focused archival and material research into clothing as a central structuring element in Tom of Finland's work. While Tom's drawings are often discussed through the male body, I am interested in how clothing operates as a primary site of power, fantasy, and identification, a formal system through which masculinity and erotic identity are constructed and communicated.

The garments that recur across Tom's drawings - leather uniforms, workwear, boots, caps, and accessories - are largely drawn from military, police, and working class trade contexts. These groups are historically associated with violence toward queer people and with institutions that excluded or criminalized queer life. Through fetishization, these uniforms were inverted and stripped of institutional authority, then recharged as tools of both pleasure and self determination. I understand this visual language as foundational to the construction of modern gay masculinity, power, and erotic identity, with these garments functioning as talismanic objects that help construct and circulate these identities.

The residency would allow sustained engagement with the Foundation's archives, including close visual study and cataloging of garments across Tom's drawings. I am interested in mapping both stylistic range (biker jackets, boots, denim, tank tops, uniforms) and material characteristics (leather, denim, metal hardware). I'd like to spend time studying how these garments structure the body itself, compressing, exaggerating, suspending, concealing, and how these manipulations construct the erotic image.

This research will directly inform the development of sculptural work exploring the physical, historical, and cultural resonance of these garments as erotic and social objects within queer visual culture

I plan to work closely and consistently with the Foundation's archives throughout the residency. This will include detailed visual analysis of Tom's drawings alongside other erotic artworks in the collection to map the range of garments, materials, and contexts represented across time. The TOM House library will support additional research into the historical and cultural roles of these garments in relation to desire, power, and fantasy. This research will support the development of a working image and reference archive that directly informs the sculptural work I will produce in dialogue with the Foundation's materials.

This residency feels closely aligned with my practice, both materially and conceptually. My work has long engaged questions of queer masculinity, desire, and power through objects rather than representation, and the Foundation's archives offer a rare opportunity to study the visual language that shaped much of this history. I approach archival research as a form of care and responsibility, and I am interested in allowing that research to quietly but deeply inform new work.

I have been familiar with Tom of Finland's work for years through my interest in the history of queer visual culture and erotic imagery. I collect vintage physique pictorials, including some illustrated by Tom, and have long been drawn to how these images functioned as both fantasy and documentation. I learned about the residency program while researching the Foundation's archives, and it immediately felt aligned with my practice. I waited to apply until my work was in a more developed phase and I had a project that would be genuinely enriched by the resources at TOM House.

I am a transmasculine artist, writer and gleaner living in Amsterdam. I call myself a 'gleaner' because I like to work with residues, be it material leftovers found on the street, or historical traces embedded in our surroundings. My installations often involve scrap metal, which I use to rust-dye fabric. Within the process of rust, which is mostly understood as one of disrepair, I recognize a transformative potential: corroded objects gain new shapes and auras, beyond their normative, original function. Recent sculptures have built on artistic research into the history of the jockstrap and its gay fetish status. I'm interested in how a pragmatic garment with medical and athletic intended functions was appropriated as homoerotic lingerie over time. For me this is an example of what scholar Sara Ahmed calls 'queer use': the utilization of things in ways other than intended, or by those for whom they were not intended.

During a residency at the TOM house I want to dive deeper into my artistic research around the jockstrap, and engage local trans- and fetish communities involved with this type of gay lingerie. 
The jockstrap is an American invention with a 150 year long history, and before the athletic jockstrap there were ‘suspensory bandages’ designed for medical use, which were promoted heavily in Victorian era postorder catalogues and said to prevent weakness and ‘diseases of men’. It’s a fascinating history with many twists and turns: in the 1970’s, the first sports bra for women was fashioned out of two jockstraps which were sewn together! 
For me as a transmasculine gay guy, wearing jockstraps has become an important way to affirm myself, to express my gender and sexuality, and to feel sexy in a masculine way. Within the transmasculine community the jockstrap has become a beloved type of affirming underwear. In fetish communities the focus is often on jockstraps being ‘well-seasoned’ with sweat, piss and cum, and on online forums users proudly share their dirty yellowed jocks. 
During the residency I would like to engage these different jockstrap-loving communities by conducting a series of personal interviews that will form the basis of a short art performance and sculptural activation to be presented during the public moment at the TOM House. Recently I have been making sculptures and installations that include hand-sewn, rust-dyed jockstraps, and I would like to develop this element further within a new, ephemeral and performative installation to be activated at the TOM House. Perhaps a public ‘jockstrap sewing circle’ could also be a nice way to include the local community. I have found that for myself, handsewing is really meditative and offers space for reflection and thoughtful conversation, and I appreciate the way in which the act of handsewing a jockstrap brings together a traditionally femme type of labour with this totem of hypermasculine gay aesthetics.  
As the jockstrap is such a staple of gay Americana it feels crucial to conduct this artistic research within the USA, and the TOM House would be an incredibly inspiring and nurturing space for this project to develop further.

I’m keen to look into the archives of queer erotic art for depictions of jockstraps. Furthermore I am also curious about erotic art that involves sweat, filth and secretions, like the drawings of the artist Bastille. For me Bastille’s world poses a sort of ‘underworld’ to the Tom of Finland universe, with the same idealized bodies and shiny round asses, that are now surrounded by dirt and scat. Lastly, I want to look into the former RoB gallery run by Rob Meijer, which was based in Amsterdam until the 90’s and was a central point for homoerotic art in Europe.

Several artists that I follow and admire like Gray Wielebinski and SWEATMOTHER have taken part in the residency and this is how I found out about its existence. Last year at Art Basel I also attended a Tom of Finland event and met your Creative Director Richard Villani, who encouraged me to apply to the residency.
It would be a total dream come true to be a resident at the Tom of Finland Foundation!

Matthew Lax is a filmmaker, artist, and writer based in New York.


His work has been shown at Viennale (Austria), IHME Contemporary (Helsinki), Rencontres Internationales (Paris), MIX New York and MIX Brasil (São Paulo), table (Chicago), Los Angeles Municipal Art Gallery, Human Resources, Los Angeles Contemporary Archive, REDCAT, and the Winnipeg Underground Film Festival. In 2025, he was the subject of a solo presentation at the 54th International Film Festival Rotterdam.


Lax’s writing has appeared in publications including Texte Zur Kunst, BOMB, the Los Angeles Review of Books, and Millenium Film Journal. He is a recipient of a 2024 Lightning Fund Grant from LACE and the Andy Warhol Foundation, and a 2025 Creative Research Grant from The Foundation for Contemporary Arts.


Lax’s films are distributed by Video Data Bank in Chicago. He received a BFA from Syracuse University and an MFA from California Institute of the Arts.

THE ZONE is a multichannel experimental film installation about the everchanging interiors of the notorious Hollywood gay sex club, The Zone. Focusing on the club’s architectural and psychic corpus as a site of sexual desire, local activism, and urban nightlife, the installation explores the building’s eventual transformation into a blue-chip art gallery, the disappearance of queer spaces, and the collective memories The Zone left behind. 


Opening at the height of the AIDS epidemic in 1991 and closing during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, The Zone was a unique fixture in the Los Angeles gay community. The club was initially intended as a drug-and-alcohol-free meeting place for sober gay men, but as one of the first sex clubs to operate within city limits, it was often raided by LAPD.


To protect the identities of its patrons, The Zone also maintained a strict no-photography policy. As a result, it has no functional archive and scarce documentation of its interiors. Moreover, the club’s identity was always in flux: it burned down once, changed ownership four times, and was constantly being renovated and improved.


In 2025, I tracked down The Zone’s original owner, 68-year-old Peter Deep, in Playa Del Carmen, Mexico. During our interview, I asked Deep to recall the club’s interiors. After insisting he couldn’t remember, he produced a blueprint by hand, explaining that he was a former architecture student. In THE ZONE, Deep’s drawing functions as a centerpiece that initiates the erotic searching at the heart of the film, using the concept of a collaborative drawing to reconstitute the desires that brought club patrons into the same space under similar repressive political conditions. 


My time at ToF will focus on expanding my embodied research into The Zone, which will include conducting interviews with former club patrons, such as pornographer ChiChi LaRue, and producing collaborative drawings like Deep’s where subjects produce renderings of the club while recalling their personal experiences there. From those dialogues, I will develop original footage that will be presented in a multichannel film installation, presented alongside documentation of rare surviving ephemera, and establishing shots isolated from the 8 porn films, including titles by Channel1 Releasing and Dick Wadd Studios, shot on site. The memory drawings will also be composited into a 3D model, reimagining the club’s interior and surrounding lot, in a series of sculptures and sets that will be used for filmed performances.

Tom died in 1991, the year The Zone opened its doors. Reviewing the archival materials in the Foundation’s holdings, I’ll try to understand Los Angeles queer life between 1991 and today. I will also search for reprepresentations of The Zone in the archive as well as artwork produced by other artists who frequented the club. 


In the archive I will research architectures of desire and representations of queer cruising spaces, so I can develop a visual language about the disappearance of cruising from physical space and its resurgence online, noting how this change parallels gentrification dynamics.
My colleagues Jamison Edgar and Wyatt Coday advised me to apply for the Artist-in-Residence program, and specifically noted that ToF board member Terence McFarland might take an interest in my work.

ARTWORKS DESCRIPTION

1. Installation view. A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG, PART TWO, Human Resources, November 2024

two-channel 4k video with sound, modified dog kennel, flatscreens, media player
72 x 120 x 120 inches
47 min, 25 sec loop
Ed. 1 of 2, + 1 AP

A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG is an experimental film trilogy and multimedia project that investigates family, survival, and discipline. Made in collaboration with my collie-breeding parents and their dogs, professional dog trainers, and the puppy play kink community, the trilogy explores the relationship between humanity and canines, searching for meaning in the various activities we do to make, become, and train dogs. 

In the installation version, the two channel film is installed inside a lifesize dog kennel. The viewer can watch the film from inside the dog kennel or through the bars. 


2. Excerpt. A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG, PART TWO, 2024-2025 Single channel version 

	Single channel version
	4k video with sound
	47 min, 25 sec loop


Structured around a performative reading of Herve Guibert’s 1982 smut novella, Les Chiens, Part Two explores pack behaviour through notions of dominance, submission, trust and consent, both within human and dog relationships and within pup play. Love and bonding become a central theme, weaving together further investigation into pup relationships, training sessions with a traumatised dog and Lax’s own relationship to the dogs in his life that he has loved and lost.

The experimental film Gay Men’s Book Club brings together seven homosexuals, ages 26-85 and strangers to one another, to discuss the book Elite Capture by Olúfẹ́mi O. Táíwò. Facilitated by the filmmaker in collaboration with community organizer, founding member of the Radical Faeries, and Jungian psychologist Dr. Don Kilhefner, the intergenerational, unscripted conversation explores Táíwò’s concept of elite capture as it relates to community, privilege, organizing, and intersectionality.

6. Erotic Non-Fiction, 2024 (still)
	
2 minutes
16mm film

Erotic Non-Fiction is an excerpt from a short film that documents my boyfriend Christopher, and I getting our nipples pierced at Aesthetic Ambition Studio in Santa Monica. The film is in conversation with diaristic artworks and frank documentation of sado-masochism by queer artists such as Law and Order (2012) by Jan Soldat and Bob Flannigan’s Fuck Journal (1987), which turn to various modes of representation to chart the body, queer intimacy, and the passage of time. 

7. Portrait of a Blind Horse, 2023 (still)

2 minutes
16mm film

Portrait of a Blind Horse is a visual study of my mother’s horse Chevy, who is the same age as my siblings and I. Chevy suddenly went blind after losing her foal in 2014, and has since become a metaphor for the punishing demands of motherhood in our family. 

8. Installation view. Brunt Drama, 2018

Animated loop loop with sound, double-sided projection
frosted plexiglass, aluminum frame
47 3/8 x 71 3/8 x 36 3/8 inches
7 min. 30 sec. loop

Brunt Drama addresses the inherent violence of the real, the simulated, and the dubious pleasure of witnessing. In the loop, narratives of safety, risk, and use-value are tested via 3D-rendered, plastic surrogates. Choreographed motion-studies and textual, Brechtian vignettes produce alternative translations and behaviors, both acceptable and indecent. Brunt Drama manipulates the slipperiness of affect-inducing data visualizations intended to ameliorate fear into consumption — presenting a simulation of the unrepresentable, visible from both sides of the screen.

LINK: https://vimeo.com/295100851/e4bfbb36b4?share=copy&fl=cl&fe=ci


9. Writing sample: THE HOUNDS OF LOVE HAVE GONE NONVERBAL, 2025
(excerpted)

An experimental essay that explores discipline, faith, disability and family alongside the production of its paired multimedia project, A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG. Mining his own autobiography as a queer, Jewish quadruplet growing up on a Collie-breeding farm in New Jersey, Lax considers puppy play and his own process in conversation with other dog films, dog ideas and dog projects by Laurie Anderson, Donna Haraway, Franz Kafka, Diana Thater, the Lassie franchise and others.

As an artist whose work incorporates their personal life and their connection with their collaborators, I have consistently faced resistance from audiences and viewers who misunderstand the intricacies of queerness and sexuality. 

For example, my film trilogy A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG (2022-2024) features a number of formal experiments and interventions with professional dog trainers and my family, who breed collies, juxtaposed with explorations of puppy play, including improvised scenes with myself and  members of the pup community. Even though the puppy players shown in the film do not perform any sexual acts, I have received resistance about the film’s content, with many saying that the work is “too gay” or that puppy play is immoral. Because of this judgement, many viewers fail to understand the broader ideas of the film or its context within my practice: I’m a queer fraternal quadruplet interested in the psychic, social, and economic realities of collective identity. My films are made in collaboration with non-actors, looking toward the “everyday ensembles” found within families, hobby clubs, and kink communities to better understand how we engage in collective activities, especially those that are considered strange, taboo, or queer, as mirrors of each other – not just individuals.

Because ToF’s existence is rooted in queerness and human sexuality, the ToF residency would be an ideal place to create, host, and assemble new work without censorship or judgement. Moreover, I believe the residency would further encourage my work’s growth and erotic capacity in a safe environment. I also believe that my practice in documenting queer communities and preserving alternative histories has become a political necessity as the US rapidly declines into an authoritarian state.

In addition to the research I plan to conduct at ToF, I also intend to visit ONE Archives at USC, the Los Angeles City Hall of Records, and mainstream and underground LA queer media outlets which are related to, or covered The Zone, such as The LA Times, Homo Riot Zine, and LA Weekly.

Francis is a filmmaker, writer, actor and producer. He moved to New York City on his own when he was only seventeen-years-old, where he attended film school. Francis started his career working with legendary avant-garde director Robert Wilson. Later, doing numerous performances art pieces internationally with the likes of Hauser and Wirth. Francis has also directed and produced independent films. As well as writing editorial pieces and interviews. His work has always focused on stories about disenfranchised communities, taking inspiration from his travels, art, fashion and literature. His Latest film “I Love My Mother” premiered, last October during Chelsea Film Festival in NYC and ASVOFF13 in Paris.

For this residency, I propose a photography series that delves into the intricate intersections of queer Latin identity and cultural heritage. My work aims to celebrate the vibrant, unapologetic spirit of the queer Latin community, drawing inspiration from the joyful and tender artistry of Tom of Finland.
As part of this project, I will immerse myself in the community in Echo Park, Los Angeles, engaging directly with individuals who reflect the diverse experiences of the queer Latinx diaspora. This will involve not only capturing their portraits but also listening to their stories and understanding the nuances of their identities.
In parallel, I will delve into the Tom of Finland Foundation’s archive, exploring the richness of his artistic process. By studying Tom’s work, I will find parallels in the way both he and I celebrate queerness—embracing joy, authenticity, and the beauty of unapologetic expression.
This project is more than just a series of portraits; it is an exploration of identity, resilience, and the transformative power of art. It will culminate in a body of work that reflects the vibrant diversity of queer Latin life, honoring the legacy of queer art while creating something new and meaningful.
In essence, this residency will allow me to blend community engagement with artistic exploration, resulting in a powerful, authentic, and inspiring project.

I plan to engage with the archive as a living source of joy, tenderness, and creative permission. What draws me to Tom of Finland’s work is its celebratory spirit, its warmth, humor, and unapologetic pleasure. Immersing myself in this archive, I want to study his process and allow its energy to inform my own: creating imagery that feels liberated, intimate, and generous. The parallels between Tom’s vision and my own work with queer Latin identity feel natural and intuitive, and I’m excited to be surprised by what emerges through that dialogue.
I heard about it through several friends and colleagues. Stuart Sandford, Charles Hovland, Konstantinos Menelaou from The Queer Archive and Lukasz Leja.

You know, a lot of things in life can get in the way of your artistic vision, especially here in America, the cradle of capitalism. As a young artist trying to “make it,” I ended up taking on jobs, cleaning up my work, and kind of putting a suit and tie on it just to survive. Over time, I noticed I got bored with my own work and didn’t really recognize myself in it anymore.
It’s kind of like that punk kid who went against the rules, who spent time in London and the gritty parts of New York, that same spirit of exploration and questioning was lost for a moment. This project, at its core, is about community and authenticity. And I think the Tom of Finland Foundation is the perfect place to really dive into that, to discover and celebrate queer Latin identities in Los Angeles in a way I haven’t seen before. It’s incredibly exciting, and I can’t wait to dive in.
Also, what really inspires me about Tom of Finland is how he made kink and eroticism beautiful and romantic, not something painful or grotesque. That’s the kind of inspiration I want to bring into my own work—something that’s joyful, whimsical, and truly beautiful.
