I Fucked Your Best Friend 


I,
fucked,
your,
best,
friend.

Bought him a drink
with the smile
you used to hate.
Rehearsed and cold.
"Yes, baby, I know."

I was only wondering
why he hated me so,
but we always knew that — 
I always told you.

On his knees,
looking up at me,
a runaway tear down his cheek,
he choked.
"This is what I always wanted," he said.

It was not revenge,
just a  sadistic, animalistic instinct,
rage and hunger —
basic and primal,
romance in self-destruction.

I'm ashamed.
I'm sorry.
You and I always deserved each other —
yes.
And I deserve what's coming.

Took boiling baths,
with French lavender, pearls, and tiger eye crystals.
I'm sober now,
but I still can't wash always      
the stank of my weakness, my wickedness.
I know I'm broken.

Was I trying to write more of our story or end it?
I fucked your best friend,
looking for you,
remaining smells on my pillow.

OH! The sickness of our generation —
fucking people we hate,
hurting people we love.

Did I tell you?
I fucked your best friend.
I fucked him,
exactly how we used to:
his back arched,
his nipples in my mouth,
his lips between my teeth,
just how you like it.

Have you ever met someone so cruel?
So weak, hurt, and scared,
in love, maybe? —
straight-up sociopathic.

Making Machiavelli look like Mother Teresa.

In our bed, I fucked your best friend.
His desire: heroin to a junkie.
Only love could muster up something so evil.

You left me without a fix.
I can’t even eat Thai food anymore.
So I fucked your best friend.

He said he didn't care,
'cause "It's been 2 years."
Clearly, I do.

So I injected him with demons of our past.
He's always been an empty vessel for despair —
a black hole for love,
my love, for you.

He'll take it to you.
You will see it in his eyes.
They will be my eyes.
I fucked your best friend,


When dreams become reality,
and poetry, prophecy.
Martinis turn into potions.
Drinking sorrows and possibilities.

The unspoken truth turns into a nuclear reactor,
constant fire burning it all to the ground,
till it implodes.
Between linen sheets and guilt,
bending truth into something more real,
lies,
true lies.

I’m sorry, no excuse would suffice.
I deserve nothing but your hate.
Perhaps that's what I was searching for.

A definite, irreversible
way out of this love.
Consumed by it,
on my knees, I pray to a god I don't believe in.

If to love is to fight for love,
I should have fought one more time.
Always one more time.

Forgive me and bury me
deep in the ice of your heart,
in the place where you keep your most ugly.
Reduce me to a lesson learned.

A scumbag of biblical proportions.
A bottle of gin. 
I fucked your best friend.

Immortalize me in the sky,
and forget me.
This work has not been printed yet

Spyros Rennt is a queer photographer born in Athens and based in Berlin whose work explores intimacy, eroticism, and collective identity within contemporary LGBTQ+ communities. Emerging from Berlin’s nightlife, his images move between tenderness and excess, positioning desire as both vulnerable and defiant. Working primarily through the photobook as an artistic form, Rennt has published four monographs (Another Excess, Lust Surrender, Corporeal, and Intertwined) constructing a sustained visual archive of queer connection and corporeality.

His work has been exhibited internationally, with institutional presentations at Berlin’s Schwules Museum and Amsterdam’s Het HEM, and the 2025 solo exhibition To Kiss Against the Fire, which framed love and intimacy as gestures of resistance in times of political unrest. In dialogue with the legacy of queer image-makers such as Tom of Finland, Rennt’s practice insists on visibility, sensuality, and self-authorship as acts of cultural preservation and liberation.

During the residency at Tom of Finland House, I propose to develop a photographic series focusing on contemporary queer erotic labor in Los Angeles through portraits of OnlyFans creators and independent sex workers. Conceptually, the project reimagines Philip-Lorca diCorcia’s Hustlers, but from within the community rather than observing it from the outside.

Los Angeles has become a central hub for queer digital creators who build their own economies around desire, image, and self-authorship. Unlike earlier depictions of hustling shaped by secrecy or exploitation, many of today’s creators consciously construct their personas, control their distribution, and directly engage with their audiences. At the same time, they remain entangled in platform algorithms, visibility politics, and precarious labor structures. I am interested in this tension between autonomy and vulnerability, empowerment and exposure.

My approach would be collaborative and intimate. I would photograph each subject in a space they choose, allowing their erotic persona and everyday self to coexist within the frame. I am drawn to the softness beneath performance, the labor behind fantasy, the negotiation between power and tenderness. These portraits would not sensationalize sex work, but honor it as a site of creativity, survival, authorship, and desire.

Developing this project at Tom of Finland House feels especially meaningful. Tom’s work insisted on unapologetic sexual visibility and redefined how queer bodies could be seen. Today, that visibility has shifted onto digital platforms, where queer creators stage themselves for both liberation and livelihood. I see this project as entering into dialogue with that lineage, expanding it into the realities of a technologically mediated present.
The intended outcome is a tightly edited photographic series and a self-published zine produced during or shortly after the residency. The zine format connects to queer histories of DIY circulation while remaining accessible and immediate.

Ultimately, I aim to document a pivotal moment in queer visual culture, where erotic labor, technology, intimacy, and self-representation converge. By situating these portraits within a broader continuum of queer erotic image-making, the project contributes to an evolving archive of desire, agency, and resistance.

Having access to the archive would allow me to closely study how queer desire, masculinity, and fantasy were shaped and circulated through Tom’s work and the broader materials preserved by the Foundation. I am interested not only in the drawings themselves, but also in the publications and ephemera that show how these images built community.

As I document my own community, the archive would serve as grounding, reminding me of earlier moments when visibility was both risky and radical. It would help situate my project within a longer lineage of queer image-making while reflecting on how authorship and agency continue to evolve.

I have followed the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program for several years and have applied previously because I feel deeply aligned with its mission. I also know artists who have participated, including Carlos Enfedaque, and through their experiences I have seen how transformative and supportive the residency can be. As a queer photographer whose work centers erotic self-representation, intimacy, and visibility, the Foundation represents an essential lineage for my practice. The residency feels like a natural step in my trajectory, both personally meaningful and artistically pivotal.

Parker McComb (b. 1990), a visual artist originally from Sarasota, Florida, and former New Yorker, is currently living and working in Seoul.

A graduate of Parsons School of Design, his work crosses disciplinary boundaries—blending photography, objects, and installation to explore the intersections of identity, emotion, and the human experience. Composing with a refined sense of tension, he constructs romantic frameworks where ambiguity and clarity coexist, revealing contradictions and emotional undercurrents through a visual language that feels both familiar and surreal.

In McComb’s practice, lens-based and conceptual elements invite introspection, tracing the contours of memory, desire, and emotional tethers. Often working within the liminal space between the physical and the perceptual, he shapes environments that hover between the intimate psyche and collective reality.

I first learned about the AIR through a former resident before he began his tenure. Sung Hwan Bobby Park and I met one evening in Seoul for chicken and beer after connecting through social media and realizing we were both practicing artists. During that meeting, he shared that he would be heading to the Tom of Finland AIR the following year. From there, I did my own research, and later, while visiting Paris, I connected with Richard Villani at NADA and followed up by email to share my intention to apply for the AIR when the 2026 application opens.

Ironically Romantic Dick Pics: 

The project leans fully into irony. It treats the most disposable image of the digital age—the unsolicited, casually captured “dick pic”—with the solemn gravity of a Dutch vanitas still life. In 17th-century painting, abundance and seduction were always paired with decay. A peeled lemon curls beside a silver goblet, a bloom begins to wilt, a skull rests in shadow. Pleasure is never innocent.

Here, the irony lies in elevating something impulsive and ego-driven into a carefully staged, painterly composition. The body is arranged like fruit at peak ripeness, draped in velvet, lit with chiaroscuro, framed by symbols of ephemerality. The gesture that once sought instant validation becomes a memento mori. The display of exposure is countered by the quiet inevitability of indifference. What is presented as virile and urgent is, in fact, already transient.

A crucial formal device intensifies this irony: the image is constructed through a cutout in a draped sheet, recalling both classical studio backdrops and the architecture of concealment associated with a glory hole. The sheet becomes both theatrical and devotional, its draping echoes Baroque fabric, while the aperture isolates the body as object. It is both revealed and obscured. The framing device transforms exposure into composition. The cutout functions like the dark void in Dutch paintings, a portal of shadow that heightens focus and suggests absence as much as presence.

Dutch still life paintings often celebrated wealth and sensuality while subtly critiquing them. This project does the same. A fleeting, digital act is staged with museum-level seriousness. The ridiculousness is intentional. The dramatic lighting, the heavy symbolism, the exaggerated romance all underscore how desperately contemporary desire seeks permanence in an environment designed for disposability.

The “romantic” framing becomes a satire of masculinity and self-display. The image is no longer a private boast but an artifact—preserved, composed, aestheticized. By placing it within the lineage of vanitas, the work suggests that modern self-exposure is simply another still life: a display of appetite, insecurity, and longing arranged for an audience. The irony sharpens when we recognize that, like the rotting fruit in Dutch paintings, the image’s power is inseparable from its expiration.

I’m interested in engaging with the texts within the archive as a primary source of inspiration for this project. The letters, personal writings, and ephemera offer intimate insight into queer subjectivity, desire, and coded language across generations. While my visual approach will most likely differ from the aesthetic language found in the archive, I’m interested in how both the textual and visual materials can inform a reinterpretation. The archive presents an opportunity to respond to its history through dialogue in expanding its legacy.

I work across collage and digital image-making, using both physical and digital processes to create materially dense, time-based images.

 My Glitches are created through a digital analog process that transforms moving images into stills through the act of scanning itself. Rather than editing or manipulating images in post-production, all distortion, interruption, and residue emerge during the scanning process, allowing motion, interference, and chance to shape the final image.
Glitching renders pornographic video into moments of encrypted intimacy. Bodies remain visibly present within the image, but are screened through a process of image scrambling. 

This process resists the clean, seamless consumption typically associated with pornography. Surface debris, smudges, and trace fingerprints are retained as part of the image, emphasizing the artist’s proximity, touch, and the physical conditions under which the work is made.

Scale is essential to the work. The images are produced at a resolution that allows them to be printed at human scale, collapsing the distance between viewer and subject. When encountered at this size, the body becomes intimate without being fully legible, forcing a more complicated position for the viewer that sits between attraction, discomfort, and responsible voyeurism.

Glitches are often produced as stand-alone works, and sometimes are combined with my work in collage using finely cut-out ink paintings, photography, and layered materials.

Sophia Poppy Ericksen (she/her) is a trans-femme queer artist born in Estes Park, Colorado, in the heart of the Rocky Mountains, and currently based in Denver. Her work has been shown in Denver, Seattle, and at RULE Gallery in Marfa, Texas.

Ericksen’s work encompasses collage and digital image-making, a practice centering the contradiction of trans visibility, where trans bodies are simultaneously hyper-consumed and violently rejected. Her work is diaristic, exploring intimacy and voyeurism, and shaped by an ongoing reckoning with identity, transition, and the legislative politics of erasure.

During the Tom of Finland program, I plan to expand my ongoing Glitch series by non-destructively incorporating archival queer imagery - particularly film - from the Foundation’s library as found material, engaging these works in dialogue with my existing practice. Until now, my Glitches have largely drawn from internet pornography, a space where queer bodies often become visible under negative fetishization, shame, and risk. Access to Tom of Finland’s collection offers connection to queer artists who documented their bodies and desires with intention and refusal of shame. During the residency, I intend to develop a new series Glitches that explicitly brings self-authored queer representations of intimacy into conversation with pornographic media shaped by voyeuristic consumption.

To be trans is to be a glitch - an interruption in the binary, a rupture in the system. Glitches explore the beauty that emerges when trans bodies are freed from surveillance, when privacy becomes a sanctuary, and when self-encryption allows for true autonomy. My work explores this through a digital-analog process that distorts and reinterprets intimate imagery, reflecting the relationship between body, identity, and perception. This series embraces malfunction as resistance - rejecting the demand for trans bodies to be legible, consumable, and controlled. Glitches insist that trans bodies are not errors to be corrected, but interruptions that reveal the system’s failure. 

Using a flatbed scanner, I manipulate found pornographic media in real-time, capturing a single moment stretched and scrambled. These Glitches mirror my own experiences of gender dysphoria, dissociation, and the hyper-surveillance placed on trans bodies - both fetishized and hated, desired and erased. Dust, fingerprints, and smudges remain embedded in the image, grounding the digital in physical presence, refusing a clean, consumable form.

I aim to be inspired by self-authored queer representations of pleasure from the Tom of Finland archives in comparison with pornographic imagery shaped by voyeuristic consumption. This contrast sharpens the critical force of my Glitches rather than softening them. Access to queer art that celebrates intimacy without apology will allow my work to clearly confront how shame-driven desire fuels both negative fetishization and the ongoing effort to legislate queer bodies out of existence.

My Glitches are about encrypting queer bodies from the exploitation of the cishet gaze, and are a way of symbolically giving them privacy and security. Exploring the material in the archives will allow me to find new avenues for my Glitches. Using material that was created to be explicitly viewed by queer people for queer people will change the direction of my practice in ways not possible under the limitation of using publicly available pornographic material as my sole source of queer media. The library will allow me to root my work amongst a history of queer artists who used erotic imagery to expose power, hypocrisy, and shame.

My use of archival and found material is non-destructive and approached with care and respect. The glitching process does not alter or damage original source material. I have extensive professional experience handling valuable artworks and archival materials through my employment in a high-end commercial art gallery, and I am attentive to ethical considerations around access, attribution, and preservation.

Carlos Us Santiago is a Portland emerging artist whose art explores the tension between intimacy and anonymity. As someone new to photography, his work is driven by curiosity rather than fixed methodology, allowing each project to evolve organically. He focuses on private moments and personal narratives around sexuality, often working with subjects who choose to remain unseen or partially obscured. Through this approach, he examines how vulnerability, desire, and identity can exist without full visibility.

Cruising is an art form—one that is intimate, romantic, and deeply human. This project seeks to explore cruising as a non-verbal language: a subtle choreography of glances, pauses, and gestures exchanged between strangers. These fleeting interactions—often occurring between people who have never met—carry a charged intimacy that is both primal and restrained, a quiet prelude to pursuit.

Approaching this work as I did at the beginning of my photography journey, I will engage directly with strangers, asking to photograph them while preserving their anonymity. Faces will remain unseen, allowing the subjects to exist outside of identity and recognition. This anonymity invites a vulnerability that encourages participants to reveal aspects of themselves they may otherwise keep private. Attention to environment—light, architecture, and place—will be central to the images, as setting plays a vital role in the atmosphere and tension of cruising.

Los Angeles will serve as my field of exploration and improvisation. I will seek out spaces and encounters organically, allowing the work to unfold through chance, intuition, and presence rather than staging or expectation. The city becomes both backdrop and collaborator.

In contrast to the dominant homoerotic imagery that centers the idealized Adonis figure, this project shifts focus away from physical perfection and toward the act itself. By photographing people across a range of ages, races, and body types, the work reflects the reality of cruising spaces as they exist—not as fantasy, but as lived experience. The emphasis is on desire, exchange, and possibility, rather than on spectacle or idealization.

My journey as an artist is relatively new, and I come to this work without formal training. I am still learning the technical aspects of photography, lighting, and editing, but the opportunity of engaging with other creatives and building community has been deeply rewarding and transformative. The more I learn, the more aware I become of how much there is still to discover, and that sense of openness fuels my desire to grow. This feels like a pivotal moment—an opportunity to redirect my path with intention—and this residency would play an important role in helping guide that transition. Spending time in Los Angeles would also allow me to connect with a broader community of like-minded artists whose demographic and sexual identities closely align with and influence my project.

Cruising or homoerotic scenes are the main undertone of my photography, and having access to work that depicts a big aspect of gay culture, would be enriching in terms of stepping back in time and learning from Tom’s craft.

My mediums are: Filmmaking, Writing, Performing, and Photography.

Chris Noon is a Chicago-based filmmaker, writer, performer, and visual artist exploring the psychological entanglements of pain, desire, and identity. Currently studying at the Chicago Center for Psychoanalysis, Noon utilizes homoerotic narratives of emotional extremity to investigate queer interiority. He holds a BFA from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago.

A volunteer at the Leather Archives & Museum, Noon is collaborating with artist John Neff on a documentary about Robert Blanchon and recently performed in Jessica Bardsley’s forthcoming feature on nightlife and cruising. His films have screened globally at festivals including Porn Film Festival Berlin, Chicago Underground, Hacker Porn (Italy), and Flat Earth (Iceland). His short Hunger received the Best Silent Film Program award at the 2023 Pan Eros Film Festival in Seattle and is streaming on PinkLabel.TV.

Noon is developing new interdisciplinary work rooted in the intersections of experimental storytelling, queer history, and the erotic unconscious.

I am drawn to transgressive queer lineages, exemplified by the works of Fred Halsted, Jean Genet, Rainer Werner Fassbinder, and Samuel R. Delany—art which frames gay desires and identity as something that is sacred, haunted, and intrinsic to the human experience. Their work possesses a melancholic core where queer subjects experience life to its absolute, sometimes painful, fullest. My practice seeks to capture these lineages, where the pursuit of pleasure is inseparable from the exploration of the soul. 

During this residency, I will develop a triptych novel composed of three thematically entwined short stories exploring the psychological and emotional shapes of masculinity, dependency, isolation, fantasy, and grief. The triptych is anchored by three distinct narrative atmospheres: 

-A gothic story of inheritance and self-obliteration, following a failed actor’s fated return to a site of familial trauma in rural Oregon. 
-A narrative centering on an aged independent pornographer navigating the friction between his failing business and a burgeoning, codependent surrogate-fatherhood with a closeted teenager in 1993 Chicago. 
-A surrealist exploration of submission and subversion, where the arrival of an infantile prisoner collapses the rigid hierarchy of a remote military encampment in Alaska.

The Tom of Finland Foundation’s extensive archives will act as a research laboratory, allowing the physical presence of queer erotic history to directly inform the novel’s formal strategies and narrative texture. I intend for the writing to change through my engagement with the collection's artifacts and private correspondences.

Parallel to the writing, I will produce a series of staged erotic 35mm black-and-white portraits and 16mm moving-image fragments with my own analog equipment. Featuring two to three performers, these works will act as a "visual language" to expand upon the written prose. The imagery will focus on fragmenting the male form into states of becoming:

-Devotional Gestures: Kneeling and reaching.
-Ontological States: Bound, exposed, and dissolving.

The residency will culminate in a solo exhibition that will feature the photographic series and 16mm loops alongside a public reading/performance of selections from the work-in-progress novel. By integrating the literary with the cinematic and the archival, I aim to create an immersive exploration of the somatic landscapes in queer erotic art.

answer: I am flexible regarding the specific room and season. My priority is the archival research and the dedicated time to develop this project within the Foundation's environment.

responses.answer:
I first encountered the program through Instagram shortly after moving to Los Angeles in the late 2010s. I was a few years out of high school and studying photography at Santa Monica College, where art-making was often confined to marketability and commercial output.

Discovering the Tom of Finland Foundation was important, proof that homoeroticism and transgressive art deserved rigorous, dedicated spaces. It became a long-term goal of mine to apply to this residency as well as to continue to search for queer artist communities interested in similar risk taking art.

I am especially interested in the unpublished papers and personal letters; these records will provide a blueprint for the 'erotic unconscious' that I aim to explore in my novel. The archive offers a rare opportunity for direct conversations with queer emotional foundations and fantasies. 

Additionally, the stark black-and-white aesthetics of Tom of Finland’s drawings are a primary influence on my practices. Gaining access to his original studies and reference photography will allow me to rigorously analyze his take on form, serving as a technical catalyst for my own 35mm and 16mm visual works.

I have been conceptualizing this triptych novel and its visual components for some time; however, I believe that developing it in California rather than Chicago would result in a different work entirely. To return to LA for this residency would allow me to incorporate my Southern California upbringing into a real dialogue with queer history.

Integrating the Foundation’s archives into my process will change the work exponentially, shifting it from a solitary writing endeavor into a collaboration with an important institution. I realize this is an ambitious proposal, however, I maintain a disciplined approach to pre-production—handling logistics like casting and scheduling in advance—I am prepared to maximize the three month window for both research and play that only this site can provide.

8. PUNISHER (excerpt). 
Context: This opening scene explores the immediate aftermath of an intense traumatic reenactment.  

Film opens on a black screen. The sound of two men panting can be heard from afar. The sound grows louder and closer, the breathing becoming more ravaged and desperate. As if the men were drowning and gasping for air.

CUT TO:

INT. JACOB’S HOUSE, LIVING ROOM. NIGHT.

Two men; hairy, shirtless, and muscular, are collapsed on the hardwood floor. Adrenaline taking hold of their bodies, the men are desperate to catch their breath. JACOB, early to late 40s, lays on his back with JAMES, late 20s to early 30s, laying on top of him. Jacob’s labored breathing is intertwined with his crying.

The house has not been cared for in many years. The floors are rotting, mold and water stains mark the walls and ceiling. The furniture denotes a once middle-class home from decades past. Faded family photos trapped in fogged picture frames surround the walls and end tables resting on both sides of the couch.

James moves first. He crawls off of Jacob and manages to sit himself up. Panting and mouth agape, James stares at Jacob with intensity. He wipes the sweat pooling at his eyebrows, blood stains dust over his knuckles.

Jacob covers his face with his hands, muffling his loud breathing. He pauses, lost in thought as James observes. Jacob removes his hands from his face and notices blood on his fingers. He touches his nose and realizes its bleeding.

James sees, then exhales sharply. He stands and walks over to the kitchen.

Jacob gingerly gets himself to sit up, using the wall behind him to rest his back. He closes his eyes and tries to slow his breathing. He wipes the tears in his eyes. Jacob hears James washing his hands in the kitchen. He looks up towards the direction of the sound and smiles to himself.

It is a long pause until James returns with a dirty dishrag and an empty expression. Jacobs smiles up at him, takes the rag, and dabs his nose with it. 

James returns to his seat on the floor, across from Jacob. He continues his stare.

 Jacob puts the rag down next to him and looks back at James.

JACOB
Thank you.

A beat of silence follows.

Then, Jacob crawls over to James. Too tired, James makes room for the other man. Jacob gets behind James and wrap his arms around the younger’s stomach. They stay in this position for a beat, Jacob holding James. Their breathing begins to calm.

JACOB (CONT’D)
That was a good one. I really felt it.

JAMES
... yeah.

Jacob kisses the back of James’ neck and continues to trail a line of kissing to his ear. James tenses up, keeping his head still to allow Jacob to nibble at his ear lobe. Jacob sighs, heavy and erotic, as James looks away. Jacob attempts to lick the inside of James’ ear. James jerks his head away instinctively.

My medium is collage, sculpture, installation, writing, and performance.

Thanos Valentine is a multidisciplinary artist based in Los Angeles and Ventura counties. His work explores gender and sexuality through his lived experience as a transgender sex worker. Valentine utilizes porn and fetish aesthetics to create artworks across collage, sculpture, writing, and performance. The pornographic in his work in contrasted by the esoteric, mundane, and romantic to create a diaristic effect akin to the lineage of autotheory and New Narrative. Valentine is interested in reclaiming tired tropes and fetishizations of the trans lesbian experience and comments on this by reclaiming vintage cisheterosexist porn while applying an art historical context.Throughout his work the common theme is justice and equality for trans people and whores and takes large inspiration from sex work and other labor activists.

If selected for the residency program, I want to build on my previous collage, writing, and performance. I'm constantly on the hunt for fresh erotic artwork to draw inspiration from. I would love to layer drawings from the archive with other sourced images for collage. I want to explore using my own nudes as a sex worker in these collages, mixing both Tom of Finland  and my own archival material. I think the writing in the archive would serve inspiration for text in collages, utilized in the form of ransom-esque letters as seen in my example works. Generally, in my collages I take inspiration from the music I am listening to and incorporate lyrics into my general practice. I think the writing in the archive would also fuel my performance practice, I want to do more BDSM scenes/performances based on the archives too. If given the space, I would invite my scene partner, a dominatrix, to do performances with me. I'm interested in filth a la John Waters and the trans Americana, and making beautiful erotic art. I want to invite my trans creative friends, the dolls, and the best people I think can support my vision to collaborate on photoshoots for my collages, and video performance ephemera and installations.

Ultimately, I want to construct a rigorous daily study of the Tom of Finland archives. I'm interested in BDSM, leather, and fetish aesthetics, along with just generally erotic LGBTQ+ art. I think the writing and erotic art in the archive would be especially important to my project, and would fuel my collage, writing, and performances. I like constantly ingesting new erotic literature to enhance my own writing practice, and being able to pull from historical queerness such as the archives would be especially beneficial to me.
I have visited former artists in residence in years prior, and my friends have also worked with the foundation.
I have an emotional support cat that I need with me if I were to move in. She is timid around other cats.

This would be my first residency, and I would need to familiarize myself with the archive, but the work I currently and previously have made, I believe makes me a prime candidate for this residency. I live, breath, and feel queer BDSM, leather, fetish aesthetics along with overall transgressiveness.

I'm not supposed to be smoking on the porch, but sitting right outside the front door is as far as I can go. I definitely do not have the energy to fuck. Presently, I'm snuggled into my bed, fresh out of the hospital, rubbing parts of my body on pillows and stuffed animals. My period cramps are killing me. All I can wish is to be stoned, consensually grinding on someone who truly cares for me, as I bury my face in their chest, breathing them in. In a culture that's so obsessed with orgasm and penetration, and I'll admit for a time I was once too. Take me back to the good ole' days where dry humping has no goal or finish line, it simple just is."

Samuel Snodgrass is an artist, historian, and educator. He received a BFA with a Visual & Critical Studies Thesis from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago and an MA in Design History and Material Culture from the Bard Graduate Center. He works across multiple media including collage, millinery, fashion, drawing, and poetry. Regardless of media, Snodgrass’ work prioritizes a physical relationship with material. In addition to his work as an artist, Snodgrass is a professor of fashion history at the Fashion Institute of Technology and works in Education at The Frick Collection. The Frick Shop recently commissioned him to create a collection of  one-of-a-kind hats inspired by the special exhibition Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture. In addition to regularly teaching guided school, college, and senior visits at the Frick, he also plans and teaches the Studio Series - soon to feature hat-making.

I propose to create a collection of erotic hats. Using couture millinery techniques, I plan to create sculptural hats that appear as abstract forms from one angle, but from another resemble a butthole, nipple, scrotum, penis, or clitoris. The collection will start with 5 unique shapes that can be remade in variations of colors and sizes. I have already made a prototype for one of these, (included in artwork samples). Later iterations will incorporate additions of hair, piercings, and other embellishments–hopefully inspired by specific individuals I meet during the residency. I will make as many variations as time allows. Once completed, the collection will be worn by the hottest models imaginable as they will strut through the Pleasure Park. There are a few LA-based photographers in mind to help me document this runway presentation.

I have an erotic relationship with felt and straw and ribbon and silk, but I have resisted exploring it further than in sketches and making a single hat. When I sew, drape, or block hats it’s almost exclusively by hand, yet I never use thimbles. I must feel the material in order to manipulate it. I don’t mind the pain of creation. Between my sore massaging fingers, materials moisten and relax. I listen by feeling. After a give and take, we reach a mutual understanding and I am able to stretch delicate fibers towards their potential for beauty. Though inherent to my process, I want to make this relationship visible to others.

I need more orgasmic stimuli to move forward with my wild ideas of buttholes made of silk and ribbon, or pubes made of ostrich plumes. I am always responding and reacting to art around me. While working and teaching in a museum full of Old Master paintings, I made a collection of hats inspired by Thomas Gainsborough’s 18th-century portraiture. Now I want to be submerged in Tom’s work, and look for sketched shapes that I can transform into wearable sculptures. I want a safe and erotically-charged place to produce this work, and the Tom of Finland Foundation Artist-in-Residence Program is the ideal place. My work explores seemingly incongruous elements of culture, juxtaposing high/low, historic/contemporary, and so on–most often through collage. I am eager to see how I can continue this practice through millinery. The residency is an opportunity to push myself further and highlight the erotic elements of a traditional couture medium.

I work in a museum because I can’t conceive of working in any other place. Archives turn me on. To produce this collection of erotic hats, I will look at this archive for inspirations in form and shape. How have artists before me crafted the erotic body in their medium? And how can I translate those forms into hats? My work is an impulsive reaction after looking at great works of art and interpreting them. This process is more literal when I make collage, but less obvious in hats. I need to study and research before I produce.

I have been a long-time follower of the Foundation and thought about applying for a few years. I applied previously in 2024. I first remember hearing about the Artist in Residence program ten years ago through the work of Stuart Sandford via Instagram. I remember being in my college apartment and obsessing over his work and setting the residency program as a goal for myself.

In addition to hats, I make collage. I plan to use the incredible Foundation archives not just as inspiration for hats, but also as source material for future collage work. (Only taking photos or making copies of things when appropriate).

Just out of curiosity, I am also interested to see if the archive has any information about or publications with work by illustrator Steven Blake. I’ve only seen his work in a few issues of Honcho from the 80s.

Vilda Ray is a queer Filipinx and Latinx multidisciplinary artist based in Los Angeles. Her influences extend from her diverse heritage being raised in the endless colors, waters, and sun of Guam. Focusing heavily on themes of duality, Vilda looks to expose those pieces that queer and trans people tend to stow away. Vilda has created works of graphite, ink, acrylic, charcoal and digital art highlighting queer and trans bodies and minds. Ray’s most recent work “Queer Bodies and Attachments” (2025), hosted by Painted Brain LA, was a glimpse into the emotional bonds of queer communities. Unveiled was a vulnerability in sharing anxiousness, avoidance, and security. As the work was created in honest yearning, it was shared to secure connections by practicing togetherness. Vilda continues on a journey of self-awareness that has been key in her effort to build a shameless future for queer and trans people of color.

“Share My Skin” series is an invitation to experience the joy in our bodies' collaborations. Love has proven itself true to me through organic exchanges of energy. Sometimes it is an unintentionally suffocating hug from a friend I see weekly. Maybe a tender bite on the neck of a lover who turns me on with just a look. Or uncontrollable laughter that morphs into silent and playful hitting with family. The significance of touch has begun to reign evident in my artwork, and the Tom of Finland Foundation artist residency will be used to share this sentiment. 

I will be showcasing the bodies of queer women of color and the joyful journey that sex, affection, and touch may take us on.This is the community I come from and a demographic that deserves to be championed just as much as the white centered hetero-normative bodies displayed in mainstream media. For this series I will utilize soft pastels, oil pastels, ink and acrylic on larger canvas ranging from 24x36 inches to 40x60 inches. There will be an emphasis on beautiful bright colors to convey the glee our bodies exude, delicately stretched upon silk fabric canvas. With the time allotted, I plan to produce 10 final pieces for this series.

 In the end, the public presentation of “Share My Skin” will primarily be attended by queer people of color, as it is human nature for people to be attracted to similarities found within themselves. However, if others are in attendance, I am hoping that they are left with a realistic  appreciation for queer women rather than the fetishization they may initially arrive with. Although there will be nudity, the real lesson is found beneath the skin. I envision these jumbo Godzilla sized bodies displayed high above the audience at the TOM house demanding their respect. 

 Our bodies can speak volumes, sometimes allowing more effective discussion than words allow. Highlighting connections of the skin on this platform will send a powerful message to queer women, as Tom himself sent to queer men during his lifetime. Tom of Finland created eye catching larger than life characters of everyday men, for everyday men. My work seeks to do the same. I want to see myself in my work, so do my queer friends, as well as the women who raised me. “Share My Skin” is a celebration of the happiness our bodies can potentially experience.

Of all that the foundation’s archives have to offer, I am most excited about reading Tom’s letters. I imagine that these letters would allow insight into what inspired Tom at different points in his career. Hopefully I can find some connections between his letters and corresponding art work. There is great advantage in having access to Tom’s archives as I am able to learn from work that is not always accessible and readily available to the general public. I see this as a mentorship by a queer elder that has moved on to the next life.

Being an artist in residence is an important step in my 2026 goals as an independent creator. Through extensive research of what Los Angeles can offer artists, a past 2025 resident artist I follow, Ryan Davis, inspired me to apply for this opportunity. I saw the beautiful project he had completed with the TOM foundation last year, and I knew that this resident program could be a great fit for my own work. Luckily, after months of patiently waiting, the foundation page posted on Instagram last month that applications had officially opened up. So here I am, ready to work!

The support this foundation can offer me would be greatly appreciated as I believe collaboration can make a much more significant impact than simply working alone. As I continue to increase the scale of my work, my space begins to no longer serve my goals towards growth and experimentation. Access to a larger workspace at the TOM house would support the rate at which my work is developed and produced. As this residency is a shared experience, I also look forward to engaging with the fellow residents as I love to learn from other creatives.

Although my visual art journey only began in 2023, I have dedicated myself to learning a variety of mediums and have consistently been setting new goals and gained much strength in meeting them. My drive is strong, and this year I want to direct my energy and effort to creating the “Share My Skin” proposal with the Tom of Finland Artist in Residence program.

My research stems from a desire to analyze the darkness of the night, the connections and bonds formed between the various personalities who inhabit it, and the shadows of my own soul. I would like to draw upon the archival material in your possession to create new graphic and sculptural works on handmade paper, which I would produce directly on-site (if possible). Into the paper pulp, I intend to incorporate cigar ash, fragments of flyers, and promotional material from the Los Angeles nightlife and the LGBTQIA+ community.
I aim to create artist's books made of paper and tar membrane, bound with bondage ropes and knots. The pages will feature an alternation of thoughts, drawings, and ideas conceived during the residency. I plan to produce 28 large-scale volumes (13 3/4 x 9 3/4 x 3 1/2 inches), echoing the format of the first edition of Diderot and d’Alembert’s Encyclopédie, published in the second half of the 18th century. My goal is to draw a parallel between Enlightenment Knowledge and street nightlife—life lived on the edge, between pleasure and pain, expectations and illusions that dissolve at dawn. The 'light' of culture in the form and scale of the books; the 'shadows' of desire in the content. The choice of tar membrane is a reference to the asphalt of the streets, symbolizing both the journey taken so far and the long battles that have yet to be faced.
I belong to the Leather community, and this would be an opportunity to closely study and experience the places that created and influenced the gay-leather collective imagination
I became aware of this opportunity through the 2025 Tom of Finland Art & Culture Festival held at Berghain in Berlin; while visiting the website, I saw that it was possible to apply for a residency

I am extremely motivated to participate in this residency. However, due to my role as an educator at a Fine Arts High School, my availability is limited to the academic break between June and August. I realize this is a specific window, but I truly hope to have the opportunity to live this experience.
Please accept my apologies for resubmitting my application. Having not received a confirmation of receipt for my previous submission, I wanted to ensure that my materials reached you safely. In this updated version, I have included a portfolio enriched with personal writings and curatorial texts to provide a deeper understanding of the conceptual and material nature of my work.

I hope you find the enclosed materials of interest. Thank you for your time and consideration.

My artistic practice revolves around themes of identity, body, consent, eroticism, and religious imagery. I openly expose my sexuality, not treating it merely as an aesthetic subject, but transforming my body into a form of diary. I sketch moments as they happen, driven by my belief that bodily memory never forgets.
I consciously seek direct eye contact with the viewer. By approaching drawing as a process of documentation, remembrance, and exposure, I aim to resist idealized representations and make visible the psychological and political conditions they produce.

I depict my own personal narrative, and through this process, I aim to make visible how the experiences of the people who shaped who I am today influence my artistic expression. I have always been interested in how the figures that contribute to the construction of the self become intertwined with subjective storytelling. Tom of Finland and TOM House offer a powerful space for me to explore this relationship. During the residency, I would like to draw the moments I experience “with Tom” as if keeping a visual diary.
Through an open call post shared on Instagram by a friend.

Getting to know Tom more closely is my starting point. However, I see this not merely as a biographical curiosity, but as a way of building a visual and emotional relationship with his world through every detail in the archive. I want to use the archive as a space of encounter with my own practice, placing Tom’s artistic practices in dialogue with my personal narrative.
No special requirements at this time.

Jurriaan Wesselink (b. Amsterdam, 1992) is a Dutch painter and performance artist. Jurriaan's work emerges from transformation processes and brings together worlds that are often kept apart: body and mind, spirituality and reason. During studying Econometrics and Liberal Arts & Sciences and working in finance, he began painting twelve years ago as a means of reconnection, painting himself out of the closet before he could do so in reality.

Over the years Wesselink works on the concept of "spatial queerness", distinguishing between identity acceptance and the dilemma of authentic queer space. His paintings and performances explore the body, its inner worlds and its surroundings as spatial architecture, for example he focused on the navel as an erotic void and origin point. His works are on the intersection of abstraction and figuration.

This residency will produce a series of paintings exploring how I relate to other bodies in intimate, erotic space. Striving to work with (sexual) partners as collaborators and models, I'll create work with a progression from distance to physical closeness.

My practice originates from direct experience: for years, I experienced queerness as fragmentation between my internal world and my environment. Painting became the medium where I could materialize queer space before inhabiting it physically. I painted myself out of the closet before stepping out. I painted my navel as a vagina before acknowledging my desire. Through performances, I exposed my body's interior, enacted falling from closets, and invited my mother to perform my rebirth. The navel, an erotic opening connecting me to her, became a central symbol.

Films like Guadagnino's Queer demonstrate spatial disruption: the protagonist appears simultaneously from above, below, and inverted, existing in fragmented realities. All of Us Strangers uses the apartment as a liminal zone where boundaries dissolve. These works show how queerness reorganizes spatial logic.

Until now, I've predominantly painted myself alone or distanced from others. While I'm attempting intimacy in life, dating, using Grindr, attending parties, I struggle to fully occupy shared space. Tom of Finland constructs realities where homoeroticism organizes everything. I want take that as an example and push myself further: painting myself in proximity, touching, merging with others. I need photography as a basis and as said will find that basis through sexual partners, sexual parties or through a call on Grindr. This will be a performative but honest approach, and so an experimental way of putting myself in connection.

Early paintings will maintain distance between bodies. Mid-series works will explore touch and proximity. Final paintings will attempt fusion, bodies becoming one, my openings relating directly to others rather than remaining self-referential.

I'll examine how Tom of Finland and other artists in the collection use bodies, but also holes, voids, and negative space as erotic elements. I want to compare works depicting isolated figures versus intertwined bodies. I'll study compositional techniques for showing merger, penetration, and physical connection. By analyzing original drawings, I'll understand how artists handle the transition from distance to intimacy, whether within single compositions or across series.

I was researching about places where I can develop myself as a queer artist. I came across the Tom of Finland Residency in this search and realised how this imagery was already in my mind. That is always a good sign. I also discovered that Bas Koster was a former resident, an artist from the Netherlands that I admire and know personally.

Paul Harfleet (he/him) is a British, London based queer artist that has been making work since he graduated from an Ma in Fine Art at the Manchester School of Art in 2004. He developed The Pansy Project in 2005 and moved to London in 2009 where he continues to practice as an artist. In 2020 he began a project exploring the cultural history of ornithology, the project known as Birds Can Fly can be explored here. Both projects run simultaneously.

I propose to use the residency as an opportunity to explore stories of LGBTQ+ life in Los Angeles and beyond. Through engagement with the Tom of Finland archives, I will uncover narratives that illuminate the challenges and resilience of earlier generations.
As part of this research, I will plant pansies at sites connected to histories of homophobia and transphobia, activating archival stories in the public realm through drawing, photography, and film. In dialogue with the aesthetic legacy of Tom of Finland, I will further develop my own visual language. This project aims to expand The Pansy Project as a conceptual tool for reanimating archives, deepening connections to queer histories, and celebrating the shared stories that bind communities across time and place. The Pansy Project will act as a conceptual foundation that will inspire new works in this unique context connecting my own queer historical work with new ideas, only possible within the confines of a residency design to protect and inspire queer work.

I plan to engage with the Foundation’s archives by studying Tom of Finland’s artworks alongside personal papers, photographs, and ephemera to examine how erotic representation has shaped queer self-image, resilience, and community. I am particularly interested in intimate and overlooked materials, letters, snapshots, and marginal histories, that reveal lived experiences often absent from official narratives. Alongside site-responsive pansy plantings as living memorials The Pansy Project will function as a framework for activating archival material and connecting historic struggles with contemporary queer visibility.

Swedish photographer who has been working in the field of contemporary art since the 1990’s. I have a long relationship with Los Angeles being a grad student at CalArts 30 years ago. In 2016 I earned a PhD in photography from the Gothenburg University in Sweden, my art practice is research based often in dialogue with archival material or art genres. An overall theme is an interest in photographically examining different aspects of belonging/alienation. Some projects have an outward direction including works on queer lives in Russia, traces of migration at the borders of Europe and most recently on homelessness in Los Angeles. The other can be seen as a movement inward where a more personal sphere of queer intimacy and community is portrayed. With a social commitment and roots in both documentary and conceptual art, a critical reflection on/with the photographic medium is also an important component.

I am part of a queer Shibari scene in my home city Gothenburg, and a segment of the practice is to tie in / together with nature. Last May I started to work on a new photographic series focusing on this latter part combining bondage with Nature Romanticism. My aim is to portray the radical potentials of queer tying, opening up for new forms of friendships and intimacies. It is a playful, tactile, meditative and sensual exploration that plays and challenges boundaries between power, desire and pleasure. The nature in tying sessions is a collaborator who is as demanding as it is soothing. Tied to a pine tree, one can feel a deep connection, but the rough bark leaves marks on the body as may the stingy ants crawling on the tree. In Nature Romanticism the landscape serves as a mirror of such things as emotions and the “sublime” (in the meaning of a mix of eeriness and beauty). By aesthetically referring to this traditional art genre (in terms of light, atmosphere and subjective experiences), I wish to lift forward also the sublime nature of Shibari itself such as pleasure and pain, or fear and trust.

At the Tom of Finland House I want to explore the possibilities of photographing Shibari in the garden, expanding the project into Californian nature together with the queer rope community of Los Angeles. I am friends with Captain (IG: @captaindaddysir) who is one of the foremost riggers in the city and we are talking about a collaboration. 

At my recent show “Tied” in Gothenburg I curated a performance with a local poet being tied by AfroDisiac and me while reading from her book. I would also like to use the garden as a space for creating live rope performances.

While photographing I am using a digital mid format camera and an analogue 4x5 camera. Lately I have started to play around with experimental developing of analogue film, using components of the nature where the photograph is taken (such as pinewood needles and seaweed) as the developer substance. I am in a beginning phase of this experiment and would like to elaborate this during the residency, equally using the plants in the garden for this purpose as well. Incorporating nature into the process of developing the film is a conceptual approach but it is also a much more sustainable way of doing photography.

Needless to say, I am very excited over having access to the archive, looking into what can be found on queer bondage in nature, on queer kink in nature generally, but also queer bondage generally. At this point, I will mostly use the archive for inspiration and learning about a history of queers doing rope, however I am working towards a research project on shibari imagery in photography and this would the start.

My name is Xavier Garza and I am a pinup and fantasy artist. I’ve been drawing since I was a kid, I can recall getting in trouble many times for not doing my class work and always drawing “naked ladies.” While in Junior High my classmates would pay me $5 for drawing them Betty Boop, she was main introduction to classic pinup and glamour.  It was around that time that I fully realized who I wanted to be when I grow up. Like many artists I spent most of my time alone drawing and dreaming up ideas of Warrior women, Goddesses, and ethereal beings that littered my sketchbooks. The past few years I have introduced more homoerotic and queer subjects to my work. Something I have always wanted to do but never had the courage to do so but I’m finding a lot of freedom in it.

My end goal is to finally create a series of queer pinups inspired by the techniques and styles of the great pinup masters that I look up to. Early on in my art career I noticed there was a big gap in diversity in the pinup art world and knowing that so many people who are different admire those inspiring paintings don’t see them selves represented really lit a fire of inspiration in me that I have played with from time to time but never fully allowed myself to embrace and execute to perfection. Like many I have experienced challenges in life and at times those personal obstacles interfere with your dreams. Here I have an opportunity to create with out much negative interference and I’d love to see what that experience could be like. I want to see what works I create that I’m not expecting to make.

This opportunity seems like the next logical step in my art career/journey. I cant imagine a place more perfect for an erotic artist like myself. I have been a long time fan of Tom of Finland and vintage erotica and it would be a dream come true to be surrounded by like minded people and embraced for my work as a queer artist.

I won’t fully know until I’m there fully immersed but I can imagine that I would never want to stop drawing and painting. Hopefully I can weave together some sort of new series of works that are unexpected to even myself. I’m open to newness and learning and of course I’m sure I will leave forever changed by this once in a life time experience.

Logan MacDonald is a Canadian gay artist, writer and educator who focuses on queer, disability and decolonial perspectives through his work. He is of European and Mi’kmaq ancestry, who identifies with both his Indigenous and settler roots. His artwork has exhibited across North America, notably with exhibitions at Küstlerhaus Bethanian (Berlin), John Connelly Presents (New York), L.A.C.E. (Los Angeles), The Rooms (St. John's), Articule (Montréal), Swift Current Art Gallery, and the Bonavista Biennale. He is currently an Associate Professor in Studio Arts at the University of Waterloo.

My proposed project for the Tom of Finland Foundation Residency centers on an evolving body of figurative drawing that reimagines, and queers, the visual languages of heraldary and crests through decolonial and erotic registers. At the core of this work is an investigation into how inherited systems of symbolic authority and tradition, upheld through coats of arms, family crests, and institutional heraldic motifs might be unraveled, re-embodied, and re-claimed through the queer homosexual gaze.

Over the last year, I have been developing a large-scale, dynamic figurative drawings that interweave homoerotic imagery with fragmented heraldic forms. This series of works are driven by questions of lineage, desire, and power: whose bodies are granted visibility, whose narratives are canonized, and whose presence remains unmarked by official symbology. By placing queer male bodies within and against the rigid geometries of heraldic visual codes, I aim to create figurative compositions that are both seductive and subversive, where desire operates as a tactic for rewriting visual histories and reconfiguring belonging.

This project emerged from earlier research into heraldry as a colonial aesthetic, which included a research visit to the College of Arms in London. There, I encountered not only meticulously drawn manuscripts but also carved figurative elements that animate and uphold these symbolic systems. That encounter heightened my awareness of how heraldic languages is a gaze that materializes power and exclusion;  it continues to inform my interest in producing work that both critiques and absorbs these inherited visual traditions, to reclaim with new contexts.

During the residency, I intend to further pursue this research through sustained drawing, material exploration, and conceptual deepening. The residency period would allow for an expansion of scale, refinement of compositional strategies, and the integration of new thematic concerns, including the entanglement of queer desire with histories of empire and migration into heraldry motifs and standard bearers. I am also especially interested in engaging with the Foundation’s legacy, and with Tom of Finland’s own bold reconfigurations of masculine imagery and homoerotic narratives, as both inspiration and critical context.

I view the residency as an opportunity to incubate a sustained visual inquiry, deepening the formal, erotic, and political dimensions of these drawings, and pushing this work toward new levels of intensity, scale, and resonance - and to enable my work to reach new audiences.

I plan to engage the Tom of Finland Foundation archives through sustained close looking and visual analysis, focusing on erotic figuration, gesture, and symbolic codes across drawings and photographs. My recent figurative drawing work relies on vintage male photography as source material, and I am particularly interested in archival photographs, preparatory materials, and unfinished or unresolved works that reveal process and possibility. These encounters will inform my large-scale drawings, translating and reimagining archival forms of queer desire and masculinity as a contemporary, decolonial homage.
A large wall to work on.

I approach this residency with care, curiosity, and deep respect for the Foundation’s legacy. My proposed project is not about replication, but about dialogue, an oppuruntiry to work in conversation with Tom of Finland’s archive to consider how queer erotic lineages can be hodnored, complicated, and carried forward through contemporary practice. I value process, experimentation, and ethical engagement, and I see this residency as an opportunity to work attentively within a shared history while contributing a distinct, evolving perspective.

Martin Dieudonné is an artist who boldly carves out his own creative journey, declaring his artistic identity without the traditional backing of art school. His work explores intimacy, desire, and human connection, capturing shared moments through paintings and performances where touch, movement, and presence take center stage.

Rooted in live modeling and queer experiences, his art challenges us to reconsider how we relate to each other—emotionally, physically, and socially—and how vulnerability can become a space of trust and beauty.

Martin Dieudonné thrives between the vibrant pulse of Berlin and the tranquil landscapes of Faucogney-et-la-Mer in the French countryside.

Being an artist in the gay erotic field, it’s hard to not hear about the residency as I follow the work of lots of residents alumni, and I have been advised since years to do an application to participate. Florian Hetz, photographer from Berlin is the one who is pushing me this time to apply.

I will use the archives to research the history of the artist-model relationship in queer art. Studying personal papers and photographs will show me the depth of this collaboration. This will directly inspire and deepen my own process of live painting, connecting my work to this legacy.

Cruising Through Paint: Somatography of Intimacy


During this residency, I propose to develop a series of live, performative painting sessions that explore gay intimacy, desire, and queer connection through the mediated touch of paint and canvas. The project translates the silent language of cruising—coded gestures, negotiated presence, mutual vulnerability—into a visual and performative practice. The canvas acts as a cruising ground: a membrane where contact is suggested, negotiated, and memorialized without direct skin-to-skin touch.


The core of the residency will consist of structured sessions with models from the queer community, invited to participate as collaborators in the creation of large-scale “somatographs” (body-writings). With a large canvas placed between us, I will paint using my hands, arms, and body to touch, caress, and map their form through gesture and paint. The model remains physically passive yet fully present—a witness and recipient of this mediated intimacy. This method transforms the artistic act into a ritual of erotic potential, focused on trust, anticipation, and the energy of shared presence.

The performance is slow and focused, but also playful making the process of looking, being together, waiting, and responding as central as the final image. It investigates how queer eroticism can be expressed through restraint, distance, and the poetry of suggestion.

Tom of Finland’s work fundamentally reshaped the visual language of gay desire, portraying sexuality as powerful, joyful, and community-forming. This project engages directly with that legacy by:

· Honoring the Gaze: Shifting the erotic gaze from a fantasy image to a lived, consensual, and collaborative process.
· Expanding Queer Intimacy: Exploring intimacy beyond the explicitly sexual to include tenderness, vulnerability, and silent recognition—key aspects of cruising culture and queer kinship.
· Creating Contemporary Archive: Producing a series of works that serve as portraits of contemporary queer bodies and relationships, building upon the Foundation’s mission to preserve and promote erotic art.

Outcome:
The residency will result in a new body of large-scale painted works and documentary photographs/video of the sessions. I propose to conclude the residency with a studio showing and/or performance, presenting the work and facilitating a discussion on queer intimacy, artistic collaboration, and the erotic as a space of creative and communal care.

This project seeks to use the Foundation’s supportive environment to rigorously and respectfully explore the erotic charge of presence, the artistry of touch, and the enduring power of queer connection.

responses.answer: Richard Sawdon Smith first came to international notoriety when his full-frontal photograph of ex-boyfriend Simon, who had an AIDS diagnosis, won the National Portrait Gallery Photographic Portrait Award but the media sponsors refused to publish the image, as the editor deemed it ‘too disturbing for a family newspaper’. As way of response, Richard, having his own AIDS diagnosis, put himself centre stage in his work, using his body to explore and challenge stigma and misconceptions of living with HIV.  A summary of this practice, the chapter, Listening to myself: AIDS representation – a personal perspective, can be found in the book HIV in World Cultures: Three decades of representation (2013). His recent photographic work, in part, explores the unknown of the future having survived so long, reflecting on the journey from ‘twink’ to ‘daddy’, from one pandemic to another, the multiple roles played and the new roles yet to discover.

‘Playing in the House of Tom’ 
The proposed title for the project comes from my research led practice, one situated in play, experimentation, risk taking and ‘not knowing’, a methodology that draws on the subconscious act of image making. The selection of images I have submitted as both examples of my practice and relevant to the project, highlight a direction I would wish to purse that gathers these various projects together under one roof. Series that include The Unknowing…X, Posture in The Rapture and The Ecstasy, Interruption, becuming and The Hunger Artist, to create something new and unexpected. The process of play means that there are no predetermined outcomes, but through a structured methodology the work will develop in response to the environment, being at the house, investigating the archive and engagement with other artist and residents.

An essential element of this work that I wish to experiment with in the House of Tom, has been the process of performing to camera, either cruising through and interrupting space through the presence of my queer [naked] body, or playing in the adult dressing up box. One created from outfits and objects accumulated from different parts of my life – from AIDS patient to university professor, gender-bender to porn star, go-go dancer to boxer, drag queen to leather queen, twink to daddy! - a form of playing myself, previous selves and personas I have inhabited or been categorised as through the years. Creating new personas to think about what comes next – what the future could hold. Central to this proposed project is that for nearly three decades I have used photography to develop a personal narrative that tells the story of living with HIV and fighting the stigma that still exist around the virus. Now with a normal life expectancy, Undetectable = Untransmissible (U=U) I find myself rewriting the self as an older gay man. Coming to the end of my academic career but starting a new artistic life, this is where the residency and the affinity I have with Tom of Finland’s work comes in, to allow a freedom to take risks, explore even further what could be, what one could becum. 

To note in terms of potential timing of the residency, I have initially chosen August to October, but also November to January would work, which room is less important, as the plan is to respond to the situation and environment.

Drawing on my academic background as an artist and researcher I fully anticipate, in fact expect the archive to inform my project through helping to develop ideas for new characters and personas to inhabit. To feed ideas on how I might interact with and interrupt the location, as I did with another project (not shown) at the Visual AIDS Archive in NYC and produce papers and articles in response to my findings.

My lecturer Peter Webb, who wrote the book The Erotic Arts first introduced me to the work in the 80s, but as a gay man and queer artist and I have been aware of Tom of Finland’s work and the Foundation for many years and know of other artists who have participated, such as Stuart Sandford and Florian Hetz. People have often suggested, because of my work, that I should apply.

I am employed as an academic, I have a very supportive research department keen for me to undertake the residency and will apply for a sabbatical to attend and may be able to draw on additional funding. I'm flexible to which room and if from August to October or November to January. Sorry I put all my images in one pdf didn't realise should be single, so added two more to make minimum of 3 uploads

Stephen Nestor Ostrowski is an American-born artist raised in Bangkok, Thailand and based in New York City. An autodidact primarily working with painting, drawing, and sculpture their work touches on consecration, faith, secrecy and exposition, response mechanisms, decomposition and belonging.

‘A World Lit Only By Desire’ will be a body of work concentrating on the multifaceted role desire plays in ones life. Desire being longing, or hoping and a conscious impulse towards something that promises satisfaction, my work done here will discuss absence and presence, how intuition and coercion play out in intimate sexual settings, and the friction between anonymity and safety within the LBGTQ+ community and for me personally as a survivor of conversion therapy. I will be analyzing the complexity of desire and sexuality as both deeply private and internal as well as experientially based in external public exploration. In contrast, how does desire lead us to discern what we reject and discard? What role do patience and potential play in this equation? How long is too long to wait? This will come to fruition as oil paintings of screenshots of empty chaturbate rooms, interiors absent of the object of desire (the body) and drawings in coloured pencil in my visual language as excavations into my own personal experiences with sex, desire, safety, satiation and belonging. I will also be creating amulet sculptures of site specific found discarded objects in Los Angeles (for example metal, leather, denim) that corollate to me and this work both culturally thematically.
I discovered this program through personal research.
I may have financial restrictions based on what is offered.

As someone who has worked in libraries and continues to use them as places of research and solace, I am extremely interested in access to the archive, especially the letters and ephemera in possession. Connection to elders and the past is paramount to both my work and personal life and to have access to excavate from a rich history of personal accounts within the themes I am exploring will provide a plethora of inspiration and rich insight into the worlds and work of my community ancestors as well as my own.

Virginia artist Shawnis celebrates queer joy by making whimsical paintings of cartoony cocks flirting, embracing, and 
engaged in all manner of sexual situations. As an akward, queer, weirdo from the middle of nowhere, rural Dry Fork
Virginia, Shawnis began drawing, writing poetry, making zines and generally has just lived life dabbling in art of all 
kinds from sculpture to drag to photography to acting. Shawnis has fronted 3 punk bands, worked in film/tv/and 
music videos, has shown work in cafes, galleries, libraries, and museums in Virginia as well as curated art shows of 
many others artists. Notable experiences have been residencies at VCU in Richmond and at SVA in New York 
City. Shawnis is inspired by and has a deep love for 70’s/80’s New York art/punk scene, odd balls, camp, kitch,
roadside attractions, and good books by and about people who have lived interesting lives.

I have been painting all my life on whatever I could find- cardboard pieces, wood panels, dumpstered
canvases. 5 years ago I decided I wanted to only paint on small 8” x 10“ store bought canvases to hone 
in on my style, to find my style, my thing. I dabbled with fun abstraction and my general simple almost
folky way. I dabbled and dabbled. 

Something clicked 2 years ago when I started a series of icons. I painted Devine and Morticia and RuPaul 
and Iggy Pop and just several of my personal heros. There were 16 of them all together, I did them all in a 
punk/folky/simple style. I kept dabbling, pop culture trash, mascots, whoopie cushions, nudes, or 
whatever I was in the mood to paint. A year ago, I painted Bert and Ernie in sexual poses. Then
like cigarettes in intimate poses, and shit like that. That led to making penises in sexual poses and that 
started a whole new series. 

These paintings are my best work and I want to keep going with them. I want to explore all options and 
possibilites (and sizes (not just 8 x 10s)) with them. I guess what I am trying to say is that my proposed 
project for the Tom House residency is painting dick pics. Cartoon cocks flirting, embracing, and fucking 
each other. And it helps that these paintings are so fun to make, and that they are erotic in a fun way, 
in a joyous way, and honey if it ain't no fun, homey can’t have none.

On the real though, I’m ready to explore, create, and take my art to the next level. I feel like being 
immersed in this residency could help me do that.

I’m not sure what all is in the archives there but I would love to explore. I crave erotic photos and ads from 
the back of vintage gay porn mags, I’m all in. I would dive head first into the Tom House archives. 
I could totally see making paintings in my style that reference his work. And also would love to see what 
other artists I could do that with. My whole thing is homage really. All I do is read and watch documentaries 
about John Waters, Leigh Bowery, Andy Warhol and all the queers that came before me. I love to spend my 
time basking in their queer glory and getting inspiration and raison de etre.
I'm not exactly sure, probably instagram.

I would love information of scholarships if available. I could even help out at the museum, archives, gardening a couple days a week. I will make it work, beg or borrow money from friends, but this does cost (with supplies, living expenses, and rent) a bit more money than I have unless I did a shorter time which I would hope to not have to do.

I selected the first session of Lawton's Lookout but would be interested in and future sessions. The only one that may not work for me is the one in June

Harsh noise electronics with extreme performance art including tattooing, needle play, body stitching, hooks, scarification, and live blood letting. dagger wound is a noise masochist bringing abrasive sounds with a strong political message and a cathartic and transgressive performance style that slices through reality. he is motivated by his desire for a better world and his lifestyle as an underground traveling trans freak, sex worker, pornstar, farmer, and leather faggot. he has toured nationally and internationally as 1/2 of mirrored fatality and as dagger wound and has collaborated with multidisciplinary queer and trans artists across the world. he is the sword and the wound, the guillotine and decapitation. He is currently creating a body of work honoring Lawu, his beloved life partner who tragically and horrifically died in a car crash.
