responses.answer: I want to look through the archives and I will be directly looking for work created during the AIDS crisis and work around people loving their loved ones. After experiencing unexpected death, I have a deep relationship to death and the bureaucracy that comes after and I want to find how people have been able to make sense of it or at least grieve throughout history. I will also be looking at the erotic work of the collection to get inspiration for my visual and film work. I am an avid researcher and I am deeply passionate about being inspired by other queer and trans artists.

I believe that my work is an impotent intervention because I occupy alot of intersections as a poor trans person of color from a conservative muslim background with a history in sex work, porn, bdsm, and erotica as well as a multimedia artist who has worked in performance, music, theatre, film, photography, visual art, body modification, and writing. I also think that as a person who has survived extreme death and also the process of having to bury my longterm partner at the age of 27, I have life experience that has led me to create extremely visceral and meaningful art. I also am dealing with the rising impacts of gentrification in LA and having access to the Tom house would also give me a stable space to work on this project instead of me constantly dealing with housing instability. I also haven't updated my personal resume because I have been trying to cope with the death of my partner but I have also been performing nationally and internationally and done residencies at A Love Supreme, Darkness Visible, and Sex Workers Giving Fellowship Third Wave Fund.

For the residency I want to create a three part project that is both visual work, performance, and a film. For the visual work element, I want to take pictures of the queer and trans people of color in my life showing different scenes that reflect the Tom of Finland drawings but from the perspective of the world that I see and get exposed to. I want to shoot these scenes at specific spots in LA that are home to BDSM, leather, and queer and trans cruising events. I believe that creating an authentic archive of our lives is extremely important and also creating an archive that represents the different bodies and types of people that are usually left out of the archive. For the performance element, I have been engaging in kink, erotic, BDSM, leather, and body modification performances for years and I want to do one that is site specific to the Tom of Finland house and  is also a response to some of the archival work I will be doing around mourning and grief. I want to do a bloodletting ritual performance  that will utilize a soundscape created with quotes from the archive that centers around grief and loss of loved ones. So much of our history is ruled by epidemics and loss, I have my own personal experience of losing and having to bury my trans life partner and I want to explore the archives and find resonance with people throughout time that have also shared this experience or varying degrees of losing the ones they love. I will also base the performance on the art that I find and be inspired through movement, visuals, and cultivating a live performance that reflects the challenges of mourning and the pain of loss. I also was recently made aware that many people have been buried in the backyard and I would also want to get field recordings from sounds in the backyard that I add to the live music score. Lastly, I want to create an erotic t4t porn film with my current partner that builds on the idea of archiving contemporary explorations of queer and trans love through a freaky and kinky lens. Each of these projects are extremely dear to my heart and visions and center around community visibility, grief and death, and queer and trans love. I believe that these are all themes that hold truth within the Tom of Finland Foundation and it would be an honor to explore these projects there.

I heard about the Tom of Finland program through my peers including M Black and Odette Engle. I saw that Odette did a previous piece with one of the former artists in residence. Odette and I have worked together on multiple body modification pieces including a live performance involving my skin being pierced with safety pins and another erotic porn piece with hooks and live piercing. I also was a part of M Black's exhibit contributing some pictures of me pissing and also did a live shoot involving bondage, needles, and scarification. After becoming aware of the residency, I realized this is something that would really support me.

Lucas Oakley is a New York City based artist who’s work focuses on pop culture, consumerism, advertising, technology, spirituality, social media and flaming homosexuality. Oakley uses high-camp and sharp-witted concepts to create a compelling multiverse of madness that disrupts the current art and fashion industries that, all too often, take themselves way too seriously!

I recently discovered a self-published book by gay erotica photographer Charles Hovland that documents his work from 1986-2009. I was very intrigued by his work for Mandate, Honcho, Torso, Playguy, Inches, Black Inches and Latin Inches. As a 90’s baby born in ’96, I was not exposed to Queer erotica until the digital revolution was in full swing, and was totally unaware of the porn industry predating the internet and the breadth of erotic print media in existence. I very quickly took a deep dive into vintage Queer erotica and recognized an art form that has been almost completely destroyed by the rise of smart phones, Only Fans and the contemporary trend of amateur and DIY pornography. I am absolutely floored by the beauty of vintage queer erotica, particularly from the 60’s through the 80’s. I’ve been extremely inspired by Queer erotic magazines and their fashion editorial-esque shoots that celebrate the male form and same-sex attraction. I have began creating works for what I’m calling “The Porn Series,” in which I draw fine art portraits inspired by the imagery I’ve discovered in my research. 

The intention of this body of work is to take an industry that has historically been viewed as low brow and elevate it into the fine art space. It also serves as a commentary on the current state of the porn industry and its lack of refinement and taste. 

This series is an exploration into my own authenticity and unashamed self-expression. Throughout art history, there are many examples of erotica exalted by critics, but they almost always feature and objectify the female form. At times, I have felt that in order to be taken seriously as an artist, I must explore the female form, but my attempts reeked of inauthenticity. Subconscious conditioning has allowed me to believe that Queer erotic art is incapable of achieving commercial success, but as I have learned to accept myself and my sexuality, that illusion has faded. Some may find these works subversive and obscene, but I feel called to share this facet of myself in my art, and feel that this series will be important and revealing in my pop art legacy. During my time at the Tom of Finland residency, I’d like to develop “The Porn Series” further, expand into color and mixed media portraiture, and eventually design and create a “Lucas Oakley” erotica magazine.

As of now, my research on Queer erotica is limited to digital archives I’ve uncovered online. Many of them have paywalls or are censored. The Foundation’s archives are essential for the research of my series, which is entirely inspired by vintage erotica publications. Having unbridled access to the Foundation’s archive will allow me to deeply explore the lost art of the erotic magazine and take this series to the next level!

When I shared my first “porn drawing” on my Instagram story, one of my followers told me I should look into the Tom of Finland residency. I’ve been a long time fan of Tom of Finland, so I immediately checked it out. I was surprised to find Lukasz Leja, who I know through the NYC art scene, had participated in the program. I asked him all about it and decided it would be a perfect fit!

responses.answer:
My practice begins with photography, working with both analogue and digital processes that move between documentary and staged imagery. I then extend this work into drawings or digital paintings, using images as a starting point for further interpretation.

I produce prints, fanzines, and artist books as a way to share stories and to keep the work physical and accessible. Across these mediums, my work engages with queer identity, male bodies, and lived experience.

I am an LGBTQIA artist based in San Francisco, working under the name XY Cæstello for my art practice. My work explores themes of identity and desire, often engaging with erotic art as a space of visibility, resistance, and self-definition. I work across drawing, photography, and print, moving between observation and imagination.

I was born in Athens-Greece, where I studied graphic design, and later studied fine arts at UAL London. Living and working across different cities, including time spent in Berlin and now in California, has shaped my sensitivity to queer subcultures, image-making, and storytelling. Alongside my art practice, I work as a branding designer, collaborating with cultural institutions and queer-led projects. I am openly HIV positive, and my work is informed by lived experience and care. My work has been presented through both solo and group showcases, as well as independent and self-initiated projects.

During the residency, I want to develop a new body of work rooted in my ongoing practice, focused on masculinity, desire, and self-representation. I will work with photography and drawing, grounded in research and contemporary image-making, and experiment with projection and archival material.

A main outcome of the residency will be a self-published project, such as a fanzine or coloring book. This will build on my ongoing project Boys of San Francisco, a body of work in which I document and imagine queer male intimacy through portraits and drawings of everyday people in the cities I live in. For the residency, the focus will shift to queer life in Los Angeles. The final form will remain open and may be shaped by the archive, the city, and lived encounters during the residency.

Set within Los Angeles, the work will respond to the city’s queer landscape, its bodies, codes, and social spaces. The residency offers a unique opportunity to engage with the Tom of Finland Foundation archive as a visual and cultural reference, allowing this material to inform my work through study and reflection.

My process moves between observation and construction. I plan to create illustrated and photographic studies of queer people, allowing images to be repeated, redrawn, and transformed across different formats. A work may begin as a sketch and later appear as a projected image or printed fragment, blurring the line between original and copy, fantasy and documentation. Alongside image making, short notes, texts, or visual codes may emerge and become part of the work as narrative layers.

At its core, the project reflects on how queer identities are shaped, shared, and remembered today, with an emphasis on desire and everyday presence. The residency also offers an important opportunity to share the work through public presentation, increase its visibility, and open space for dialogue with wider audiences. Being part of the Tom of Finland Foundation community would allow me to build meaningful connections with other artists, curators, and cultural practitioners in Los Angeles, supporting future collaborations and the continued growth of my queer practice.

At the Foundation, I want to blend my own visual language with the archive, allowing its erotic codes to shape new work and create a dialogue between past and present queer expressions. I already work with archival material, including vintage queer zines sourced from Autoerotica in San Francisco, which I scan and reinterpret through drawing, photography, and print.

As both a designer and an artist, I am interested in studying the archive’s imagery, films, publications and translating selected elements into my practice. I see the archive becoming an active layer within the project and its outcomes.

The program has long been on my radar, but now living in San Francisco, it feels like the right moment to apply, especially given my experience of both European and American queer cultures.

It was also mentioned to me by a friend and collaborator, Konstantinos Menelaou. We have worked together on projects including The Queer Archive and the Kavla zines, and he previously took part in the residency.

Seeing the work of artists connected to the Foundation whom I admire, such as photographer Florian Hertz, whose practice resonated with me during my time in Berlin, further encouraged me to apply.
I do not have any special requirements at this time.

Tom of Finland’s work has influenced my practice through its freedom of expression and commitment to queer and erotic art. Being able to spend time in this context feels like an important moment in my artistic journey.

Beyond the archive, working inside Tom of Finland House itself is especially inspiring. As a lived space with a long history of safety, creativity, and connection for LGBTQIA artists and communities, the house offers a unique environment to create work in situ and to exchange ideas naturally. I would approach the residency with openness, care, and deep respect for the Foundation, the house, its archive, and its community.

Besides my art practice, I also work as a graphic designer and would love to contribute these skills in a supportive way if useful to the Foundation or its community during the residency.

Textile sculpture, latex and leatherwork, performance, text

I graduated with my MFA from Bergen Art Academy in 2025, and have since exhibited in a group show at Gyldenpris Kunsthall (NO) and solo at Nonneseter Kapell (NO). In the summer of 2025 I was in residence at Fish Factory Arts (UK). Notable previous shows include KODE, Bergen Kunsthall, and Visningsrommet USF, all Bergen, Norway. I am part of Conditions Studio’s Online Programme cycle 2025/26, which support artists through online tutorials and group sessions. My work has been acquired by Swedish museum project Homografiska Museet, building Sweden’s first queer museum and collection.

In my work, I explore hierarchies of purpose and erotics through sculpture, performance, and text. I work to queer ideas of functionality and pleasure, producing work that brings together fragments - textual, sculptural, and performative -  of larger alternative narratives. I am interested in the archive as a failing device: failing to encompass a totality of history, and to archive the ephemeral and the poetic. In response to this, I make soft sculptures of “failing” functional objects, use fragile materials such as latex, and produce performances that move between research-based writing and poetics.

At ToFF, I will produce a series of works on latex using a technique of painting and rubbing with boot grease. This technique achieves a multitonal shadow image, with deep black where the latex is uncoated and fuzzy grays where grease meets talc powder. The “incorrect” use of boot grease, a substance that is both purposeful and erotically charged, on a material that it will effectively age and warp, causes a break in the solid, traditional nature of the painting medium. In response to conventions of figurative drawing, I will experiment with depictions of bodies and erotic scenes. I will combine these human body elements with abstracted images of vehicles, industrial machinery and tools that allude to other hierarchies of function and production. The combination of tools and bodily forms is recurring in my practice as a way to destabilise the hierarchy between capitalist productivity and the production of pleasure for pleasure’s sake. At ToFF, I will push the relationship between these different bodies and values by exploring how they can come together in a directly figurative way. 

The project envisions utopian, non-conforming bodies, and the fragility of the structures that order identity and desire. I am interested in the properties bodies shaped by an approach to pleasure and desire different from the one that governs our society, and I will look for these properties within queer histories of desire. This is a vital jumping-off point for the experimental work I will be doing: incorporating  references from these stories of leathersex, community, and pleasure into temporally unstable visual works. As I work with latex, which is notoriously difficult to maintain, I want to incorporate archival findings more or less directly as a question posed to the archive: what is the purpose of archiving queer experience? What can the archive hold, and what is the value of  preserving something “forever”?

I will use the archive - and primarily Tom's own sketches and works - as a starting point to understanding Tom’s specific approach to pleasure, and incorporate it alongside my intention to reflect active, shapeshifting queer pleasure and making. In the resonance between these traditionally made images and the materials I will bring, I hope to discover unexpected formulations and expressions that reflect on the archive as a potential tool to formulate not just past but present and future expressions of queerness. I want to go in dialogue with the items in the archive and see what combining them with my making process can achieve.

I have been aware of the ToFF AiR program for several years, namely through the Instagram. Having worked in the UK, and currently being part of the Conditions Online Programme based out of London, I have followed Sweatmother’s work closely for a long time and took great interest in the work he shared from his time in residence at Tom House.

(Excerpt of the text FRATERNISE (2025))

in secret i put my nose deep into your underwear. my moans are a secret even to myself, they give themselves away as they hum down my ribs, slip out through my toes into dry ground. i rumble to get free, deep in there, i am like in a scabbing forest, i like it here, i like it. 

in secret i dig my heels into the wet sand that is wet only because it is under water, which is wet. in secret i dig my toes in too. 

i wonder what it is like to wrap my hand around your neck, or not wrap, i would cup it and dream my fingers and the palm of my hand could wrap around you all the way like a brace. i wonder what it is like to hold a neck and feel needed, like you need me. it has been so quiet here. i like it here. i wonder when you will come back for me. i left all this space for you, i am wearing through in places where i made space for you. 

in secret i put my nose deep into your underwear. i wear them on my head and peak the tip of my tongue out the flap. i taste what i think your skin would taste like if i licked you but i couldn’t see you, didn’t know you were you. in here i am in my favourite armour, a knight of your sweat, a boy of your making. 

they took him in the night, out of my chest of treasures and down to the laundry room. it was an assassination, no worse, he was a changeling, he was not the same after that. they took my friend from me, and they said because his skin was not his anymore he was better. when i was a kid, all my best plush toys were boys. 

i rip my skin off so i can become someone else. i wear my skin like a jacket and a safety pin at once, in and out, inside out. threaded through myself and dressed up claspy - undeniable barrier - no porosity and no transition - zero to a hundred - the glossy latex the zipper and next the soaked through skin 

a raisiny finger hand and body that goes matt, moist, saturated, groovy 

in secret i put my nose deep into your underwear because i have never seen anyone wear them like you before except for in the archives and on the boys that beat up my friends. i tuck the waistband in and my thumb further in. i ask my brother what he has been up to, i thank my lucky star that i don’t have a brother. sounds a mess. in secret i hope that your skin smells just like mine, in secret i grow new hairs and flaps.

The proposed project I wish to do during my residency is to create portraits and scenes of various kinks using mostly Analog (120 film/Slide Film), Digital (Medium Format Cameras), and alternative photographic methods.  I then plan to take all these images and produce large pieces to be shown, as well as culminating all of them into a book; The book will contain quotes, context and notes from not only the participants but also myself. This will all be presented at once near the end date of the residency for all audiences to view. 

The main intent in this project is to create a body of work that is not only inclusive of as many different types of body styles and kinks as possible but can be enjoyed by a multitude of people in the future as time continues to move forward. This project will stand to continue to solidify the kinks/leather/etc. communities that are a part of our lives.

I plan to use the archives by reading the letters and engulfing myself into the drawings and photographs to create work at the deepest level from raw exposure. I plan to use that combined with being surrounded by the art to push my imaging to speak to the viewer stronger by having even more informed thoughts about my subject matter. I believe that taking note of all the subjects (The kinks, the sexual notions, etc) will imbue the list of images to create with priorities.

MFD uses a combination of digital and analog photographic methods to create images with strong moments to capture not just the imagery of a subject but the soul of it.

I learned about some of the artists that had done the residency in the past a couple years ago and thought it would be a dream to be able to stay there and create in that headspace as well. The sizes of my work can vary due to me using medium format in both analog and digital so if certain dimensions are needed it will not be a problem.

While Touko Valio Laaksonen’s international influence is widely acknowledged, its presence within Australian queer history and artistic expression remains under-examined. This influence becomes particularly visible when tracing visual, erotic, and cultural lineages across the late twentieth century. In 2006, Blue magazine ran a feature on Tom of Finland. While not a monumental publication, it was a very mainstream low-brow magazine being consumed by a broad section of Australian homosexual men. This feature is indicative of Tom’s penetration into the cultural lexicon of mainstream gay Australia by the 2000s. When viewed alongside earlier artistic trendsetters associated with Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras — including David McDiarmid and Peter Tully — and figures such as Juan Davila and Gary Carsley, Ross Harvey and, later, Tony Albert, Tom’s aesthetic and ideological influence on Australian queer art becomes unmistakable.
Letters from Tom is a speculative history project that imagines relationships, correspondences, and points of exchange between Tom of Finland and notable Australian artists, and cultural figures from the 1970s through to the early 2000s. The project takes the form of a hand-drawn animation, a series of drawings, and creative writing fragments that collectively work to annotate and expand queer histories that are often fragmentary, euphemistic, or absent from official archives.
Rather than attempting historical accuracy, the project embraces speculation as a queer method. Letters, magazines, photographs, and drawings become points of ignition: a mailed image prompts desire, a published illustration travels continents, a letter sparks intimacy. Through imagined encounters, Tom’s work is positioned as an active agent in shaping artistic confidence, erotic possibility, and visual language within Australia’s queer communities.
During the residency, I will develop new drawings and animated sequences that trace these exchanges across time and geography. I will illustrate parellles between Tom’s archive and notable Australian works. Working through hand-drawn processes allows slowness, intimacy, and labour to mirror the acts of letter writing and image circulation that underpin the project. The resulting work proposes a queer history that is porous, embodied, and sensorial — one where influence is not merely stylistic, but erotic, emotional, and communal.
Ultimately, Letters from Tom positions the Foundation’s archive as both historical record and generative site, asking how queer histories are transmitted, misremembered, and lovingly reanimated through fantasy, desire, and drawing.

My proposed body of work will engage directly with drawings, letters, photographs, and ephemera from the Tom of Finland Foundation archives alongside newly developed speculative scenes. Prior to the residency, I will interview Australian artists about Tom’s influence on their lives and practices. My process then begins with intensive archival research and drawing, followed by studio development using models or performers to visualise scenes drawn from the archive. Through this process, Tom’s letters and drawings are reanimated and collaged into a final exhibition or video outcome.

During residencies, I maintain a daily practice of completing one resolved work on paper alongside visits to archives or cultural sites, a methodology influenced by Brett Whiteley. This approach has proven highly effective in Rome, Paris, and regional Australia. Following the residency, I plan to develop exhibition proposals and partnerships with Australian institutions, extending the life of the research through national touring and presentation.

Todd Fuller is an Australian artist, curator, and arts administrator whose practice focuses on queer history, storytelling, and the gaps within official archives. Working primarily through hand-drawn animation and drawing, his work uses speculation as a queer method, transforming fragmentary historical evidence into intimate, often bittersweet narratives that foreground desire, companionship, and vulnerability while challenging public history narratives of Australia.

Fuller’s practice frequently draws from legal records, personal correspondence, popular culture, and vernacular archives. Time is a key material in his work, with frame-by-frame animation used to slow history down and make space for reflection and emotional connection. His work has been recognised through multiple art prizes and has toured and been exhibited nationally.

Fuller has undertaken international research residencies in Rome and Paris, alongside extensive regional Australian residencies, which have shaped his archive-driven methodology. Alongside his studio practice, he works as a curator and educator, and was an inaugural curator at QTOPIA Sydney, advocating for queer visibility within public institutions.

Dane Eissler (they/he/it) is a theater maker, visual artist, educator, and creative researcher. Rooted in queer theory and phenomenology, their interdisciplinary practice uses play, imagination, and embodied experimentation to investigate the formation, fragmentation, and fluidity of identity in contemporary life. Dane works across performance, visual art, puppetry, and design, often blurring disciplinary boundaries to explore how selves and world-perceptions are constructed, undone, and reimagined. Dane has an extensive creative history with EgoPo Classic Theater, where they have directed, designed, performed, curated, and served as Artistic Producer. In Chicago, Dane was co-founding Artistic Director of A Dead Whale Productions, creating original interdisciplinary performances. A 2024 Independence Foundation Fellow, they studied puppetry in Indonesia with Papermoon Puppet Theatre. Proud member: Trans+ Virtual Centre of Excellence (an international inter/transdisciplinary research/advocacy group). Adjunct Professor: Rowan University and Temple University. PhD Candidate: Transart Institute via Liverpool John Moores University (UK).

Inspired by Larry Mitchell’s "The Faggots & Their Friends Between Revolutions," this proposed project takes the form of a character-study exhibition and immersive installation activated through performance. The work centers on themes of otherness, chosen community, and radical authenticity, imagining queer mythological beings who, existing outside linear time and normative social structures, are preparing to engage in their next revolution. Using Mitchell’s book as a conceptual touchstone, the term “queer” (used contrary to the book’s assimilative definition) is intentionally redefined as more than identity alone; queer becomes a disruptive, regenerative force that resists assimilation and reclaims the grotesque, the absurd, and the joyful.

Building on my ongoing practice of illustrating grotesque, cartoon-like characters, I would develop a series of character portraits alongside medium-to-large-scale puppet creatures. These beings emerge from nature as guardians, tricksters, and helpers preparing humanity for an unnamed but inevitable next revolution. They are not heroic in a traditional sense; instead, they are awkward, tender, humorous, and strange, embodying survival strategies born from marginalization. Each character would have its own mythology, role, and emotional register, forming a loose ecosystem rather than a singular narrative.

The exhibition component would include drawings, sketches, and character studies rendered in pen and ink, watercolor, and mixed media, allowing viewers insight into the development of each creature. These works would be complemented by the sculptural puppet forms themselves, which function both as static objects and as vessels for performance. An immersive installation element would transform the gallery space into the creatures’ hiding/waiting place - part sanctuary, part nursery, part ritual site - encouraging audiences to step out of everyday time and into a speculative queer world rooted in both a mythological ancient time and futurity.

At scheduled times, the puppet sculptures would be activated through an interactive performance that operates as a ritual or communal celebration. During these moments, the creatures would move, interact with one another, and engage the audience directly, collapsing the boundary between observer and participant. The performance calls on the next revolution not through spectacle or aggression, but through play, embodiment, and collective presence. By animating these figures, the project emphasizes the power of queer imagination as a living, breathing force that insists on visibility, care, and radical connection as tools for survival and transformation.

The Tom of Finland Foundation's archives will provide me with direct design inspiration for my mythological creatures, acting as prompts and themes on which I can devise and explode variations into concepts. The nature of this piece is a playful way of building upon lineage to prepare for the future, and being able to devise in collaboration with the Foundation's archive would provide a direct engagement of intergenerational queer art and creative research.
I originally heard about this Residency through an artist-friend in Provincetown who was considering submitting an application.

While my main artistic practice at the moment is theatrical direction and design, the motor of my artistic life is in my visual artwork. In all of my theatrical work, I approach it from an aesthetically driven place, from the fine details to overall composition, as well as in how I apply direction and collaborate with actors through embracing chance, refining in layers, and rejecting legacy acting styles for more experimental approaches. In the past two years, I've been slowly shifting my practice to take less conventional theatrical work and slip more into exploring an interdisciplinary practice that embraces visual art and performance.

Nicko Cecchini is an acrylic painter based in Toronto. Educated in Fine Arts at the University of Ottawa, he currently works as a painting specialist for renowned Cree artist Kent Monkman and has displayed works internationally. His work explores notions of self commodification, brand identity and commercialism and its effects on queer culture. His interests also include sexuality, environmentalism, politics and pop culture

I’m applying to the Tom of Finland Foundation residency to develop a new series of paintings that uses the Foundation’s archive as both inspiration and critical ground. I want to explore self-commodification, the male gaze, queer identity, and surveillance; particularly how the male body circulates within queer culture today.

Tom’s work built a bold, unapologetic visual language for queer desire. His figures were exaggerated, coded, iconic; at once liberating and aspirational. I’m interested in what happens to those archetypes now, in an era where visibility is constant and desirability is measurable. During the residency, I plan to spend time studying recurring motifs in the archive such as uniforms, repetition of physique, stylized masculinity, to understand how empowerment and idealization became intertwined.

The paintings I develop will translate these archetypes into chrome figures: highly reflective, seductive, but hollow. The chrome surface will function as a metaphor for the self under late capitalism. These bodies won’t just reflect their surroundings; they’ll reflect us. They represent the way identity increasingly becomes performance; how we shape ourselves according to what we believe others want to see.

I’m especially interested in the commodification of the male figure in queer spaces. Apps, feeds, metrics, and algorithms have turned desirability into currency. Where Tom’s figures once asserted erotic visibility as freedom, my work asks what happens when that visibility becomes compulsory...when we internalize the gaze and begin optimizing ourselves for it.

In these paintings, the male gaze won’t disappear; it will be complicated. The figures may appear aware of being watched, suspended in poses that feel both powerful and exposed. The chrome surface becomes a kind of armour, but also evidence of erosion—a hollowing out of interiority as everything becomes surface.

The Tom of Finland Foundation provides a critical lens for my research into queer visual culture. Tom’s illustrations engineered a global aesthetic of desire, where hyperbolic, uniformed bodies operated as both liberation and ideal. During the residency, I will study the archive to examine how repetition and stylization produced empowerment while constructing a visual economy of masculinity. From this research, I will develop paintings that translate these archetypes into symbols of branding, data capture, and algorithmic desirability, asking what happens when visibility shifts from freedom to obligation.

I have applied to the artist in residence program numerous times now as I am a huge admirer of Tom's work and of the foundations mission. I believe my work blends seamlessly with his exploration of queer identity and form and my analysis of said identity in the age of social media, influencer culture and surveillance capitalism.

If given the opportunity to be the artist in residenc I would jump at the chance to continue Tom's artistic legacy through use of his archive and through his magnetic and incomparable artistic vision.

F. Tibiezas Dager (Guayaquil, 1997) is a writer and artist whose work critically explores the ideas of im/purity, violence, belonging, aspiration and assigned vulnerabilities in spaces of togetherness where people come to be. In her work the family, the couple, and the identitarian community get portrayed as defining stages where the sensual psychosis of this micropolitics shape the bodies the objects and the raptures of everyday life.

In her sculpture, lack and threat find a symbolic embodiment through the harsh manipulation of found objects. And in her portraiture where the intimate turns sinister, the drawing practice functions as an archive where subjectivity rewrites the self. Most of her visual work sets a dialogue with her writing which spans poetry, fiction and the literary essay. Her exhibitions tend to unfold as precarious playgrounds where materiality takes an emotional turn, political iconography gets to be revisited and the erotic overflows with possibilities.

Despite the reinsurgence in the media of sexual grooming and gender ideology influence panics during the Trump administrations (2016 – 2021), (2024- ) and the exhaustive debates around consent and power relations coming from feminist circles after #MeToo, intergenerational relationships continue to be key pillar aspects of queer intimacies. Being the younger trans partner of a mixed age straight passing relationship with a sixteen year gap, I find myself trapped in the middle of debates where the two major political forces operate withinn a securitist logic to argument against or in favor of my modes of existing in the public-private realm dichotomy. 
Interested in the visual representations and discourses around intergenerational lovers that can be found in the archival imagery and writing from 20 and 21st century gay press I want to trace the tones, sidings and general takes on this (not so) radical form of bonding. Understanding that any form of ethics sustains itself through an aestethtic lineage I want to find pictures, drawings, letters, communication between staff and audience where the benefits, seductive aspects and moral concerns of the young/older dynamics are submitted and illustrated by the open discussions that were documented by these alternative communities. 
Contrasting recent and older  “scandals” regarding these mixed aged couplings and encounters, findings at the Tom of Finland archive and conversations with a few of my older lovers on changing desire and desire for those who are changing (eg. Aging, gender transitioning, sex change ) I would come up with a moodboard that would function as investigation field from where I get the inspiration and affect based materials to start Half-bodied river crossing, a series of pocket sized drawings and site specific sculptures. 
These small drawings charged with queer semiotics and confessionals would ressemble love notes, the ones that with the intention to solicit contact, shyly get passed at bars or between classmates. Depending on the narrative content, some “love notes” would get collaged into bigger pieces, others may function as singular ones. The site specific sculptures which I would refer to as “stations”, would consist of asseambleages of found objects set to be intervened with the techniques (clay, casting, etc) that can be seen in my recent work. Conceptually these sculptures will sublimate the objects left after the imagined affair by turning them into a sort of anti monument to the heavily policed age and gender crossings of the intergenerational lovers.

Aware of the Foundation vast material regarding gay presses, bulletins and ephemeras which capture fragments of previous queer lifestyles. Ill like to mainly explore two aspects of these historical documents: The first one is language and its effects on how alternative communities and dissident experiences are recorded. The second is the vintage imagery and how these aesthetiziced narratives help build radical politics of the personal.

The way embodied desired and explicit sexuality are central for the production of projects in the residence has been of my consideration for the development of my own practice for some time. By being a follower of Ms. Carta Monir´s interdisciplinary deviations I was made aware that the Foundation was seeking to expand its community of queer creatives to artists of trans experience. Seeing how passionate she is about the existence and legacy of the Foundation, and how fun and formative her stay was, I would like to join the long list of image makers reshaping contemporary queer visual culture.

I don't have any special requirements since I'm fully abled body and I don't have any dietary restriction. For the production of the drawings and small sculptures a spacious room with exposure to natural lighting would be ideal.

Currently I'm developing and writing my first novel in english, "Winter break" (working title) where I work through similar themes that I would be exploring during my stay at the residency, which are intergenerational intimacies, sub/dom relationships, scandal, body worshiping and the erotization of vulnerability.

Hilton Dresden is an illustrator, writer, and actor. Their homoerotic, trans-centric drawings have been published in magazines like 'Out' and 'Paper,' with further illustrations included in brand partnerships like with Nicola Formichetti’s Nicopanda, debuting at London Fashion Week. As a writer, they're the author of the erotic thriller film 'The Poolboy,' and have profiled queer artists in magazines including ‘TIME,’ ‘The Hollywood Reporter,’ and ‘Interview’ - interview subjects have included everyone from Billie Eilish to Sasha Velour to Hari Nef - as well as written numerous think pieces about the intersection of awards shows and gender in publications like ‘THR.’ As an actor they've appeared in numerous short films, including writing and starring in ‘AC Unit,’ winner of the Frameline Completion Fund, screening in festivals across the globe, and a Vimeo Staff pick. Other film credits include the pornographic comedy 'Cucumber/Knife,' and the TV show 'Search Party.'

I am incredibly excited to be working on my first full-scale solo illustration show, after having had a handful of small galleries showing prints of my digital work. My new series is titled “Stills from the Oscar-Winning Erotic Thriller Crossdresser Scorned,” and is comprised of a series of large pen-on-paper drawings, some black-and-white, others filled in with colored pencil. As a film obsessive and horny bastard, my drawings reflect scenes from a fictional film detailing a wild cast of grotesque characters engaged in melodramas, mania, and mating. Translation: fornicating frenetically. Drawing from Cindy Sherman’s Untitled Film Stills, the illustrations of Brett Helquist and David Rappeneau, and the unflinching sexuality of Tom of Finland, “Stills From..” is a tribute to my insatiable lust, to my non-conforming gender expression, and to the ugliness and beauty I see in the world, in relationships, in other people, and in desire, both requited and unrequited. It draws on my love of fantasy, often incorporating magical and fairytale elements like mertwinks (which happen to be the subject of my erotic horror screenplay The Poolboy). This series also celebrates my improvisational technique, where in not deeply planning out my work, I discover each intricate detail as I go, and each “mistake” dictates what I draw next. An oversized hand can inform the bone structure of another character, which shapes the environment unfolding around each subject. This keeps the drawing process exciting and continuously full of discovery for me. A huge theme in my work — across drawing, writing, and acting — is desire, and the subsequent pain, euphoria, and misery that often come with it. As I am creating my drawings, I’m releasing the agony of my broken heart and titillating myself with humanoid forms intertwined carnally. Most of all, I’m creating worlds — worlds I might further explore (or have already begun to investigate) in screenplay or essay format, worlds with different rules than our own, where individuals can exist outside the cages and confines of gender binaries. My work is thus about freedom, from homonormative and heteronormative gazes, from the hyperstructured perspectives of society, from the rules of civilized sexuality and sociality. The fictional film Crossdresser Scorned follows ravenous, sexually insatiable gender-bending freaks through cityscapes and countryscapes alike. My work is fueled by a love of cinema and a fundamental desire to create magical, sexual universes of androgynous, deranged heroines exploding with grief and ecstasy.

As a longtime fan of Tom of Finland’s work, from which I draw a great deal of artistic inspiration, having access to his archives would be both a creative and personal miracle. I would study his drawings closely to understand his techniques and compositions, in order to inform my own work and to develop a deeper understanding of the history of homoerotic art and how both his and my work fit into a larger visual, political, and narrative context. I plan to spend time studying Tom’s work to inform both how I depict sex and depict scenarios in my illustrations.

I heard about the residency program because an acquaintance of mine, Robert Knox Hayes, posted about it on Instagram. When I checked the residency out, I saw friends of mine had done it, like Lukasz Leja and Brontez Purnell. I'm super excited at the chance to be a part of this program.

For me, the social component of this residency, the opportunity to make new friends and artistic connections, is just as exciting as the rest of it. I love to build community, and as a trans nonbinary person I feel that I would bring something special to the Tom of Finland home. I value kindness above all else!
I am interested in both the March-May and Sep-Nov (and maybe the Dec-Feb) sessions, and would be curious to know the exact dates and details in order to know the best fit.

I am a visual artist working primarily in photography across documentary and diaristic modes. My work examines gay male intimacy, community, and masculinity, from the aftermath of the AIDS crisis to contemporary sexual culture. I produced a body of work depicting the diminishing gay community in Laguna Beach, CA, and I am currently an MFA candidate in Photography at the Rochester Institute of Technology. My recent work depicts erotic encounters as sites of desire, projection, tenderness, and loss, emphasizing emotional experience over technical perfection.

During the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence, I plan to expand my ongoing photographic project exploring gay male sexual encounters as emotionally complex, temporally layered experiences. Rather than treating sex as a discrete event, the work situates intimacy as something that unfolds before, during, and after the experience.

My photographs focus on bodies in proximity, moments of touch, and gestures register vulnerability and desire. I often create soft or blurred images, resisting legibility in favor of emotional immediacy. This approach reflects how sexual encounters are experienced through the various emotional registers of erotic and intimate contact. Alongside images of bodies and encounters, the work includes photographs of spaces and objects that register anticipation, absence, and aftermath, allowing intimacy to extend beyond the presence of figures.

This project is informed by a lineage of gay photographers whose work has shaped my understanding of intimacy as quiet, psychological, and ever-changing. Their images suggest that desire can be tender and emotionally charged, even when it is explicitly sexual. I see my work as an extension of this lineage, responding from within my own lived experience and generation.

The residency’s setting offers a productive tension between this photographic intimacy and Tom of Finland’s legacy of hypermasculine erotic depictions. Tom’s work asserts clarity, confidence, and abundance: bodies without hesitation or doubt. Working within his home, I am interested in exploring what happens when that fantasy is filtered through lived sexual experience: moments where the eroticized masculine body performs, negotiates, or softens. My photographs aim to hold both pleasure and vulnerability at once, allowing fantasy and reality to coexist. This project seeks to honor Tom of Finland’s unapologetic erotic vision while offering a quieter, more intimate view of contemporary gay desire, where sexual encounters are shaped by longing and feelings that remain after the moment has passed.

Having previously lived in Los Angeles, I have access to an established community of collaborators, as well as the broader population of the city. I plan to use the house, its grounds, and the surrounding Los Angeles landscape as sites of intimate exchange. In addition to photography, I intend to experiment with incorporating text with this body of work.

I intend to use the Tom of Finland Foundation archives as both historical and conceptual research. I am particularly interested in Tom’s drawings and photographic references as models of how fantasy, masculinity, and erotic freedom operate visually. Through studying the archive, I hope to better understand how Tom and other artists construct desire and community through image-making, and to place my own work in dialogue with this lineage. The archive will inform both the conceptual framework and formal evolution of my project.
I've followed the foundation and program on social media for several years and have visited the house multiple times.

As a classically trained artist my formal art education included traditional techniques of Western art along with formal training in graphic design, photography, and animation. 

While my professional career was in Instructional Design, the analytical and implementation rigor of this profession ran parallel to and I believe complementary with the creative, visual, and conceptual skills of fine art.

As an erotic artist I explore the intersection of sex and sexuality of the human form to promote and validate gay, sex positive identity through a blend of expressive drawing and painting techniques utilizing primarily pen and ink, Prismacolor, graphite, and watercolor.

Southeast Asian/Thai Gay Sex and Sexuality –
 
“Beauty, when used skillfully, can open the mind to deeper truths.”

The continent of Asia represents approximately 60% of the world’s population which is defined as individuals whose origins include the Asian-Pacific regions (China, Japan, Korea, Mongolia, Taiwan), Southeast Asia (inland countries of Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Thailand and the maritime regions of Indonesia, the Philippines, and Singapore) and the Indian subcontinent (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal and Bhutan including the island nations of Sri Lanka and the Maldives).  

Grounded in my own interests and familiarity, I have chosen to concentrate on Southeast Asia, specifically Thailand as I have spent the better part of the past three years engaging with my partner to better understand the language, culture, traditions, aesthetics, and mindset of his native homeland. Within the context of my own experiences, I have also come to recognize that there exists a race-based attraction and/or fetishization towards gay Asian men, be it for sex or romance, that is oftentimes reduced to race and ethnicity. I have also come to notice that in Western Culture, this attraction and/or fetishization oftentimes accompanies both Western preconceptions and stereotypes as to what an “Asian” is. 

As an artist I have in the past portrayed gay erotic art of Asian men in neutral environments. For this project my aim will be to expand on this Asian representation in erotic art not through the traditional art principles, social norms, belief systems and values of Western culture, but more appropriately my objective will be to both examine the Southeast Asian/Thai cultural and artistic aesthetics, mindset and spiritual teachings and from within this framework create a blend of nature, architecture, ritual, tradition, spirituality, and symbolism whose outcome will not simply be illustrative, it will be instructive. It will teach through beauty. It will convey the vibrant reality of form that is designed to attract and make the sacred both accessible and desirable. It will explore gay eroticism, sex and sexuality with the goal of, promoting dialog, identifying, validating, and empowering a more representative gay, sex positive identity of this culture group.

With the recent legalization of same-sex marriage in Thailand and the subsequent dialog this fostered among the LGBTQ+ communities and families, it is my hope that with access to the archives at the foundation I might better examine and understand the gay/same-sex visual aesthetics and philosophies that define the world of Tom of Finland and juxtapose these with traditional Asian perspective of sex and sexuality, culture, and spiritual norms, with the goal of identifying, promoting and validating a gay sex positive identity.

Through my participation in figure drawing sessions, volunteer opportunities at various Tom House Art & Culture Fairs, and by engaging the erotic art community through sponsored exhibitions by the foundation, I learned of the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residency program.

responses.answer: I work at the Rose Island Lighthouse here in Rhode Island and our busy season is late Spring throughout early Fall so ideally I would love to be able to still work the full season and participate in this iconic residency at the end of the year, BUTT I would totally prioritize attending this residency so if my only chance is in the Spring or Summer I'll take it. Thanks :)

I've been a long time admirer of Tom, and I've met people while exhibiting at the LA Art Book Fair and New York Queer Zine Fair who told me I should apply; so I applied. Since then I've been honing in on my art practice, looking forward to showing y'all that I am still super interested in contributing to this erotic legacy. My friend Michael Espinoza, a 2024 ToF Resident, and I discussed the residency too and he told me that I am definitely worthy of being given this opportunity which felt really sweet.

Chris Moody (b. 1992 Little Rock, Arkansas) grew up deep in the heart of Texas where nature and art were his saving escapes and nurtures. The overwhelming beauty and queerness of nature still prevails throughout Moody's artwork as he is endlessly inspired and thankful for its wonders. Primarily creating visions through the lenses of film & digital cameras Moody has also been publishing DIY zines for over 10 years formerly under the name 'horse gurl press'. Moody knows no bounds for creating and sharing love furthermore, and is also an alternative artsy campy drag queen named Candy Whoreholla.

For the overall bigger picture I will be making a zine about the Tom of Finland house. This zine will be multifaceted and the pages within will contain a whole series of several other projects including: Photographing the queer men of LA and surrounding areas on Polaroid & Digital, Documenting life at the house, Collaging with Tom's work throughout the archives, Journaling about the gay/queer connections throughout the house and artists who have been there, and a further exploration of self in the freedom of such an accepting, creative, and open home. I think the legacy and spirit of this special place will harbor a lot of inspiration and collaborations which will create some powerful, sexy, and innovative artworks. In addition to these all being within a zine, most will also bode well as framed/physical pieces on their own too. Of course, I also gladly welcome the unexpected mysteries and magic that always happen in life and cannot wait to create and share the love with y'all! <3

Research! Research! Research! I am a deep lover of archives and would be overcum with joy spending hours reliving Tom's works. I would love to use these artifacts for inspiration as well as incorporating them into the photographs and in collages. I can see how overlaying humans with his drawings in the same photograph would create some beautiful magic through connections of the past, present, and future. I am a true sucker for an archive like his and I would love nothing more than spending hours digging through history feeling its magic wash over me. Yes, please!

I am a self-taught contemporary artist based in Los Angeles whose figurative expressionist paintings interrogate identity, desire, and historical erasure. After a transformative 24-year career as a celebrity hairstylist and Global Ambassador for Procter & Gamble’s Herbal Essences, working with clients including Mariah Carey and Ariana Grande, I began painting at age 42.
My current “Out of Time” series explores gender-subversive and marginalized historical figures from the 17th and 18th centuries, drawing parallels between historical identity performance in portraiture and contemporary digital self-curation. Through extensive archival research and accessible figurative painting, I create representation for LGBTQ+ community members while educating broader audiences about queer histories that have been systematically erased. My practice centers beauty as a strategic tool for engaging viewers with marginalized narratives that demand recognition. I believe art can serve as both mirror and window—reflecting our community back to itself while inviting others into deeper understanding.

“Out of Time” Series Description
I’m a painter working in 17th and 18th century portraiture traditions—using the visual language of court painters like Jean-Marc Nattier who created the Instagram filters of their time. My “Out of Time” series uses this classical framework to pull forgotten gender-subversive figures into contemporary visibility through witty, ironic paintings that speak across centuries.
Most people have never heard of the Chevalier d’Éon, Princess Seraphina, Philippe I Duke of Orléans, Lord Hervey, or the Macaronis. That’s the work—bringing these stories forward so LGBTQ+ youth know we have always been here. D’Éon was a French diplomat who spent 33 years legally declared female after blackmail and exile. Princess Seraphina moved through 1732 London in white gown and scarlet cloak, prosecuting someone at the Old Bailey despite sodomy being a capital offense. Philippe I wore gowns and cosmetics to state functions while commanding armies. These people faced real danger yet lived as themselves anyway.
What fascinates me is the artifice of dressing as identity construction. In aristocratic society, when all wealthy men wore powdered wigs, silk, and cosmetics, performing identity was mandatory. This created unexpected space for gender fluidity—when masculinity itself requires elaborate staging, boundaries become porous. We do the same thing now with filters and curated feeds. The tools changed; the human impulse didn’t.
This connects to why I’m applying to Tom of Finland Foundation. Tom understood that dress functions as both armor and code. His hypermasculine figures in leather and uniforms weren’t just erotic—they created visual language for community recognition when being found out could destroy you. Where 18th-century aristocrats used courtly dress to perform subversion, Tom transformed authority symbols into instruments of pride and belonging. Both understood: we find each other through shared codes.

I’m interested in exploring ways to layer the figurative elements of his work, the dressing and presentation into this type of portraiture to challenge the viewer, to articulate the coded elements and to elevate the conversation in a way to brings all of this forward into a contemporary art experience.
Instagram and I’ve known of Tom of Finland for years. I’ve been out for 33 years.

I’m actually mostly looking for the platform and exposure this experience would give me. As I am already, LA based, I don’t need to be relocated, I have a home as well as a studio in my home.. access to the archives and the foundation itself as well as the exposure this would provide me to create an interesting body of work is really all I am hoping for.

While my current work employees classical portraiture elements, I have a body of work that speaks to queer culture, and is primarily about identity. I love the way painted surfaces that sometimes articulate or obscure that identity, the tension between the abstract and the literal, as well as an understanding of the human anatomy, and the way that emotion/identity can be expressed to the way that we carry and present ourselves. This is something that Tom of Finland’s work did beautifully and I would be honored to have an opportunity to work with the foundation

“Whatever Blows Your Wig Back” (2025), Oil on canvas 30 x 40 inches 
This painting establishes the visual and conceptual language of the “Out of Time” series by drawing directly from Jean-Marc Nattier (1685–1766), court portraitist to Louis XV. I’ve appropriated his signature elements—white dress, blue satin drapery, powdered hair, rosy cheeks—to create what appears to be a traditional 18th-century aristocratic portrait. But look closer.
The towering pouf hairstyle reveals its construction: these elaborate structures were built using “hair rats”—padding made from hair saved in ceramic pots and stuffed into stockings. Kept for weeks, coated in rancid animal fat pomade, they literally attracted vermin (the origin of “rat’s nest”). The lace-front wig “blowing back” exposes what was always hidden: the artifice beneath impossible beauty standards.
A Banksy-inspired rat wielding a blow dryer operates as triple reference—street art’s subversive tradition, the actual vermin infesting these hairstyles, and the hair rats themselves. Abstract graffiti (“the writing on the wall”) collapses 250 years, suggesting that identity performance mechanisms haven’t changed, only the tools. Nattier’s portraits were the Instagram filters of 1740.
The painting works generationally: classical technique offers entry for traditional audiences; contemporary wit speaks to younger viewers. Beauty becomes Trojan horse—it insists on looking, then reveals the constructed nature of what we’re seeing. The title itself is a contemporary idiom applied to historical vanity, inviting humor while prompting recognition: we’re still doing this, just with different

Building A Sin, Oil on canvas 36 x 48 inches 
Artist’s Collection
“Building A Sin” examines the psychic cost of self-realization within religious and social constraint. Strahan constructs a fractured urban-psychological landscape where multiple male figures strain against literal and metaphorical bondage—external doctrine internalized as restraint.
Erotic imagery serves as clarifying tension: the human form confronts abstracted narratives designed to confine it. Natural elements—rock, water, earth—function as emotional truth, wearing down imposed structures through persistence.
The painting reclaims “sin” as an act of self-construction rather than self-betrayal: a queer body asserting agency while navigating the architectures that would prefer it remain divided.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Hooked on Dopamine 24 x 36 inches 
From the series Beyond the Flesh
Charles Baker Strahan
Hooked on Dopamine examines the social media “thirst trap” through deliberate gaps in representational flesh over abstract underpainting. By exposing the artifice of the medium, Strahan challenges the engineered images designed to trigger dopamine responses, revealing how digital sexualization often prioritizes engagement over authentic identity. The calculated pose suggests willing participation in the attention economy, inviting viewers to project their desires onto a curated construction that remains fundamentally separate from the human reality behind the screen.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Genevieve Kuzak’s work explores, by way of erotic offerings, the capacity of the category of “butchness.” He offers, through vivid and tactile videography and photography, windows into butch sexuality, delicate butch feelings, and the generative slippage that occurs between the butch, the butch boy, the twink boy, the t-boy, and more. 
Using imagery from the realm of internet porn and Tumblr aesthetics, he not only offers preview into the private lives of butches who fuck each other, but also brings together a complex cast of characters to depict queer liberation in its most intimate settings. Kuzak’s lens challenges us to explore, affirm, and comprehend the bridge between FTM, faggot, and dyke sexuality. The heavy, personal aspects of Kuzak’s work often make viewing a more private experience; his films are best shown on large screens in small theaters and on the laptops of curious viewers.

I want to make a feature length film titled ‘I Am Not Afraid’ that reflects the current moment’s dominant cultural narratives of Fear and Power. Fascism is inescapable for most of the populace, even those with the privilege to safely navigate day to day life. During times of stress and economic anxiety the culture wars flourish and people hate and fear one another; their minds polluted by narratives meant to alienate and terrorize. How can we move through this moment when we feel powerless? Does power have limits and how can we transcend these limits? Can sexual intimacy confront the rules of power in a lasting and impactful way?

My film ‘I Am Not Afraid’ will approach these questions through the medium of performance and video. The context of power framed via the intercommunity dynamics of gay man and dyke, cis man and FTM, as well as sex worker and client. With my background in creating art-porn, I will facilitate a safe environment to explore the full complexity of sex and desire. 

Separated into three main scenes, each begins with a frank discussion where I interview the subjects, the subjects being different gay cis men, about their most personal sexual fantasies and negotiate how I’ll help facilitate it for them. Afterwards, we will engage in these sexual acts and/or BDSM onscreen. The shots will be interlaced with different elements, such as vintage pornography from the archive, in order to place the encounters within living history. Then the scene ends with a role reversal wherein the subject assumes the role of the vulnerable, having just revealed to me their hidden Shadow Self. In doing so, the sexual interaction that occurs is transformed into a social, spiritual and artistic practice; one that exposes the complex networks of Fear and Power inherent to our closest encounters with one another.

This program will afford me the time, freedom and platform to materialize this project during a moment where censorship is the norm and being a trans person making erotic art is extremely complicated and difficult. I have spent four years working as an artist in NYC, shown my films internationally and am ready to create my most ambitious project yet in a space where I will not only be accepted but also supported.

An integral part of my art practice involves looking at BDSM and queer sex imagery for inspiration and research. I would love the opportunity to study the archive and learn about the history of deviant sexuality, doing so will help me develop the ways I use sex as a practice and position it within a broader cultural context. To be more specific, I identify as a dyke and have been exposed to mostly lesbian-adjacent archival material. I’m eager to use gay male archives to inform how I might play that role in my proposed film project.

I have heard about the program because a few friends, Carta Monir and Sweatmother, have participated in the residency and posted about their involvement with the foundation on social media. Another friend, Alannah Farrell encouraged me to apply because he has worked with the foundation and suggested that I would be a good fit.
Not that I can think of!

I’ve been working in the sex industry in different capacities and making art seriously for over a decade now, and finding an arts organization where I can equally express these facets of myself has been a challenge. Generally speaking, it’s been easier to work in Europe as opposed to the USA, so coming across this residency in LA was very exciting for me since it's one of few opportunities available in the United States for a marginalized artist like myself making sexually explicit work. 

Note: This is the second time I've submitted my application. I was given permission to re-submit.

I am an artist, writer, and educator living and working in Cincinnati, OH. I received my BFA from the University of Nebraska-Lincoln and MFA from the University of Connecticut. I am currently an Assistant Professor of Fine Art at the University of Cincinnati, where I manage the BFA Foundations program, printmaking area, and teach both undergraduate and graduate courses. My research utilizes archives as a starting point to explore what it means to be haunted.

Finding this residency program excited me about the possibilities of this work that I have been keeping hidden until recently. I am living and working in a state that has recently created massive restrictions to the rights of its higher education staff, faculty, and students. Put frankly, I am terrified of being a queer educator in Ohio right now, but I have decided to put aside those fears in order to pursue the research and work I am most passionate about right now. Queer joy, pleasure, and sex are subversive in a time where increased conservative pressure is attempting to force us back into the closet.

I am currently creating a bestiary of creatures that express both queer desire and monstrosity. Combining imagery from medieval manuscripts and alchemical texts with the bodies of queer men, these monsters reflect my own body type; one that is counter to popular standards of beauty. These fat, hairy, trans bodies mesh with the uncanny depictions of creatures, both fictional and real, creating chimeras that contain both the erotic and the abject. As queerness is becoming more demonized in greater culture, I offer a counter-narrative to assimilationist rhetoric that instead embraces monstrosity as a form of liberation.

These works range in process and media, utilizing collage as a primary form of methodology. Much of the research so far has taken place online utilizing a wealth of open-access archives and scans of various bestiaries and alchemical texts. While in residence at the Tom of Finland Foundation, I intend to not only utilize the archives in-house for research, but also expand into the greater Los Angeles area to research artists and objects I do not have access to. This may include the Getty, USC, and UCLA, among others yet to be identified. It is my intention to make the most of my time at the Tom of Finland Foundation to expand my research as much as I can.

The objects I plan to make include continued exploration of paper-based drawings and paintings, as well as oil painting, collage, and digitally-based works. As an educator, I am interested and eager to include programming ranging from workshops to lectures and other events inviting the local community to collaborate. I have become a bit of a hermit since moving to Ohio in 2022 and kickstarting my academic career, and as such I intend to make the absolute most out of this opportunity by putting myself out there.

Archival research has recently become a staple in my process. I am most interested in connecting with materials that relate to process, iteration, and idea generating surrounding queer erotic art, not just final products. Included in my examples are works that purposefully retain the perforated edges of the sketchbook they were created in as a means to reference their creation process. I’m interested in researching other elements of process and ideation that are currently missing in these works. I find the tension between final product and work-in-progress both fascinating and fruitful for conceptual development.
Performance, sound, video, installation, sculpture, writing

Robbie Trocchia is an artist working in performance, sound, video, installation, sculpture, and writing. His projects engage with themes of embodiment, desire, power, and intimacy, often through collaborative processes. He has presented work in New York, Los Angeles, Miami, and San Francisco. In 2025, Robbie was a Visual Art Fellow at Vermont Studio Center. He has also participated in residencies at Catwalk Art Institute (Catskill), Locust Projects (Miami), Espacio Lavadero (Granada), and Espai Souvenir (Barcelona). He received a BA in American Studies from Vassar College in 2014 and an MFA in Digital Arts and New Media from University of California, Santa Cruz in 2024 where his thesis project “eroto-system” was awarded the Coha-Gunderson Prize in Speculative Futures by The Humanities Institute. In fall 2025, Trocchia and collaborator Nicki Duval presented Death Spiral as part of “X: Gender, Identity, Presence” at Colgate University’s Picker Art Gallery and bout at INVERSE Performance Art Festival at The Momentary in Bentonville, Arkansas.

PUBLIC LEWDNESS is a series of mixed-media sculptural pieces using paperwork I have received after being arrested for a sexual encounter at Gunnison Beach on Sandy Hook, NJ last May. During a residency at Catwalk Institute in June, I began printing a scan of my citation on transparency film and mounting it to sheet glass with Mod Podge. I experimented with displaying these glass pieces in a pile of sand—a cheeky nod to Robert Smithson. I also overlaid these surfaces with imagery drawn in Mod Podge and sand, transforming punitive state documentation into sites of erotic reclamation. Around the time of my arrest, Amtrak police (a federal police force similar to the federally-funded park rangers who arrested me at Sandy Hook, a national park) began cracking down on public sex in NY Penn Station restrooms, using the map-based hookup app Sniffies as a tool of surveillance. While this project has offered me an opportunity to abstract and degrade these legal documents, it also feels urgent in the wake of this police crackdown in NYC. After my court appearance in September, I continued working with documentation from my probation period (which will end in early May). As this residency period at Tom of Finland Foundation will potentially coincide with the final weeks of my probation period, it will offer me the first opportunity to share this work publicly after my probation ends. I intend to use my time there to make additional works in this series that are in conversation with the Foundation’s archives, specifically material that relates to the policing of cruising spaces. While I have continued using this method of mounting transparency film onto glass, I plan on experimenting with different materials and techniques including image transfer methods using gel medium, acetate, and heat transfer. As the terms of my probation dictate that I cannot be present at Sandy Hook for the remaining period of my probation, I am interested in using a proxy, likely a close friend, who can travel to Gunnison Beach to collect objects like driftwood and other natural material, debris that has washed ashore, and litter including glass. I’m interested in using these objects in further mixed media pieces and as base materials for image transfer experiments. I will use my time at Tom of Finland Foundation as a chance to comb through the archives, looking for other examples of resistance to state surveillance while continuing my research into current manifestations of fascism.

I'm specifically interested in the archives at Tom of Finland Foundation as they contain myriad examples of the knotty intersection between queerness and criminality. Since my probation period began, I’ve thought of this project as a process of surveilling my experience of being surveilled by the state (through background checks and monthly check-ins with my probation officer). I'm interested in seeing how Tom and other artists have responded to criminalization of both gay sex (specifically public cruising) and gay culture writ large. I’m curious to see what strategies other artists have used to respond to this repression.
I know people who have participated in the residency program and I also lived for a few months in Echo Park, quite close to the Foundation.

My proposed project continues my ongoing series If You Know, You Know, an intimate exploration of queer desire, connection, and identity through photography, analog processes, and experimental printing techniques. The series captures fleeting, often private moments of intimacy—raw yet tender, personal yet universal—creating a visual language that celebrates queerness while questioning societal norms around the body and erotic expression.

At the residency, I hope to deepen this work by situating it within the rich historical lineage of queer visual culture. Engaging with the legacy of artists like Tom of Finland will allow me to expand the series both conceptually and materially. I envision integrating video, collage, and layered print techniques, experimenting with how historical references and contemporary perspectives can converse within a single frame. This process will enable me to develop If You Know, You Know further—creating work that honors queer histories while exploring the fluidity, vulnerability, and complexity of desire in today’s context.

The Tom of Finland archives pulse with a fearless queer erotic history. Immersed in drawings, letters, and photos, I want to let this legacy collide with my own practice—through photography, analog printing, collage, and video—to expand If You Know, You Know. My goal is to fuse past and present, creating work that is intimate, daring, and unapologetic, a visual celebration of desire, vulnerability, and queer identity in all its forms.

Rik Versteeg (b. 1990, Drunen, Noord-Brabant) is a queer visual artist whose work explores identity, desire, human rights, and our interconnectedness with nature through a distinctly queer lens. Art has been both sanctuary and empowerment, allowing him to navigate personal and collective experiences while creating transformative spaces between reality and surrealism.

His practice spans photography, film, makeup, and (costume) design, redefining traditional boundaries and crafting layered, immersive narratives. Through vivid colors, intricate concepts, and layered metaphors, Rik challenges societal norms, deconstructs patriarchy and heteronormative perspectives, and fosters dialogue around authenticity, intimacy, and belonging.

Rik’s work celebrates queer identity while inviting audiences to explore their own stories, perspectives, and connections. Exhibited widely, including solo shows The Masquerade Ball (2019) and Sensitive Obscurities (2020), and through his photobook Mind Mirage (2024), his art is both an invitation to empathize and a call to embrace authenticity, equality, and community in all its forms.
