responses.answer: mixed materials, participation, civic process, or other artists and fabricators. The body of work includes photographic portraits, experimental documentary videos, public art installations, and exhibition projects.

Much of my work as an artist is created collaboratively – whether that be in collaboration with the public participation, civic process, or other artists and fabricators. The body of work includes photographic portraits, experimental documentary videos, public art installations, and exhibition projects. Using public spaces and public archives as a method of interrogating the familiar, the art works investigates urban space, ideas of neighborhood, and new forms of citizenship. From protests to proposals for new urban green space, this work aims to reconfigure the relationship of sharing and explore understandings of public and private. Much of this work is linked to ideas of place and family, and much of these works echo a sense of connectedness with something very primal – our capacity to share the world with others. I believe that contemporary art is a common denominator to change the way we all experience the world.

The final looks and format of the project will be determined by the image and content contributed to the body of work. These images, texts, memorabilia, recipes, ephemera, etc. will be organized into a digital archive volume, however these contributions will not be 'edited' or 'redesigned' by the person/s organizing this project. The original voices and visual vocabularies will be retained in the body of work / archive. It is important for this project that the integrity of the submitted and sourced historical materials are retained with integrity, authenticity, and respect.

FFriends and FFamily: An Invitational Yearbook Residency Project is a joyful, research-driven, and community-centered cultural publishing initiative conceived as a contemporary queer annual—somewhere between Better Homes and Gardens, Research Magazine, and the visual language of a 1970s–1990s high school yearbook. The project brings together oral history, portraiture, recipes, artworks, and interviews into a bound volume that celebrates intergenerational knowledge, intimacy, creativity, and chosen family within FF culture. It is designed not as a mass-market product, but as a limited-edition cultural artifact that honors lived experience as a form of expertise and treats erotic, domestic, and communal knowledge as heritage worth preserving.

The publication project will be structured as an invitational, with contributors co-creating interviews, stories, practical knowledge, and how-to guides—ranging from recipes and rituals of care to community lore such as how to make lube or poppers. Additional sections will include profiles of intergenerational leaders; short essays on history and mythology; regional features on FF communities; and profiles on clubs and play spaces. Visually, the book will combine documentary photography, studio portraiture, archival ephemera, selected artworks, historical material, creating a layered time capsule that feels rigorous, intimate and celebratory.

As part of the residency, one of the Tom of Finland Foundation’s spaces could be used as a temporary photo, video, and interviewing studio. This temporary dedicated production environment will support trust-based storytelling, focused research, and collaborative exchange, allowing contributors to gather in a setting that already carries deep cultural resonance. During the residency, we will conduct oral history interviews, produce portrait sessions, and video conversations that will feed both the book and a parallel documentary archive. These materials will also form the foundation for future exhibitions, screenings, and educational programming aligned with the Foundation’s mission.

The goal is to publish the physical book and digital editions by end of 2026 supported by a grass roots fundraising campaign, positioning the project as community empowered. The publication first life is intended to be FF community specific and values-aligned. The aesthetic will balance archival care with humor, sensuality, and visual delight—designed as something people want to contribute to, revisit, and pass forward. 

Optimistic, inclusive, and deeply respectful, FFriends and FFamily positions the Tom of Finland Foundation not only as host and archive—ensuring that living queer knowledge, intergenerational intimacy, and community memory are recorded, honored, and carried into the future.

The approach to the Tom of Finland Foundation archives is to work directly with selected materials—drawings, photographs, letters, and ephemera—not only as objects but as voices in an ongoing historical conversation. The archives can inform interview prompts, portrait sessions, and editorial structure for the FFriends and FFamily project, helping situate contemporary stories within a longer lineage of historical materials. Rather than reproducing archival content, the intent to let its tone, humor, intimacy, and courage shape prompts, allowing the past to illuminate the present while remaining intact and honored.

The Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program is well known within the FF community. The initial research for this project began organically from attending FF events nationally and regionally, as well as private gatherings hosted by notable hosts and event organizers. Through these experiences, I began documenting layered narratives through photographs, videos, and informal conversations. It quickly became clear that this material represented an important and meaningful body of work that deserved deeper care. Ongoing conversations with community members naturally led to the suggestion of the Tom of Finland Foundation supporting this work.

I've been active in the FF community for many years and have strong connections with event organizers and a broad cross-section of FFplayers in the community across different parts of the United States. I feel it's important to reach out to communities in the Pacific Northwest and the Northeast, Florida, and the Midwest, the plain states, Texas, the Southwest – across the United States. I feel confident in already having the network to support this project's scope of work. 
 
I think it's important to focus on intergenerational knowledge. Connecting the voice of older generations (pre-hiv&AIDS) to the younger guys (post AIDS/PREP). It's probably the biggest thing that I've noticed in my life being part of Generation X is the life experience and knowledge gap about community in this category. 
 
I was a teenager in the era of AIDS growing up in Los Angeles. My first introduction to Dirk and Tom of Finland Foundation was back in the early 90s with Daniel Babcock (Danny being a friend of mine from CalArts). And growing up in Los Angeles, of course, Tom of Finland Foundation is extraordinarily important, not just in our local community, but representing culture at large. 
 
It would be an honor to have the support of the Tom of Finland Foundation to work on this. invitational collaborative project. I am excited about putting this together with the help and guidance of a lot of different great guys who have important stories and memories to share. I think of this as a communal project and the project scope as an all-inclusive, meaning that everyone who is interested will be included in the work. And that would be collecting all of the recordings and photographs and notes and ephemera and flyers and posters and margin notes and tributes and all of these other beautiful things that make strange FF guys wonderful. And I hope that you can understand the sincerity in which I approach this opportunity.
 
Thank you for your consideration. 
David Allen Burns.

During the residency, I propose to create a series of sculptural counter-archives investigating the material culture of lesbian and trans sexuality, specifically focusing on the strap-on, harness, and other prosthetic intimacy technologies as archival objects. This project responds to the systematic exclusion of explicit queer sexual materials from institutional preservation, what I call the "double erasure" where trans and lesbian pornography faces both legal suppression and archival neglect.
I will produce bound structures, vitrines, and display frameworks that treat these intimate materials with the same preservation protocols typically reserved for "legitimate" artifacts. Using leatherworking, bookbinding, and architectural containment strategies, I will create objects that simultaneously preserve and obscure, display and protect. These pieces will incorporate sourced materials from queer erotic publications, documentation of prosthetic objects, and my own photographic and sculptural investigations of material transmission.
The conceptual framework draws from censorship history, particularly how the Hays Code forced queer desire into coded visual language while creating systems of what could and could not be preserved. I am interested in how prosthetic objects operate within this history of substitution and erasure. The strap-on functions as both literal tool and conceptual stand-in, evidence of trans and lesbian existence that institutions have deemed too obscene for collection.
The Tom of Finland Foundation's archives are essential to this research. Unlike mainstream institutions, the Foundation has protected the explicit, embodied materials I am investigating. I plan to study the erotic art collection not just as imagery but as material documentation, examining how harnesses, straps, and leather gear are depicted, constructed, and used. What design histories exist in these objects? How were they made, worn, shared?
My sculptural methodology mirrors archival vulnerability through processes embracing impermanence and material transformation. Some pieces will function as unopenable books, bound volumes suggesting both the desire to preserve and the impossibility of complete recovery. Others will use vitrine-style framing to position prosthetic fragments as museum specimens, challenging institutional boundaries around appropriate display.
This project insists that trans and lesbian sexual materials deserve preservation, study, and artistic attention. By creating sculptural counter-archives, I argue for the material specificity of queer intimacy as historical evidence worth protecting and honoring.

I will study the Foundation's erotic art collection as material documentation, examining how harnesses, strap-ons, and leather gear are depicted. I will treat these not as representations but as design blueprints and historical records of how prosthetic objects were constructed and used. I am particularly interested in ephemera and photographs showing actual objects. This research will directly inform my sculptural work, helping me understand the material evolution of these intimacy tools. The Foundation's preservation of explicit materials makes this investigation possible in ways mainstream archives cannot support.

I learned about the residency through Instagram, where I follow queer artists and arts organizations, and through a group chat with my graduate school cohort. We regularly share queer artist opportunities and resources with each other, and this network has been invaluable for discovering programs centering LGBTQ+ perspectives.

I would be grateful for opportunities to speak with the Foundation's archivists and preservation staff about their methodologies for collecting and caring for explicit queer materials. Understanding the practical and ethical considerations of preserving content that mainstream institutions reject would be invaluable to my research. I am also interested in connecting with activists and archivists working in queer preservation, particularly those focused on trans and lesbian materials. I see this residency as an opportunity to learn from the community actively doing this essential preservation work.

August Puplava (they/them) is a multimedia artist working in photography, print media, and sculptural installation. Born in Arizona and educated at the University of Hawaiʻi Mānoa (BFA Print Media, BS Fashion Design, minor in Museum Studies), they are currently completing an MFA in Print Media and Photography at Boston University. Their practice explores queer and trans embodiment through material experimentation, combining industrial elements like metal and wood with soft, tactile materials. Using fragmentation, layering, and chemical interventions, Puplava interrogates how bodies are seen, categorized, and preserved. Their work has been exhibited at Leica Gallery Boston, Griffin Museum of Photography, and in partnership with the Boston Public Art Triennial. With experience in archival digitization and collections management, they investigate identity as process rather than fixed image, creating work that resists singular readings and invites extended engagement.
My primary medium is oil painting on canvas, and I am currently expanding my practice into sculpture and relief.

Sonja Rychkova (b. 1998) is a Frankfurt-based painter. On canvas, she depicts young men from her community, from the housing project where she grew up, in images that speak of the attitude to life of her generation. Faces disappear; the figure is reduced to male body language, clothing, accessories, and the gestures of hip-hop and skate culture. She studies movement and the draping of clothes, exaggerating bodies erotically and manneristically until they feel sacred. Her paintings fuse art-historical references with the visual grammar of film and dance choreography. Family influence matters: her grandfather’s socialist-realist monuments; her grandmother’s Orthodox icons; and her skater father, who introduced her to Tom of Finland and gave her a TOFF sculpture at twelve years old. She graduated from HfG Offenbach (Blum/Bouchet) in May 2025 and received various grants and prices. Exhibitions examples: Robert Grunenberg (Berlin),  Ruttkowski68; Frankfurter Kunstverein museum, Art Basel Miami 2025.

My visual language comes from growing up in a housing project and finding belonging with my friends and in the different cultures each of us carries within us. I treat painting as a documentation of my generation - capturing posture, gesture, distance, touch, and the codes people use to signal safety, desire, loyalty, and defiance. I want these moments to read as specific and immediate, but also timeless. Something the future can recognize as lived truth.
Formally, I will continue my “Lovers” series (begun in 2023, expanded in Paris, first shown in Berlin at Robert Grunenberg Gallery, now part of the Miettinen Collection). The core focus is love as pride, survival, and freedom: how intimacy holds people together under pressure, and how tenderness becomes a form of resistance. In parallel, I am currently beginning my first sculpture/relief works, and I want to keep developing them in Los Angeles alongside my large-scale oil paintings. Homoerotic sculpture still feels underrepresented, and I want to add to that visual history with forms that carry weight and presence. The residency will culminate in a cohesive new body of work shaped by the site, the people I encounter, and the special environment the TOM House provides. I would also love to present the work I develop in an exhibition at the end of the residency.
To give a visual sense of the work I want to continue in Los Angeles, I have attached sketches, paintings from the “Lovers” series.

I expect it to be an intensely inspiring place. I feel that working with the archives at the TOM House will hit me with full force, and I need that impact. Every artist waits for moments like this: to be completely taken over by something, to process it, to feel it, and then to create from it. What I love about the house is living alongside the custodians of his legacy - those carrying his story forward through careful archiving and preservation. I’m excited to meet them and learn from their experience. Tom of Finland has been my favorite artist since I was a teenager, shaping how I observe body language.

Richard Villani encouraged me to apply to the residency program, and around the same time I saw the announcement on his Instagram as well as on the Tom of Finland Foundation’s profile. Tom of Finland and Los Angeles bring two of my biggest dreams into alignment at once.

Some moments feel like they come full circle. When I was 13, my dad gave me a Tom of Finland sculpture. Back then I could never have imagined I would become a professional artist and now apply for a residency in Los Angeles at the TOM House. I completed my Fine Arts studies at HfG Offenbach and graduated in May 2025 with a 1.0 diploma, studying under Mike Bouchet (a protégé of Paul McCarthy) and Heiner Blum. Since graduating, I’ve been seeking a Los Angeles residency because I believe I will learn immensely from the city. A three-month stay at TOM House would shape my post-graduate path. I want not only to take from the program, but also to give back - honoring Tom’s legacy through my work. I am available for any of the proposed periods, including the earliest March slot. Esta is active, and I hold a German passport. Thank you for your consideration. Sonja Rychkova

Based in Atlanta, I am a Seoul-born Korean American artist working primarily in drawing and painting. My practice centers on explicit depictions of male sexuality. Pornographic imagery often initiates my work.

I am interested in what happens when porn is translated into drawing or painting. While its original function is masturbatory, that desire becomes a starting point rather than an endpoint. Through the act of making, choices such as partial erasure or blackening reshape the body, breaking its photographic immediacy while allowing its sexual charge to remain.

This tension between arousal and transformation is central to my work. By working with gay porn, I examine how it begins to register differently once it passes through my hand, testing how explicit sexuality can exist as art in a contemporary visual context.

My proposed project centers on direct, in-person engagement with the Tom of Finland Foundation archives as a way to expand and adapt my existing process of erasure, blackening, and reworking pornographic imagery. I am particularly interested in closely observing Tom’s compositions and developing a body of work that takes his imagery as a conceptual point of departure before moving away from it through my own process.

Through selective erasure, enlargement, fragmentation, and recomposition, I aim to develop a new body of work that responds to his work. This approach allows me to test how explicit imagery changes when parts of an image are withheld, obscured, or overworked, disrupting its original clarity while allowing sexual charge to persist.

Working within the Tom of Finland House, where explicit sexual imagery is openly held and historically grounded, offers a rare environment to pursue this inquiry without restraint. Being physically present with the archive enables a form of looking and responding that cannot be replicated remotely.

Being in Los Angeles also offers visual and cultural inspiration, much as California’s bodybuilding culture influenced Tom’s work in the 1950s. Having formed my gay adulthood in NYC, I am curious how LA’s distinct sexual atmosphere might inflect the images I produce during this residency.

My project requires direct access to the Tom of Finland Foundation archives as a point of departure. Studying Tom's drawings and materials in person will reveal his working process, revisions, and decision-making through close physical observation. This close engagement will ground my new work in the archive itself, informing how I begin and develop work during the residency.

My first in-depth encounter with Tom of Finland’s drawings was at the 2015 exhibition 'The Pleasure of Play' at Artists Space, which reframed homoerotic imagery for me as something that could exist fully within a fine art context. Since that experience, I have followed Tom’s publications closely and watched the film Tom of Finland, deepening my understanding of his work and legacy. More recently, a Los Angeles–based artist friend introduced me to the Tom of Finland Foundation Artist-in-Residence program and encouraged me to apply.

At the core of my artistic practice is a desire to preserve the fleeting nature of queer identity. Through photography, printmaking, and bookmaking, I give permanence to narratives that might otherwise disappear. Using found photographs as well as institutional and incidental archives, I unpack the relationships between queerness, memory, and archive. With this comes a confrontation with the blatant acts of erasure that exist within archival and collective memory as it pertains to queer sexuality. My practice grapples with this confrontation as my creative efforts aim squarely at presenting queer eroticism as worthy and vital to the telling of queer histories. My work anchors these images and narratives in permanence, preserving the memories that might otherwise slip away, and presenting them in a way that invites audiences to see, feel, and understand.

My time at the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program will be spent expanding upon an established body of work I have been building over the last year. This work, titled (in)visible, looks at different visual archives of queer sexuality and simulates the experiences of archivists, historians, and artists who search within institutional archives to find evidence of queerness throughout history. (in)visible’s exploration spans the archival gamut, looking to found vernacular photographs, institutional archives, and incidental archives. Attending this residency will allow me to further expand this exploration, working with the Foundation’s collection of art and other archival materials.
Two series within this project, Degradation and (in)visible, use varied approaches to archival investigation that set the groundwork for what I will create during the residency. (in)visible uses found photographs of sexual encounters between men to present a visual representation of the hidden nature of queer sexuality in the archive. Degradation establishes AIDS-era gay pornography as an incidental archive that I mine with the intention of emphasizing its ephemerality. As technological decay eats away at the recorded memory of uninhibited queer sexuality taking place at the height of the AIDS epidemic, the work communicates this deterioration through its imagery and materiality.
A recent addition to this body of work is titled Unwanted Artifacts, which references both the technical term used by photographers that describes aspects of an image that are undesirable (noise, grain, banding, etc.) and the story of sexual artifacts uncovered at Pompeii. Once collected, these artifacts were housed in the Secret Cabinet at the National Archaeological Museum in Naples. Inspired by these technical and institutional efforts of removal, I have photographed vintage gay pornography, accentuating their noise and abstraction to mirror the approaches to (in)visibility I have previously employed.
The work I make during this residency will further develop this already established project by incorporating the Foundation’s archives, delving into strands of archival research I have not yet had access to. Depictions of queer sexuality through art, ephemera, and sculpture will add more three-dimensional and object-based aspects to this work that I have not yet been able to fully explore. The impermanence of queer identity and the existence of the archive as a dictator of worthiness and queer presence in history is something I will be forever unpacking in my work. To deepen my immersion in queer archives is invaluable to my practice and identity.

My work is rooted in a deep engagement with archives, whether established by others or created by myself. An archival well I have not yet been able to tap into is an existing collection of queer erotic art. The Foundation’s resources of photographs and drawings will allow my work to be in direct conversation with that of other queer artists of the past, building narrative between them. With the Foundation’s erotic ephemera, I see endless curatorial opportunities and find the physicality of an archive to be one of the most palpable ways to speak about memory through an artistic lens.

I have been familiar with Tom of Finland for a few years at this point, but over the past year, I’ve been exposed through social media to the Foundation’s existence. With that, of course, comes my introduction to the Artist-in-Residence Program. As an emerging artist, I believe residencies hold immense value in building a career and when deciding which to apply to, I take into careful consideration how each residency aligns with my practice. The dedication I share with the Foundation to preserving queer history through art and archival research is what compels me to apply.
Dietary preferences include dairy free and gluten free.

Michael Todd Cohen (aka saltpeter) is a queer writer, artist and adoptee living in New England. He stewards lordship house, a private home with public programs to celebrate and serve the literary community. In 2025, Michael was named a Lilac Fellow for Environmental and Social Justice at Vermont Studio Center.

"Cruising Ecologies" is a series of graphic narratives that document and celebrate the natural world where it meets queerness; the notion that cruising converts the estranged into kin through a communion of witness, desire and carnality. How does this essential connection function in the shadow of the Anthropocene?

The initial work explores river lands. Other chapters or narratives in this series will explore: Dunes, Pools, Bathhouses, and all other associated ecologies, sociologies and sensualities.

When first we meet, we are all strangers.

I began work on “Cruising Ecologies” at Vermont Studio Center in the summer of 2025, through a Lilac Fellowship for Environmental and Social Justice. Over two weeks, I was able to use my studio to build out storyboards in pen on large-scale rosin paper for the primary narrative, while making sojourns to the Gihon River and surrounding community for sketch study.

Since then, I’ve worked towards refining the aesthetics and style of “Cruising Ecologies,” moving toward a more confident visual voice rooted in sketch-style. A fellowship over three months would significantly increase the concentrated time I previously had, allowing me the space to develop the additional narratives of Bathhouse and Dune, and dig into the archive around process-work, correspondence and ephemera that would help me to put the present American moment of peril in context. 

I’m seeking a lineage of cruising, the promise of its radical empathy and resistance.

Beyond a space or the archive, I’m seeking queer conversation, camaraderie, adventure, understanding, debate, and encouragement across a spectrum of encounters in Los Angeles. I’m counting on it, in fact, to bring life and shape to “Cruising Ecologies” through lived experience. I’m seeking mentorship that is spontaneous and reciprocal.

In the library section I’ll say more, but I hold Ed Firth’s “Horny and High” up as inspiration for what a risky, vulnerable, bold queer work in graphic narrative can be. I also love the humor and tenderness of Tim Barela’s “Leonard & Larry,” and the warped classical aesthetics of Wakefield Pool in “Boys in the Sand,” and others.

Originals of Tom’s sketch work, in particular wooded cruising scenes, would open a world to me in terms of process, line and stroke. In my personal collection, I’ve pored over "An Imaginary Sketch Book," "Blue Collar," Tachen’s tome, but I want to know Tom better. I want to see lines I haven’t seen. I want to study the cruising dynamics in the composition of negative space; map the dance there. Correspondence is of particular interest, specifically where it meets political action and personal stakes that would help me to put the present American moment of peril in context.

through the queer ether, TBH. My XY magazine teens led to nights at the NYC Eagle, which introduced me to the aesthetics of Tom of Finland and from there opened up...everything. The Foundation itself came to my attention through CultureEdit, as I sought out books to add to my library.

I think a lot about the Queer Archive, Jonathan Ned Katz. About loneliness inherent in "otherness," adoption; about alternative kin constellations offered through essay work like "Queer Kinship" (Duke, 2022).

I think about imagining new forms of gathering and being via Sophie Lewis and James Baldwin. I think a lot about Cruising Utopia (Munoz), the sex-delirium aesthetics of 1970s adult animated films, specifically Ralph Bakshi.

I doubt utopia is a place we can stay, even as I encounter it in queer cruising spaces and attempt to reproduce it in ink, but I keep looking. I keep drawing.

Mixed medium: architecture, material research, collage, etc.

Pauli is an architect born in Finland and currently based in Switzerland where he works at the world-renowned architecture studio Herzog & de Meuron. His architectural work is focused on working with existing buildings and heritage protection. He studied architecture at the University of Oulu in Finland and the Royal Danish Academy in Denmark. He has completed post-master studies at Royal Institute of Art in Sweden with the topic of Decolonizing Architecture. 

During the post-master studies, he focused on exploring the conflict he experienced between his personal identity of a gay man and his professional identity of an architect, which he continued researching during a residency period in New York hosted by the Cooper Union and the Finnish Cultural Institute. Pauli is currently expanding his queer focused architectural praxis through material and spatial research to cruising practices and in general spaces for lust and sex.

Leather, sweat, steel, spit, skin, rubber, cum, latex - Lustful Materialities explores the materiality of spaces where queer people find company for friendship and sex. The project combines an architectural approach with queer perspectives with the aim of expanding my architectural praxis and vocabulary towards visual arts.

Typically not in the focus of architectural discourse, spaces for cruising, sex work, and in general lust, have a seedy reputation. Darkrooms, public toilets, gay saunas, and apps, e.g. Grindr, are arenas for queer people to find sexual fulfilment, or at least a temporary fix. Lustful Materialities takes a sex-positive point-of-view looking into new aesthetic possibilities within these spaces.

Sex creates a need for supportive infrastructure. Healthcare, hygiene, loneliness and abuse are just some of the issues which need to be addressed in a society for sex to be a positive force. Typically, people from privileged backgrounds have access to this infrastructure, but marginalized groups such as LGBTQ+ people, immigrants, or sex workers might have limited access to them.  

Furthermore, shame, pride, or lack of knowledge might prevent people from accessing the supportive infrastructure when needed. Lustful Materialities highlights the positive aspects of spaces for sex to remove the stigma preventing people from enjoying sex and staying safe(r) while doing so.

What the materials used in lustful spaces should achieve is highly ambitious. They need to stimulate, excite, create fantasies, be tactile, while providing a hygienic and safe environment supporting some of the most intimate human emotions. Which materials create an atmosphere? Which are there to keep you safe? How does the smell make you feel? Which materials are there to stimulate and which are making you envision climatic endings?

The project, Lustful Materialities, marks a departure from my architectural practice where I am typically focused on physical outcomes restricted by budgets and technical feasibility. I am eager to explore and expand my practice in the direction of visual arts, but I am still at the start of my journey in this regard. With that in mind, I will use my time at the Tom House to discover new mediums and expressions to my art. This will include, for example, collage, drawing, photography, physical materials and spatial models, as well as writing and archival research. I want to be open and receptive for inspiration that the Tom House and its residents provide while contributing with my expertise in spatial thinking in return.

As an architect working with heritage buildings I am used to diving into archives to inform the architectural process. As I am in the process of developing my personal artistic voice beyond my already established architectural point of view, I am eager to be inspired by as much existing art and historic materials as possible and will utilize the archives on regular basis especially at the beginning of the residency. I am curious to find out about historic cruising practices and imagine restructuring some elements found in the archives as physical structures during the residency.

As a Finnish gay man, Tom of Finland was always a present figure to me when growing up and figuring out about my sexuality and what my sexuality meant in terms of politics and the society at large. Becoming an architect, I have developed a professional interest on queer spaces, especially on cruising practices. The “misuse” and appropriation of space present in cruising provides me with a link to mediate my personal and professional identities. Thus, to develop this line of enquiry as an artistic practice, I naturally turned to Tom of Finland and noticed the A-I-R program.
I am vegetarian, that is I don’t eat any meat, but I do eat milk products and eggs.

I am aware that in terms of a visual art resume I don’t have too much show as of yet, but I strongly believe that the Artist-in-Residence program at the Tom House would be the perfect place for me to expand my artistic voice and to prove to the erotic art community that I have an individual and unique perspective to share with the world. I would be extremely grateful and excited to get a chance to do just that! I have attached 3 collages giving an indication of the direction I would pursue during the residency with my project Lustful Materialities, and my architectural portfolio to showcase my architectural practice.

Ryan Danny Owen (they/him/her) is a non-binary award-winning author, researcher, queer archivist, and interdisciplinary artist based in Mohkinistis/ Treaty 7/ Calgary, Alberta. Their work reexamines nostalgia, trans identity, longing, sexuality, and kinship through a transdisciplinary approach combining archival practices, installation, video, craft, and writing. 


Utilizing various process based approaches in order to reexamine nostalgia, trans identity, longing, and kinship, focusing on automatic response and long term relationships with materials to uncover ways of rendering the absent body, loss, sexuality, longing, and time. Deconstructing archives of gay pornographic media as representations of intergenerational loss and constructing spaces through a fixation with artifice, manipulation, research, and experimentation.

During the previous year, I focused my studio time developing skills in designing and handcrafting stained glass windows completely self taught. From my years of experience pattern drafting for garment making, construction, and illustration, I’ve been able to experiment with learning stain glass design and production.

Over a month-long period I designed and assembled a stained glass window project for my exhibition DIRTY PICTURE II  at Struts gallery in Sackville, NB.  Double Feature, 2025 is a reinterpretation of archival gay pornographic centerfold into a stained glass window. Based on a magazine centerfold from, SOLO, Number. 2, July 1979 featuring Jeff Hitchcock in his only appearance in print and video as he examines his nude body reflected across a series of mirrors. The photographs included in the magazine were taken on the set of the pornographic looping film, Spittin’ Image, produced by Nova Studios in 1979 and directed by Scott Masters. Inspired by the myth of Narcissus, Hitchcock watches himself in the mirror over his bed and fantasizes himself masterbating in an increasingly complex and stimulating hall of mirrors. Appearing at first as multiple figures, the image reveals itself to be one form examining his body in the various reflections. Sexual in nature and introspective at second glance, this image becomes an incredibly charged examination of body dysmorphia, loss, and dissociation. With no digitized editions of this video it has become a piece of lost media with only my personal archived magazine remaining as a rare printed remnant.  

During a residency at Tom house I will design and create a series of stained glass windows reinterpreting selected images, text, and material from the Tom of Finland archive as a way of elevating queer hypersexualized material. Through transforming and elevating perverse material into cathedral glass, I document queer history on the edge of being lost and reexamine how a viewer experiences these images as well as engage an archive in a way that challenges both myself and the material. As a non binary queer individual growing up in the Albertan prairies pornographic imagery became a point of sanctuary in isolation but also revealed to me my own absence within these images. In the process of deconstructing the imagery into abstract representations in carefully broken glass, I can examine the complicated reality of these images as both reflections of pleasure, masculinity, and hyper-sexuality and documentations of loss, absence, and time.

My body of work deconstructs material as a way of reclaiming an archive and using it to explore sex, loss, longing and kinship. This opportunity to make work that specifically examines and explores the archives at TOFF is a unique opportunity to utilize my approaches from years of engaging my personal archive of pornographic magazines, bootleg gay porn vhs tapes, and collected queer media in new ways. Since this project will be entirely informed by the archive over the three month period, it allows the work to transform and form as a reflection of my time on-site at the foundation

I heard about the Tom of Finland AIR program through Canadian artists, Zachari Logan and August Klintberg during a residency at the Banff Centre for Arts and Creativity. It was suggested as an opportunity to continue to expand my exploration in deconstructions of archival material as well as an opportunity to further my engagement with pornographic media without the threat of censorship as I had experienced my work being heavily censored or devalued during my experiences as a queer trans artist based in Alberta.

Information about the projects included within my work sample,

Fantasy Sequence (Blue Fantasy Motel), 2024 & Fantasy Sequence II (Blue Fantasy Motor Inn), 2026 are site specific installations which explored the constructing of temporary spaces exploring the idealization of sexuality, masculinity, gender through hypersexualized material. Imagined as a constructed motel room set of a fictitious gay porno film as a pavilion to queer longing, nostalgia, fantasy, loss, and the absent body. Utilizing a tone of blue, which has appeared within the work of various other queer artists such as Derek Jarman and Felix Gonzalez Torres, as a way to render a pavilion to queer nostalgia, loss, love, and time, through a combination of found material and handmade objects. 

These installations included the experimental video piece, BED (30 min, 2024-2026) assembled of shots of sleepers, sheets, clocks, beds, and weapons from an archive of over 70 found VHS bootleg gay pornography tapes re-recorded by the previous owner over home tapes of late-night cable films, TV shows, and personal videos from the early 90s to the early 2000s. Worn down by time, and repeated viewings, sections of clips are cut out and manipulated using magnets, heat, analog editing and video bending to distort and disrupt the tapes. The footage evolves into a disassembled dream-like representation of nostalgia, fantasy, queer identity, loss, and desire, in collaboration with the imprint of the previous owner. 

The ongoing Rubbing series utilizes original pages and archival prints of enlarged gay pornographic magazines from the 1970s to the early 2000s, applying erasers, razors, sandpaper, blades and chemicals to destroy and fade out the pigments to form unique compositions through the use of removal at the cost of the original. Exploring performing an act of pleasure such as “rubbing out” onto a loaded material to distort and destroy the archived page challenging loss, sexuality, and identity.

I am a multidisciplinary artist working at the intersection of botanical illustration, homoerotic imagery, and queer fetish aesthetics. My practice interrogates binaries of hard and soft, nature and artifice, tenderness and power, reimagining flowers and organic forms as sites of erotic charge rather than passive beauty.

Influenced by Robert Mapplethorpe, Irving Penn, Judy Chicago, and Tom of Finland, my work engages queer erotic history as a living, embodied language. By subverting traditional botanical conventions, I reclaim floral imagery as symbolic of desire, control, submission, and transformation. Leather, coded colour, and anthropomorphic forms collapse distinctions between body and plant, ornament and flesh.

Eroticism functions in my practice as a political and archival act—insisting queer pleasure, fetish, and fantasy remain visible and unapologetic.

Over 22 years, I have exhibited nationally and internationally, received major awards including the ASBA Dianne Bouchier Award (2013), and am represented by Scott Livesey Galleries. My work is held in significant public and private collections worldwide.

During the three-month residency, I will undertake focused research within the Foundation’s archives as a primary source of creative inspiration. This research will inform the development of a new body of work that merges botanical painting with leather and fetish aesthetics, drawing conceptual and visual reference from Touko Laaksonen’s oeuvre. The colour palette and meticulous painting techniques central to my botanical practice will be extended into this series, foregrounding the interconnectedness of masculinity, desire, and the natural world.

“The wearer should fit the leather, not the leather fit the wearer.”

Building on my established practice, these works aim to engage audiences in a deeply personal and culturally specific way. The project focuses on gay fetish subculture with a strong commitment to realism and embodied experience. Painting functions not only as an outcome, but as a method of sustained inquiry. The TOF residency will act as a testing ground for ideas that explore leather identity alongside organic form, drawing on the erotic material of the Foundation’s archives while addressing the psychological and cultural concerns of the contemporary gay man. By examining the relationship between fetish identity and nature, the project seeks insight into broader social constructions of masculinity.

At its core, the project investigates productive dichotomies—hard and soft, nature and artifice, intimacy and exhibitionism—through a synthesis of botanical art, homoerotic imagery, and fetish aesthetics. Inspired by Tom of Finland’s visual language, the work stages a dialogue between delicate organic forms and assertive fetish iconography, creating imagery that is both sensual and subversive.

Flowers, leaves, and vines are interwoven with symbols from fetish culture such as leather, chains, and the hanky code. This juxtaposition reimagines botanical forms not as symbols of purity or passive beauty, but as sites of erotic charge, transformation, and self-discovery. Anthropomorphic suggestions further dissolve boundaries between body and plant, evoking hybridity and embodiment.

Colour symbolism plays a critical role, including direct references to the hanky code as a visual lexicon of desire and coded communication. These layered cues invite viewers to engage on multiple levels.

In a global climate of rising conservatism and increasing challenges to LGBTQI+ visibility, this project asserts queer pleasure and fetish as acts of resistance, archival continuity, and cultural survival.

I plan to engage with the Tom of Finland Foundation archives through close study of finished works and supporting materials, including sketches, correspondence, publications, and ephemera. I am particularly interested in Tom of Finland’s use of exaggeration, visual confidence, and coded masculinity as tools of empowerment and communication. This research will inform how desire, fetish, and identity are visually coded in my project Floreoscuro, especially through parallels between Tom’s use of uniforms and signifiers and my own use of botanical symbolism and the hanky code.

I first became aware of the TOM House Artist-in-Residence program ahead of Touko Laaksonen’s centenary in 2020. Since 2018, I have developed a series of leather watercolours, culminating in the 2019 exhibition Sobriquet at Scott Livesey Galleries, aligned with Melbourne’s AWOL (August Week of Leather). Actively engaged in the local leather and fetish art community, I learned more about the Foundation through my gallery and explored residency opportunities, reaching out directly before COVID-19 disrupted centenary initiatives. I am now a member and contributing artist, with three works held in the Foundation’s collection.

I would like the panel to note that most of the works I have submitted are works in progress. They demonstrate the extensive layering, washes, and intricate detail involved in my process, reflecting the time, precision, and care required to bring each piece to its full expression. By sharing works at this stage, I hope to provide insight into the methodology and dedication behind my practice, highlighting the intensive engagement with colour, texture, and form that underpins the development of my ongoing projects.

responses.answer: Archival research, photography, sound, publishing, installation, performative and collaborative practices.

TOMBOYS DON’T CRY (TBD’C) is a queer transfeminist artistic platform founded in 2011, moving fluidly across visual art, performance, sound, publishing, and nightlife as political and cultural practices. TBD’C investigates desire, erotic imagery, and informal systems of transmission, treating archives as living, embodied processes. Collaboration functions as both method and medium, generating collective situations that activate communities and challenge institutional hierarchies.
The platform was co-founded by artist Dafne Boggeri, whose research explores collaboration, queer archives, photography and installations, using margins, errors, and collective processes as generative tools for knowledge and memory production.
A core member of TBD’C is Alien, an artist working between photography, publishing, and moving image. Her ongoing research BODYBUILDERS investigates alternative drag practices, identities and realities vs fiction.

HARD COPY SOFT TOUCH is a research project developed by TOMBOYS DON’T CRY, initiated by Dafne Boggeri in collaboration with Alien. The project moves through queer archives worldwide, treating them as spaces where desire, memory, and transmission take shape, dissolve, and reappear. Rather than fixed repositories, these archives are understood as living bodies, marked by touch, repetition, absence, and circulation.
The residency at the Tom of Finland Foundation represents a crucial moment within this broader research. Working in Los Angeles, with access to one of the most influential queer erotic archives, allows us to engage deeply with its material and legacy. This archive does not stand alone, but enters into dialogue with informal, local, and ephemeral archives that persist through copying, sharing, and collective care. Los Angeles becomes a strategic site where historical material, contemporary practices, and community-based knowledge intersect.
As queer lesbian artists, we approach an archive historically centered on gay male, often cis, erotic imaginaries from a connected but lateral position. This perspective does not aim to overwrite the archive’s legacy, but to allow new questions and points of contact to emerge without flattening its history. We are interested in how an archive can remain faithful to its origins while making space for other bodies, desires, and ways of looking.
The project treats the archive as a material and sensorial space rather than a neutral repository. Research and production develop simultaneously: archival materials are studied and reworked through scanning, photography, sound recording, and writing. Bootlegging and graphic reworking are central to TBD’C’s methodology, reflecting long-standing queer strategies of access, survival, and circulation. Words, fragments, and typography function as visual and critical tools, shaping how images are read, accessed, and shared.
The residency will lead to a mixed body of work made of sound, images, scans, and text. Rather than a closed outcome, the project favors formats that circulate, that can be listened to, held, copied, and passed on. The aim is not to monumentalize the archive, but to keep it alive, porous, and available to many.

We plan to engage with the archive through close, attentive work with original artworks, photographs, personal papers, and ephemera, focusing on marginal, less-circulated, and fragile materials. Our approach privileges physical proximity and slow observation to access details that often remain implicit or overlooked. We are particularly interested in elements that are ephemeral or less immediately visual, such as traces of sound, voice, and gesture. Through visual manipulation, sound work, and graphic intervention, we approach the archive as a form of collaboration, translating and re-materializing its contents to open new perspectives.

We learned about the Tom of Finland Foundation Artist-in-Residence Program through our professional and community-based networks of queer artists, publishers, and researchers. We have followed the work of past residents, including Sweatmother, and encountering his work developed during the residency encouraged us to apply.

This application is submitted by TOMBOYS DON’T CRY as a collaborative project developed by Dafne Boggeri and Alien. We are applying as a duo, with the intention of living and working together during the residency, as shared time and proximity are central to our collaborative process.

Tom of Finland has long been a primary reference in my understanding of queer art. Through research into artistic residencies and ongoing attention to the Foundation’s social media and publications, I discovered the Artist-in-Residence Program as a space that strongly aligns with my artistic investigation and development.

I plan to engage with the archives by identifying visual symbols and cultural codes present in Tom of Finland’s work and historical context. Through this research, I aim to establish a dialogue between his approaches and contemporary queer art practices.

This process will allow me to reflect on which themes remain central within queer communities today and which aspects have yet to be fully explored in painting. The guiding question of this research is: "How would Tom of Finland exist and create within our current cultural context?"

My residency proposal focuses on developing new discussions around queer individuality and the construction of the queer body today. Through research within the Foundation’s archives and engagement with the local community, I aim to question how queer bodies are represented, perceived, and historically constructed.

The project seeks to investigate queer existence beyond fixed categories, understanding it as fluid, non-binary, and constantly evolving. By analyzing visual codes, symbols, and narratives present in queer culture, I want to identify how these elements operate in contemporary visual language and how they can be translated into painting.

A central goal of this residency is to develop my own canon, one that reflects present-day queer experiences while remaining in dialogue with historical references. Painting will function as a space of research, where eroticism, intimacy, and memory intersect.

The outcome of the residency will be a series of works that reflect on the continuity and transformation of queer representation, positioning painting as a tool for questioning norms and expanding imaginaries around queer bodies.

I am an architect and visual artist based in Lisbon. My work explores queer bodies, desire, and intimacy through painting, focusing on emotional and sensual narratives.

Balancing instinct and intention, I create layered compositions that engage memory, eroticism, and personal experience. Since completing my studies in Architecture (FUMEC, 2019) and Fine Arts (Escola Guignard, 2021), I have been developing a distinct visual language that challenges normative representations and embraces the complexity of queer existence.

My practice combines traditional painting techniques with experimental approaches, seeking to create intimate and transformative encounters between the artwork and the viewer.

The image appears to me like a flash in my mind - including fragments of memories and dreams, which generate an image that pierces my innermost being because it connects with a series of symptoms felt throughout the experience of queer life.
The figurative expression of my work comes from the need for communication that connects with reality through human bodies, as sensations perceived and developed by humans, who, through their attributes, narrate human sensations - and this is what will manifest the reflection of oneself through the other and discuss relationships that build sentimental values.

In technical terms, the image functions like an architectural project, or a film set, where elements are placed in harmony in the camera/viewer's field of view, that is, presented in a three-dimensional field, intentionally, in order to construct a concise narrative reading through internal and external elements - this is the intention, the driving force of the process but which does not define it, the work loses me until it finds itself.

The narrative is built on enjoyment, which attracts me, pulsates, propels me, and so the first questions and a need begin, as well as the desire that arises from lack. I need to place it on physical space, so I develop the first traces of multidimensional reality that traverse memory, experiences, the everyday, and in confrontation with fantasy, imagination. Both acting in favor of questioning desire.

I start on paper or even intuitively on the canvas, sometimes in subtle layers, other times not so much, thus I construct a sketch of the mind's projection, transfer it to the physical, and this projection reflects and intensifies as new layers appear. I use time to generate a being that is built with the context, the feeling, the material, the intention, the symbolism, the meanings.

A series of elements construct the painting as a whole; the artwork depends on the artist just as the artist depends on the work in a relationship of constant, living development, of distinct elements that feed each other.

The intuitive gesture is important, even when distorted in proportion and perspective; the distortion of reality belongs to the idea of ​​a work of art, it shifts from reality when presenting the result of the act of creation itself.

The work of art is multidisciplinary in its process. Elements are connected in order to enhance a feeling, or a heightened perception of what is real, so that, through its context, it causes a shift that resonates with the collective.

Gio Ventura is an artist whose practice is steroid-charged and architectural, treating muscle as both construction material and erotic surface. Drawing from sports mythologie, especially bodybuilding, Ventura approaches hypertrophy as a game of excess, control, failure and seduction. Their first film, Les Sports Xtrem, a muscular bromance self-financed through the sale of their scooter, reclaims bodybuilding from macho cis norms to invent macho trans, asserting the pleasure of being buff.
At the core of their work is an investigation into how dissident sexualities circulate through urban grey zones: cruising areas, disappearing sex cinemas, forgotten gyms. Working from within spaces historically coded as gay male only, Ventura navigates their frictions as a trans body, exploring how intimacy is increasingly regulated and isolated by the disciplining of urban space and the gentrification of desire. Their work invents new scripts where bodies meet through play and desire.
Ventura is also an actor, notably in Queens of Drama (Cannes, 2024).

Bite me (how to know if you’re sleeping with bedbugs)
People associate Paris with croissants and the Eiffel Tower. For me, it is mostly toilets on the landing that smell like piss, thin walls through which you hear neighbors fuck and bedbugs. A few months ago, my entire building was infested. I was broke and unemployed. With bills piling up, I returned to sex work, something I had stopped years earlier. I first bought myself a “girl” uniform again after years living as a trans boy. My first gig was with a ninety-year-old fist enthusiast I spent a holiday with. With that money and the infestation worsening, I bagged almost everything I owned and sent it to an industrial freezer. My life disappeared into garbage bags. Then I spent weeks fucking men for cash. The disinfection company came seven times, but somehow the bugs always returned.
At night, two images began to merge in my mind: dicks and bugs, infestation and desire. Paranoia became erotic as I fantasized about spreading them, letting pleasure and contamination intertwine. Eventually, I ended up working with a former client who liked me, a guy organizing gangbangs and orgies in the Parisian suburbs. The fear of infestation never left, it only grew. I was constantly close to people, and desire and infestation became inseparable, intoxicating.
Straight men seem obsessed with making women pregnant, as nearly all of my clients tried to fuck me without a condom and wanted to “cum inside me”. Or as in Cooper’s novel Sluts, one character says: “I love the idea that having hot sex with a bottom can have a permanent, negative impact on his life, making sex heavy and meaningful(…) I love how gay guys can be like straight guys who wonder how many illegible kids there could be out there with their DNA.” What is left then for transboys, I wonder? What dirty stuff can we infect others with?
During the Tom of Finland Foundation residency, I wish to develop this project as a series of works in multiple media (Bedbug bedtimes stories, drawings and more) merging the entities that shared my bed: men and bedbugs, both parasites in their own way. These works explore how queer desire, infestation and erotic obsession circulate together.

During the residency, I will engage with the Tom of Finland archives as both inspiration and dialogue. I want to study his drawings, photographs and ephemera, how desire is formalized, bodies are staged and erotic obsession is made visual. I aim to connect my own archive of intimate, chaotic and experiences with his, merging personal obsessions with another proud, perverse vision. The reference to gaze, ritual, and repetition in his archive will inform my project, shaping the way I render bodies, parasites and desire.

I heard about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program through Sweatmother, who founded the Otherness Archives and was a resident this year. They posted enthusiastically about their experience, which made me curious. They recently invited me to screen my film as part of Do You Even Lift Bro?, a program celebrating three years of Otherness Archives in London with a series of queer and experimental films.

Zachary Zane is a sex columnist and author whose work focuses on sexuality, culture, and the LGBTQ community. He is the author of Boyslut: A Memoir and Manifesto and co-author of Men’s Health: Best. Sex. Ever. He is currently Grindr’s sex and relationship expert and is editor-in-chief of the BOYSLUT Zine, which publishes nonfiction and fiction erotica from kinksters across the globe. He’s had sex and relationship columns at Men’s Health, Cosmopolitan, and bi.org, among others. His work has been featured in the New York Times, Rolling Stone, Washington Post, GQ, Playboy, and more.

As a leading bisexual activist, Zane has spoken across the country at universities and panels alike, discussing issues pertaining to the bisexual community, sex-positivity, and ethical non-monogamy. 

He attended the "Bisexual White House Briefing" at the White House (under Obama) and has been featured as an expert on several podcasts, including "The Savage Lovecast" and “Whoreible Decisions.”

I’ll be honest: I’m still shaping the project, and it may change with new findings from the ToF archive. That said, here’s what’s piquing my interest:

I’ve started noticing a backlash against the Tom of Finland aesthetic, especially among more progressive queers (which is how I identify). There’s an assumption that embodying the hypermasculine, very shredded Tom aesthetic makes you a douche-bag. The stereotype is that you’re a self-absorbed “toxic top,” secretly insecure and endlessly working out; that you don’t care if the bottom finishes, that you cling to traditional gender roles—even as a queer man. You’re not the type of gay who can hang with the dolls, even though you desperately want to fuck them, and you don’t call your gay besties “sis.” In short, you are what’s wrong with the gay male community.

I vehemently disagree.

An aesthetic is just that—an aesthetic, a style. It doesn’t dictate behavior or moral character. I think any marginalized group, but especially gay men, often cannibalize each other. We attack fellow members of our community instead of focusing on larger threats from outside our community, including this administration, right-wingers, etc.

Still, I understand why many gay men are turning on Tom. Having run a very queer-inclusive sex column at Men’s Health for half a decade, I’ve fielded hundreds of messages from gay and bisexual men who covet men projecting the Tom archetype, yet get rejected by those same men for being undesirable (i.e., “unfuckable”). These hurtful interactions have left them feeling small. 

This rejection is powerful. It festers, tainting both the brain and soul. It warps our perceptions of reality and turns our thinking binary, as a form of protection (e.g., I am the victim, these men are bad, and what’s wrong with the gay community today). 

So, I’d like to write something that interrupts this line of thinking—something that celebrates the hypermasculine, Tom of Finland aesthetic and redirects the gay community toward external threats to our existence. This could take several forms, including a 3,000-word cover story for a sex-positive magazine like Playboy that delves into the nuances of these discussions. I’m also assembling a collection of essays, and an in-depth piece on this topic could fit beautifully within that body of work.

See attached for the writing sample. (Also, it seemed like you only wanted one writing sample, but then I also see 3-8 work samples for review. I believe that's for illustrators/visual artists. But if you'd like more writing samples from me, please let me know!) Additionally, I ended up just uploading the same PDF three times because the form required three PDFs in order to submit.

I hope to be inspired by the archives and find relevant works for my thesis that help bolster my argument and separate the ToF hypermasculine aesthetic from behavior and morals. I hope to find illustrations, photographs, and written material that make my argument as strong as possible and demonstrate that ToF can foster community rather than diminish it.

Sam Cottington is an artist, writer and theatre maker living between London and Frankfurt. 

He’s presented work at Ginny On Frederick (London), Nir Altman (Munich) and Sculpture Centre (New York). 

He’s an associate artist at London Performance studios (London) and a collection of his plays was published by Montez press. His first book ‘People Person’ was published by JOAN (London)

He is a graduate of Monica Baer’s class at the städelschule (Frankfurt) and an intern at the Wooster group (New York)

Through interdisciplinary experiments in collage he often presents “reality” as contradictory and mutable; the product of specific historical and social economies.

The production and trajectories of the theatre, the family, and desiring subjectivities are recurrent in his practice, often culminating in devised interventions which address their formal capacities as well as their audiences expectations.

During this residency I would research, write, rehearse and perform a new participatory performance performed over the phone. 

Following on from my ‘phone plays’ project, in which I wrote plays for live actors to perform over the phone as a one-to-one performance— I will complete my first ‘text play’. 

Photographic, video, and graphic elements will be produced in collaboration with LA-based artists— and presented within the time-based new media project—during each one-to-one performance, straight into audiences phones.


The script will focus on a character who is compelled to contact a stranger they’ve seen images of online but have never met. As the performance progresses, sexually explicit still and moving images are shared by the performer alongside links to found material. I’m in communication with Ron Athey and Calla Henkel to produce visual material on location in LA, to be shared with audiences during the performance.

The performance will last 48 hours per person. Audience members will be able to sign up and choose a slot. After confirming their participation, they will receive their first text message at 7am, at which point the performance begins.

During the residency I would focus on reading the archive of erotic books and periodicals. 

I'm interested in researching the changes in styles and themes of queer erotic writing, particularly in relation to the communities which form around erotic communication concomitantly with technological innovation.
My friends Gray Wielebinski and P.Staff have previously been Artist-in-Residence and suggested I apply.

Zach Grear (b. 1984, San Francisco, CA, USA) is a writer and self-taught artist based in Brooklyn. He attended San Francisco State University and graduated with a BA in Creative Writing. In 2010 he moved to New York City and in 2018 quit his full-time job to pursue his art practice. His work focuses on gay erotic imagery inspired by the gay porn he shoplifted as a teen. His art has been featured in New York Pride Guide magazine, the Oslo Erotic Film & Art Festival, The AIDS Memorial T-Shirt, and LaMama Galleria among others.

I'm excited to expand upon my project ENDLESS BLISS, which was first actualized in my solo show in New York this past October. ENDLESS BLISS is about exalting gay sex, which is still largely marginalized or ignored in the mainstream art world, into a deified realm. I do this through drawing ornate and overwhelming tattoos on found gay erotica. Tattoos are a beauty standard for me. The more I get the hotter I feel. As a Black heavily tattooed person this also provides a way for me to enter the primarily white clean-cut fantasy presented in much of vintage gay erotica. During my residency, I will expand ENDLESS BLISS into a place of worship within the TOM House. Alongside the vintage gay erotica, I intend to photograph queer LA natives and the other artists-in-residence in order to incorporate contemporary queerness into ENDLESS BLISS. I'm excited to tap into the natural cruise-y elements of LA as well. Places like Pleasure Park will provide an appropriately Eden-esque backdrop for my collage work. Finally, the hand drawn one-of-one leather vests and jackets I create during this time will be a way for guests to interact with and wear my tattoo'd beauty standard.

As someone who shoplifted gay porn magazines as a teenager, I'm vividly connected to archival and vintage queer erotica. My art utilizes vintage gay erotica and it will be a boon to research imagery to use for ENDLESS BLISS. Being heavily tattooed, with several Tom of Finland designs on my body, I'd love to see the ways in which tattoos are represented throughout the archives, and how they are used to signal time, profession/socio economic status, and desire. I'm excited to take note of the tattoo designs I find and the ways I can modernize them in the art and leather work I create for ENDLESS BLISS.
I've heard about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program through the Tom of Finland Foundation website, and through previous Artists in Residence Michael Espinoza, Lukasz Leja, and Ryan Davis.

Matthew Lax is a filmmaker, artist, and writer based in New York and Los Angeles. 


His work has been shown at Viennale (Austria), IHME Contemporary (Helsinki), Rencontres Internationales (Paris), MIX New York and MIX Brasil (São Paulo), table (Chicago), Los Angeles Municipal Art Gallery, Human Resources, Los Angeles Contemporary Archive, REDCAT, and the Winnipeg Underground Film Festival. In 2025, he was the subject of a solo presentation at the 54th International Film Festival Rotterdam.


Lax’s writing has appeared in publications including Texte Zur Kunst, BOMB, the Los Angeles Review of Books, and Millenium Film Journal. He is a recipient of a 2024 Lightning Fund Grant from LACE and the Andy Warhol Foundation, and a 2025 Creative Research Grant from The Foundation for Contemporary Arts.


Lax’s films are distributed by Video Data Bank in Chicago. He received a BFA from Syracuse University and an MFA from California Institute of the Arts.

THE ZONE is a multichannel experimental film installation about the everchanging interiors of the notorious Hollywood gay sex club, The Zone. Focusing on the club’s architectural and psychic corpus as a site of sexual desire, local activism, and urban nightlife, the installation explores the building’s eventual transformation into a blue-chip art gallery, the disappearance of queer spaces, and the collective memories The Zone left behind. 


Opening at the height of the AIDS epidemic in 1991 and closing during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, The Zone was a unique fixture in the Los Angeles gay community. The club was initially intended as a drug-and-alcohol-free meeting place for sober gay men, but as one of the first sex clubs to operate within city limits, it was often raided by LAPD.


To protect the identities of its patrons, The Zone also maintained a strict no-photography policy. As a result, it has no functional archive and scarce documentation of its interiors. Moreover, the club’s identity was always in flux: it burned down once, changed ownership four times, and was constantly being renovated and improved.


In 2025, I tracked down The Zone’s original owner, 68-year-old Peter Deep, in Playa Del Carmen, Mexico. During our interview, I asked Deep to recall the club’s interiors. After insisting he couldn’t remember, he produced a blueprint by hand, explaining that he was a former architecture student. In THE ZONE, Deep’s drawing functions as a centerpiece that initiates the erotic searching at the heart of the film, using the concept of a collaborative drawing to reconstitute the desires that brought club patrons into the same space under similar repressive political conditions. 


My time at ToF will focus on expanding my embodied research into The Zone, which will include conducting interviews with former club patrons, such as pornographer ChiChi LaRue, and producing collaborative drawings like Deep’s where subjects produce renderings of the club while recalling their personal experiences there. From those dialogues, I will develop original footage that will be presented in a multichannel film installation, presented alongside documentation of rare surviving ephemera, and establishing shots isolated from the 8 porn films, including titles by Channel1 Releasing and Dick Wadd Studios, shot on site. The memory drawings will also be composited into a 3D model, reimagining the club’s interior and surrounding lot, in a series of sculptures and sets that will be used for filmed performances.

Tom died in 1991, the year The Zone opened its doors. Reviewing the archival materials in the Foundation’s holdings, I’ll try to understand Los Angeles queer life between 1991 and today. I will also search for representations of The Zone in the archive as well as artwork produced by other artists who frequented the club. 


In the archive I will research architectures of desire and representations of queer cruising spaces, so I can develop language about the disappearance of cruising from physical space and its resurgence online, noting how this change parallels gentrification dynamics.
My colleagues Jamison Edgar and Wyatt Coday advised me to apply for the Artist-in-Residence program, and specifically noted that ToF board member Terence McFarland might take an interest in my work.

As an artist whose work incorporates their personal life and their connection with their collaborators, I have consistently faced resistance from audiences and viewers who misunderstand the intricacies of queerness and sexuality. 

For example, my film trilogy A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG (2022-2024) features a number of formal experiments and interventions with professional dog trainers and my family, who breed collies, juxtaposed with explorations of puppy play, including improvised scenes with myself and  members of the pup community. Even though the puppy players shown in the film do not perform any sexual acts, I have received resistance about the film’s content, with many saying that the work is “too gay” or that puppy play is immoral. Because of this judgement, many viewers fail to understand the broader ideas of the film or its context within my practice: I’m a queer fraternal quadruplet interested in the psychic, social, and economic realities of collective identity. My films are made in collaboration with non-actors, looking toward the “everyday ensembles” found within families, hobby clubs, and kink communities to better understand how we engage in collective activities, especially those that are considered strange, taboo, or queer, as mirrors of each other – not just individuals.

Because ToF’s existence is rooted in queerness and human sexuality, the ToF residency would be an ideal place to create, host, and assemble new work without censorship or judgement. Moreover, I believe the residency would further encourage my work’s growth and erotic capacity in a safe environment. I also believe that my practice in documenting queer communities and preserving alternative histories has become a political necessity as the US rapidly declines into an authoritarian state.

In addition to the research I plan to conduct at ToF, I also intend to visit ONE Archives at USC, the Los Angeles City Hall of Records, and mainstream and underground LA queer media outlets which are related to, or covered The Zone, such as The LA Times, Homo Riot Zine, and LA Weekly.

MATTHEW LAX
ARTWORKS DESCRIPTION

1. Installation view. A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG, PART TWO, Human Resources, November 2024

two-channel 4k video with sound, modified dog kennel, flatscreens, media player
72 x 120 x 120 inches
47 min, 25 sec loop
Ed. 1 of 2, + 1 AP

A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG is an experimental film trilogy and multimedia project that investigates family, survival, and discipline. Made in collaboration with my collie-breeding parents and their dogs, professional dog trainers, and the puppy play kink community, the trilogy explores the relationship between humanity and canines, searching for meaning in the various activities we do to make, become, and train dogs. 

In the installation version, the two channel film is installed inside a lifesize dog kennel. The viewer can watch the film from inside the dog kennel or through the bars. 


2. Excerpt. A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG, PART TWO, 2024-2025 Single channel version 

	Single channel version
	4k video with sound
	47 min, 25 sec loop


Structured around a performative reading of Herve Guibert’s 1982 smut novella, "Les Chiens," Part Two explores pack behaviour through notions of dominance, submission, trust and consent, both within human and dog relationships and within pup play. Love and bonding become a central theme, weaving together further investigation into pup relationships, training sessions with a traumatized dog and Lax’s own relationship to the dogs in his life that he has loved and lost.

The experimental film Gay Men’s Book Club brings together seven homosexuals, ages 26-85 and strangers to one another, to discuss the book Elite Capture by Olúfẹ́mi O. Táíwò. Facilitated by the filmmaker in collaboration with community organizer, founding member of the Radical Faeries, and Jungian psychologist Dr. Don Kilhefner, the intergenerational, unscripted conversation explores Táíwò’s concept of elite capture as it relates to community, privilege, organizing, and intersectionality.

6. Erotic Non-Fiction, 2024 (still)
	
2 minutes
16mm film

Erotic Non-Fiction is an excerpt from a short film that documents my boyfriend Christopher, and I getting our nipples pierced at Aesthetic Ambition Studio in Santa Monica. The film is in conversation with diaristic artworks and frank documentation of sado-masochism by queer artists such as Law and Order (2012) by Jan Soldat and Bob Flannigan’s Fuck Journal (1987), which turn to various modes of representation to chart the body, queer intimacy, and the passage of time. 

7. Portrait of a Blind Horse, 2023 (still)

2 minutes
16mm film

Portrait of a Blind Horse is a visual study of my mother’s horse Chevy, who is the same age as me and my siblings. Chevy suddenly went blind after losing her foal in 2014, and has since become a metaphor for the punishing demands of motherhood in our family. 

8. Installation view. Brunt Drama, 2018

Animated loop loop with sound, double-sided projection
frosted plexiglass, aluminum frame
47 3/8 x 71 3/8 x 36 3/8 inches
7 min. 30 sec. loop

"Brunt Drama" addresses the inherent violence of the real, the simulated, and the dubious pleasure of witnessing. In the loop, narratives of safety, risk, and use-value are tested via 3D-rendered, plastic surrogates. Choreographed motion-studies and textual, Brechtian vignettes produce alternative translations and behaviors, both acceptable and indecent. Brunt Drama manipulates the slipperiness of affect-inducing data visualizations intended to ameliorate fear into consumption — presenting a simulation of the unrepresentable, visible from both sides of the screen.

LINK: https://vimeo.com/295100851/e4bfbb36b4?share=copy&fl=cl&fe=ci
