An experimental essay that explores discipline, faith, disability and family alongside the production of its paired multimedia project, A TIRED DOG IS A GOOD DOG. Mining his own autobiography as a queer, Jewish quadruplet growing up on a Collie-breeding farm in New Jersey, Lax considers puppy play and his own process in conversation with other dog films, dog ideas and dog projects by Laurie Anderson, Donna Haraway, Franz Kafka, Diana Thater, the "Lassie" franchise and others.

Our current moment’s cultural narrative is dominated by Fear and Power. Engaging with fascism is inescapable for most of the populace, even those with the privilege to safely navigate day to day life. During times of stress and economic anxiety the culture wars flourish and people hate and fear one another; their minds polluted by narratives meant to alienate and terrorize. How can we move through this moment when we feel powerless? Are we truly powerless or do we all have an innate capacity to resist? Does power have limits and how can we transcend these limits? Can sexual intimacy rewrite the rules of power in a lasting and impactful way?

My film ‘I Am Not Afraid’ will approach these questions through the medium of performance and video. The context of power framed via the intercommunity dynamics of gay man and dyke, cis man and FTM, as well as sex worker and client. A complex power play initiated by the question: What is your deepest sexual desire? And how can I, a transmasculine person, help facilitate it? By curating a societal vacuum to safely explore the potentially dangerous Other, I will use my vulnerability to showcase the precarity, fluidity and performance of power dynamics.

Separated into three or four main scenes, the body of the film will be set inside a liminal space--such as a motel room--where the interactions between me and the subjects will occur. The subjects being different gay cis men who I will interview and then proceed to engage in BDSM and/or sexual acts with. The scenes will be intervaled with various images meant to illustrate the spiritual and creative space of a sexual encounter: dancing, breathwork, a couple kissing, cute animals, vintage pornography, someone reading etc. Then each scene ends with a role reversal wherein the subject assumes the role of the vulnerable, having just revealed to me their hidden Shadow Self. In doing so, the sexual interaction that occurs is transformed into a social, spiritual and artistic practice; one that exposes the complex networks of Fear and Power inherent to our closest encounters with one another.

An integral part of my art practice involves looking at BDSM and queer sex imagery for inspiration and research. I would love the opportunity to study the archive and learn about the history of deviant sexuality, doing so will help me develop the ways I use sex as a practice and position it within a broader cultural context. To be more specific, I identify as a dyke and have been exposed to mostly lesbian-adjacent archival material. It will be interesting to use gay male archives to inform how I might play that role in my proposed film project.

Genevieve Kuzak’s work explores, by way of erotic offerings, the capacity of the category of “butchness.” He offers, through vivid and tactile videography and photography, windows into butch sexuality, delicate butch feelings, and the generative slippage that occurs between the butch, the butch boy, the twink boy, the t-boy, and more. 
Using imagery from the realm of internet porn and Tumblr aesthetics, he not only offers preview into the private lives of butches who fuck each other, but also brings together a complex cast of characters to depict queer liberation in its most intimate settings. Kuzak’s lens challenges us to explore, affirm, and comprehend the bridge between FTM, faggot, and dyke sexuality. The heavy, personal aspects of Kuzak’s work often make viewing a more private experience; his films are best shown on large screens in small theaters and on the laptops of curious viewers.

I heard about the program because a few friends, Carta Monir and Sweat (Sweatmother on Instagram), have participated in the residency and posted about their involvement with the foundation on social media. Another friend, Alannah Farrell, encouraged me to apply because he has worked with the foundation and suggested I would be a good fit.
Not that I can think of!

I feel it's helpful for me to mention here that I have a lot of experience with different aspects of sex work and I exist within a community of sex workers. Therefore, I have learned the tools necessary to handle facilitating a sex positive and safe environment. Not just for other participants but also for myself.

1). Layers of Leather, 2025, video, 44 minutes
A regular day in Paris – terrace, cigarettes, clouds lingering overhead. A couple makes their way back home, notice each others’ shoes, and begin to caress and tug at each other’s black leather jackets. An intimate episode hence unfolds between four acts — leather worship, needling, stapling, and fucking — in which sex and the idea of sex stretch to encompass a suite of absolute pleasure and resolute pain.
In Layers of Leather (2025), a transsexual fantasy between a loyal sub and his skilled dom perverts the very same tools modern medicine uses to treat, test, and true the bodies shared on screen. Director and co-star Gen Kuzak does not only take pain like a champ, but is poked and probed by the same syringe needles, medical staples, and latex gloves used so often in the medicalization of trans bodies. 

2). The French Boys, 2022, video, 43 minutes
After exploring butch4butch BDSM dynamics in their 2020 film American Pornography, filmmaker Gen Kuzak delves into trans fag sexuality with their latest work, The French Boys. A narrator is masturbating as they fall asleep… and in their dreams, Kuzak creates a space where actors can compose, freely, allowing them to show on screen what can’t be performed: vulnerability. By staging a real-life couple, The French Boys transcends the idea of sexuality as a performance or physical aesthetics, allowing us to be the witness of how love expresses itself.
Brightly light, minimal staging, and absolute freedom on screen, The French Boys is a new kind of hybrid, showing that sex can be raw and direct, and still be extremely moving, perfectly illustrating that fisting is an act of love.

3). Transmasculine Hole of the Universe, 2023, video, 35 minutes
In Transmasculine Hole of the Universe, the lines between fantasy and reality are blurred — this appears as an interview conducted by the director between two trans men. The video, a trans man’s fantasy, opens with frank discussions, such as testosterone injection sites marked by tattoos, then leads to sex. This sight of pleasure takes on a life of its own, as the director and performer's lust makes a tongue-in-cheek metaphor for the universe, blackholes, the hole in the middle of a tattoo through which testosterone flows.

Kaden Bard Dawson (b. 1999, Bisbee, Arizona) is a queer Hispanic artist based in New York City whose interdisciplinary practice spans photography, film, performance, and installation. Their work explores how materiality, time, and the body shape memory, intimacy, and visibility within queer experience. Using alternative photographic processes, including cyanotype and sustainable dyeing, they transform digital imagery through tactile experimentation that foregrounds process and transformation. They consider the erotic as a site of power and care, examining how gesture and desire act as forms of preservation and resistance. Their film and performance extend these concerns into embodied space, positioning the image as a living record of connection and queer lineage.
I am vegan and have no other special requirements at this time.

In researching my queer artistic lineage, I began studying artists such as Robert Mapplethorpe, George Platt Lynes, PaJaMA, Jimmy DeSana, David Wojnarowicz, and Peter Hujar, which led me to the work and legacy of Tom of Finland. I later learned more about the Foundation and its Artist-in-Residence program through the Talk Art podcast, where they discussed TOM House as a safe, welcoming space for queer artists to create and explore. Since then, I have been closely following the program and waiting for the opportunity to apply.

Access to the Tom of Finland Foundation archives would directly support my research into reconnecting queer lineage, particularly through queer erotic art produced during the AIDS crisis. I am interested in studying photographs, ephemera, and works that were created under conditions of censorship, loss, and resilience. I plan to place selected archival materials in dialogue with my ongoing project Flowers of Preservation, which pairs inherited family photographs with ephemera from chosen queer relationships. Curating these materials in parallel would honor artists who came before me and activate the archive as a living, evolving conversation.

Flowers of Preservation is an ongoing body of work that explores queer lineage, inherited memory, and joy through performance, quiet altar making, and photographic documentation. The project began from my relationship to absence. My uncle, Shawn Patrick Dawson, died of AIDS at thirty in New York City. His life was largely erased through family silence and the political conditions that shaped the AIDS crisis. Learning about his death at a young age taught me that queerness was dangerous, but it also tethered my identity to a lineage I could not see yet felt deeply. I carry this inherited grief and its resilience into my work.

The project centers on performance as a method of materializing lineage and preserving the ephemeral. In one iteration, I performed with queer elder Blane Charles, cofounder of ACT UP Montreal. Sitting cross legged between small altars, we blew dandelions into the space between us. This gesture of remembrance bridged generations through breath, fragility, and care. The performance was intentionally undocumented, allowing memory itself to become the archive. This work marked a shift in my practice from static image making toward embodied action as a way to rebuild queer bonds shaped by erasure.

TOM House holds deep significance as both a site of creation and preservation within queer cultural history. During the residency, I plan to engage directly with the Foundation’s archives, particularly AIDS era erotic art, personal papers, and ephemera created under conditions of censorship and loss. These materials will inform new performances developed in conversation with the house itself, treating the space as an active witness to queer survival, pleasure, and resistance.

Within TOM House, I envision creating intimate, site responsive performances and altar like installations that draw from both archival materials and my own collected ephemera. These gestures will explore how eroticism, intimacy, and chosen family functioned as tools of survival during the AIDS crisis and continue to shape queer life today. The residency’s emphasis on living with the archive allows memory, embodiment, and daily ritual to inform the work in ways that cannot happen in a traditional studio setting.
The residency would provide the space, time, and critical environment necessary to refine my performance practice and expand my photographic methodology through video and installation based documentation. Through sustained engagement with TOM House, I aim to produce work that honors queer ancestors while activating intergenerational connection as a living, shared experience rooted in place.
Multimedia arts ~ fiber art, documentary film, site-specific installation, literary arts

Aubrié Costello (they/them) is a Philadelphia-based multidisciplinary visual artist, director, writer, and curator whose work centers the urgency of collaboration, queerness, catharsis, and collective healing. Their practice moves fluidly across fiber art, film, installation, sound, and participatory archives, creating emotionally resonant spaces where vulnerability is not only welcomed but necessary. Costello’s projects are rooted in co-authorship, prioritizing lived experience, intimacy, and care as essential artistic methodologies rather than byproducts of process.

Through large-scale silk text installations, moving portraits, and public-facing activations, they explore how queer bodies hold grief, pleasure, resilience, and tenderness in a world shaped by precarity and erasure. Their work reframes softness as a radical, sustaining force and positions art as a tool for witnessing, release, and repair. Costello’s collaborative projects invite audiences to slow down, feel deeply, and recognize healing as a shared, ongoing practice.

TORN | Queer Sanctuaries is a multidisciplinary film/fiber/participatory archive project that documents where queer people go physically/emotionally/erotically for refuge. Conceived as an extension of THE EROTIC PROJECT, TORN understands the erotic as a vital life force. It is the embodied intelligence that connects desire, memory, grief, pleasure, and survival. In a world that repeatedly asks queer people to harden, disappear, or self censor, TORN insists on softness, intimacy, mutual care and feeling as necessary modes of resistance.

The project centers intimate interviews and moving portraits with queer participants, exploring questions of safety, longing, touch, fantasy, loss, and restoration. These conversations are not extracted or sensationalized. They are co authored exchanges rooted in trust, consent, and care. Participants speak to the places, rituals, bodies, relationships, and imagined worlds that function as sanctuaries. Lovers’ kitchens, public parks at dusk, libraries, clubs, bedrooms, memories, and moments of erotic aliveness become sites of reprieve from isolation. Through these narratives, TORN frames sanctuary as both a physical site and an internal state activated through connection, desire, and presence.

Visually, TORN weaves short form films with large scale silk text works and soft installations that hold language close to the body. The silk banners and fabric portraits function as tactile witnesses, fragile in appearance yet resilient in materiality, mirroring endurance. The erotic emerges through breath, gaze, voice, fabric against skin, and the permission to name what feels good, what hurts, and what heals. Rather than separating sexuality from emotional life, TORN treats erotic consciousness as a pathway to catharsis, truth telling, and communal repair.

TORN also includes an interactive participatory component that invites visitors to physically engage with the work. Audiences are encouraged to tear paper and contribute their own reflections, memories, and confessions. This gesture echoes TORN’s central metaphor. Tearing is both a violent act and an empowering one, a cathartic act of tearing off a piece that is yours and claiming agency through rupture and release.

Within the context of the Tom of Finland Foundation residency, TORN finds profound alignment. Tom of Finland’s legacy protects erotic imagination as cultural history, artistic rigor, and political defiance. At TOM House, TORN would deepen its research through filming, writing, and installation development while engaging the Foundation’s archive as inspiration and lineage.

Ultimately, TORN | Queer Sanctuaries is a living archive that affirms erotic feeling as essential, queer intimacy as knowledge, and sanctuary as something we build together again and again.

The TOM archives is both historical lineage and creative catalyst. I am particularly interested in studying Tom of Finland’s original drawings, personal correspondence, and intergenerational ephemera to understand how erotic imagery functioned as survival, affirmation, and coded communication during periods of heightened repression like today. Research will inform the visual/emotional tone of TORN, especially around how desire, erotic fantasy, and the queer body operate as sites of refuge. I will treat the archive as living record, studied and dialogued with through film/text/installation to reflect us now and to imagine forward.

What I'd love to share is that this work comes from lived necessity, not theory. I am making TORN | Queer Sanctuaries because I know what it feels like to search for safety, softness, and erotic aliveness in a world that often withholds them from queer bodies. I know the loneliness, the grief, the pleasure, the survival strategies, and the quiet rituals we invent to keep ourselves intact. This project is my way of honoring those strategies and offering them back to community with care.

This residency feels urgent to me because time matters. Many of the stories, desires, and bodies that shaped queer erotic culture are disappearing or being flattened by algorithms, shame, and erasure. The Tom of Finland Foundation protects something rare: a lineage where erotic imagination is not separate from dignity, tenderness, and political resistance. Being in that space would allow me to listen more deeply, slow down, and make work that is braver and more honest than I could elsewhere.

I am not seeking permission to be erotic. I am seeking responsibility. Responsibility to history, to the people who trusted me with their stories, and to future queer artists who deserve to inherit evidence that softness, pleasure, and vulnerability are not indulgences but survival tools. If invited into this residency, I will show up with care, rigor, humility, and deep gratitude, ready to make work that honors the love that made me possible.

My Bed ~ Depression, Submission, + Solitude | The one commonality is my bed. My bed is my longest and most complicated relationship. It’s noon and I’m still in my bed. When I was little it was my childhood bed, then my best friend’s bed, then later my boyfriend’s bed, now my bed in South Philly. Today isn’t particularly unusual or noteworthy even. I hear the street from my cracked open window and remove the pillow off my face. The weight and pressure of it brought me comfort throughout the night and through the morning while others began their day. Now I’m awake in my bed, exposed to the daylight. I lay there for a minute and look out the window at the open sky, broken by wires and telephone lines, trying to breathe it in. I watch a flock of pigeons scatter and awkwardly fly up into it. Will I be able to get up? Climb out? Will today be different? I grab for my phone and scroll instead before coaxing myself out of bed with the promise and comfort of hot coffee. 

He led me to his bed. There’s a dim colored light on. He slowly touches my thighs then palms my ass. He massages my thighs and ass for sometime. He slides his hands up and down and when he reaches my waist line, he tugs at the elastic of my panties, then slowly lowers them down, removing them. He continues to rub my body like he’s worshiping it. He starts teasing his big hands between my ass cheeks and slowly reaches between with his thick fingers, touching my wetness. He moans with both pleasure and agony and so do I. The anticipation is stronger than the act itself. He takes his fingers and has me taste myself and add my saliva to his hands. He enters me and we both sigh in total relief. His hands that were cupping my wetness raising. He slides his dominant hand up over my breasts until he reaches my soft neck. He invites me to look into his dark eyes as he tightens his grasp on my neck, the neck he was just softly kissing and whispering into. As he stares, he closes his hand even harder, taking away my ability to breathe. He watches me very carefully until my skin turns flush and red. Then he releases. I gasp for air. He strokes my hair and gently kisses my open mouth. “Breathe,” he says. “Breathe,” he encourages. Then he asks me if I’m ready again. I look into his eyes and say yes. He slaps me like a newborn. I offer my trust and give away my power and invite him to give me life or take it away. He repeats this ritual over and over, waking me up and putting me down, until I have fully surrendered, removed from thoughts, fear, present, wet and hot in his bed. This feels sacred. Cathartic. Our secret. I savored. This was church. You were my Savior. (excerpt from the SOFT Book written by me, Aubrié Costello)

Conceived and created by Aubrié Costello with collaboration from Pig Iron Theatre’s Dito van Reigersberg, Invisible Ink featured contributions from 14 individuals who bravely share their stories of love and vulnerability within Philadelphia’s queer community. Visitors were encouraged to take their time in this reflective space, contributing their own experiences to the growing narrative and connecting with the stories of others. This powerful exhibit was documented by videographer Rosie Simmons and photographer Gabriel Rosario. The installation was free and open to the public, supported by the William Penn Foundation.

COSTELLO4: Mockup for TORN | Queer Sanctuaries, 2026, TORN is a soft, intimate, queer-centered, participatory installation exploring vulnerability, communal catharsis, and collective authorship through immersive soft safe space, video, sound, text, and rest. While the word torn can feel violent, it also signals release, transformation, empowerment, and agency. In this work, I explore how tearing off one’s piece of a whole to claim truth, mark presence, and share story reflects the nuanced queer experience, one shaped by fragmentation but reinforced through intention, community, and mutual care. TORN will be housed within a dedicated gallery or project space and feature two looping video projections. One presents intimate interviews with local queer individuals speaking to love, loss, identity, grief, and joy. The second projection features tranquil nature footage paired with responses to the question: “Where do you go to feel seen?” These projections face each other, creating a dialogue between emotional truths and nervous system regulation. Floor-to-ceiling sheer chiffon drapery, soft lighting, fans, and subtle soundscapes create a calming, breathing environment. At the center of the room, a low futon Japanese mattress with silk bedding invites rest and reflection. Audience members participate by tearing a piece of white paper, writing their response to the central question, and leaving it within the space. These anonymous contributions will later be compiled into a zine documenting collective queer visibility and shared vulnerability, transforming the audience into co-authors of the work. Dimensions TBD upon site.

Portrait artist with a keen eye for inner life and current discourse; as an active networker in key photography associations, I combine artistic practice with community and visibility. In my series, I explore identity and intimacy and work experimentally—from light architecture to material interventions to collaborative setups—to make inner processes visible. My work is created in dialogue and remains precise without closing off the space for genuine encounter. In addition to my own practice, I teach photography and visual methods and am active in political and cultural education: from projects with young people in prison to formats in senior citizen work. This educational connection strengthens my eye for context and creates approaches that make images comprehensible and effective. As a networker, I promote cooperation between artists, institutions, and the public so that relevant topics are addressed. The goal is a visual language that touches, provides orientation, and opens up spaces for conversation.

Project Description — “Serendipity Portraits: Conditions for the Unforeseen”

I propose a portrait‑driven project that cultivates serendipity—creating conditions where the unforeseen can ethically emerge and be seen. Rather than “letting randomness happen,” I design light, space, and interaction to invite chance: concise setups, mobile lighting, simple material interventions, and brief collaborative exercises that open a field for genuine encounters. My artistic language and trained eye provide structure; situations are composed, not chaotic.

In dialogue with Tom of Finland’s legacy—celebrating desire, presence, and the politics of visibility—I will explore intimacy and identity across queer and allied communities in Los Angeles. The residency’s unique ecosystem (the house, the archives, the garden, and the network) becomes both site and collaborator. I will work in three modes:

1. Studio Serendipity: Short, iterative portrait sessions at the House using shifting light “architectures” and prompts that allow subjects to co‑author moments.  
2. Field Encounters: Micro‑sessions in everyday spaces (bars, parks, rehearsal rooms) to let the city’s rhythms disrupt and enrich composition.  
3. Archive Dialogues: Visual responses to selected archival materials—pairing new portraits with gestures, textures, and motifs from Tom’s drawings and ephemera, acknowledging influence without imitation.

Ethics and access are central: consent is ongoing, participants choose levels of exposure, and I share work‑in‑progress in small viewings to build trust and refine approach. My experience in teaching and in political/cultural education—from projects with incarcerated youth to senior programs—supports sensitive, inclusive facilitation.

Outputs will include a cohesive series of portraits (prints and high‑resolution files), a small artist‑book dummy, and an intimate salon presentation at the House. The aim is to render the “conditions for the unforeseen” visible: how light, touch, and context shape honesty in images; how community co‑authors what can be shown. By situating new work within the Foundation’s living archive, the project contributes a contemporary layer to ongoing conversations about desire, care, and the right to be seen.

I will treat the archives as engines of chance. By compiling places, people, and stagings in a “lexicon,” I will design micro-reenactments that invite chance, in which pose, light, and proxemics change with the subject of the participants. New portraits will be created in dialogue with archival templates, but will remain open to the unexpected and will then be paired with selected references to reveal continuities, breaks, and sudden developments—and to show how queer desire and queer representation persist, diverge, and reconfigure themselves through collaborative encounters and archival conversations.

I use photography, AI, sculpture, performance and installation.

Arvida Byström (b. 1991 Sweden, Stockholm) is a digital native with an intrinsic relationship to pink. Her work is rooted in ideas that deal with the internet and its social, aesthetic and commercial implications. She is known for employing a hyper-feminine aesthetic to explore themes concerning femininity, body image, social dynamics, emerging technologies and economic principles through primarily a lens based practice including, performance and sculpture work.
Previously living in London and Los Angeles she is now based in Paris where she travels in an aesthetic universe of disobedient flesh, fruits in lingerie and AI embodiment and disembodiment. Her photography, performances and sculptures have been in art shows all over the world like V&A London, TATE, as she starred both behind and in front of the camera of numerous influential brands and magazines. 2024 year her work "In The Clouds" got featured as one of the most defining projects of the year by Art News.

I want to create a contemplating AI porno that will be posted to pornhub. The main character will be a younger version of myself, asking the existential question: what can you do to others through AI, even if this other person is yourself just 10 years ago?
The AI me will be contemplating her Frankenstein-like existence, created by thousands of beautiful women; just like Frankenstein’s monster she is abandoned by her creator, the big AI companies and tech conglomerates suppressing bodies.
Taking inspiration from Philippe Parreno’s and Pierre Hyuhge’s work “No Ghost Just A Shell” where the two artists invited peers to create contemplative videos with an anime stock character, existing as a shell to be bought and filled with the buyers intentions. Leaving the character as a machine, a feminine entity, to exist for other people's usage.

The video also keeps examining my ongoing work with feminine AI. From the video Disembodied Daughter (2018), where iPhone’s Siri contemplates her forced disembodiment, to my live interviews with the AI sex doll Harmony, as well as the video with the doll which examines AI’s capacity to question human superiority and the fragility of the corporeal. This trajectory continues through In the Clouds and Abyss. In In the Clouds, I fed my own images into an AI-powered undressing deep-fake website and circulated the generated nudes as though they were real, interrogating digital objectification while playing with how these technologies produce something stranger than human. Abyss pushes this further: AI-generated hyper-feminine bodies glitch into stretched, fragmented abstractions, where desire meet distortion. Together these works map how AI reshapes both the aesthetics of femininity and the conditions of visibility for bodies online.

I hope to get some time to research the intersection of art, body horror and erotics and having access to such a vast and important erotic archive will be especially important for a deeper understanding of what I am working on. I think this can be both visually stimulating and inspirational as well as providing me with a more complex understanding of the history of erotic art. 
A lot of inspiration for the topics surrounding my art tends to be on research and challenging norms regarding our bodies, I have no doubts that the Tom Of Finland foundation can provide me with even more depth on these topics.
I am a vegetarian and I hope to bring my small dog Pedro.

DL Alvarez makes work that stands on a bridge between oral histories and visual art. Their art celebrates states of betweenness and intersections. Befittingly, Alvarez is non-binary, queer, mixed-race and peripatetic. They draw on this fluidity and a study of code switching to create dialogues between static works, moving images and the viewers themselves. They have exhibited at Kunsthaus Dresden, Berlinische Galerie, the Hammer Museum, the Berkeley Art Museum & Pacific Film Archives, the NY MoMA and the 48th Venice Biennale. For the Tom of Finland House they are embarking on a series of collage-inspired drawings that illustrate their early years as a BDSM Sexworker. Pro Dom work was a way for Alvarez to finance their artistic aspirations as an emerging artist, and this reflection on those years is their way of honoring beginnings in general while literally embarking on a new chapter of life and work that embraces pleasure.

I’d like to use the time at the Tom of Finland House to work on illustrations for ‘Guarded View,’ a book which tells the story of a Professional Dominant working in San Francisco in the mid-nineties. The writing is funny, sexy and touching, and was just awarded the Bo Huston Literary Prize. The illustrated version is on track to be published late in 2026 by the very queer-positive and international press, Publication Studio. My goal is to complete at least twelve 18x12 inch drawings during the residency and probably more. The drawings take about a week to ten days in “normal” circumstances, so I anticipate my output will be greater given the pure artistic focus your house provides. 

These drawings will have collage-like elements in that borrow formatting styles from nonfiction books (biographies, histories, true crime, etc.) where several images are crowded onto each page, often clumped in sections (this book will have four such sections). To capture the hectic lives of young creative queers living and working during the AIDS pandemic, the writing is done in small bits that play into the comedy-of-errors-style of constant hat-changing that took place. The narrator bounces from a water sports client to a museum exhibition opening, from a caretaking situation to kinky sex on his on own watch to an ACT-UP protest, from a nightclub to a wrestling scene with a regular client who likes to be called “Grandma.” The drawings will echo this organized chaos, in which the room the storyteller has just left follows him into the next.

Because it is the Tom House, I want to make the sexiest of the illustrations there, which include a range of BDSM scenes: a security guard spanking his captor, a medical exam that gets intrusively intimate, a cock and ball torture scene, a man who—while not pretending to be a dog—needs to be treated as such, temporary piercings, fucking outdoors, whipping/flogging and so forth. In short, lots of good fun.

If possible, I’d love to hold a reading of a small sections from the book when the drawings are shown to place them in their context.

I’ve been a long-time fan of Tom’s work: impeccable drawings, sublime watercolors! Those early watercolors bring enormous joy. I’ve redrawn those figures in personal sketch books over the years and want that spirit of the personal to push forward in my next chapter. 

But, the primary inspiration for this proposed series are Tom’s collages and source books. I’m eager to see some in person. I want to base my drawings on these collages. The idea of placing men who inspired Tom into my own drawings is exciting in a way that speaks to my history-loving, horny heart.

I’ve heard about the house over the years in various ways. The first one to let me know you existed was the artist Richard Hawkins. Years later I got a more detailed description of the Tom of Finland Foundation’s Artist-in-Residence Program from a friend of mine who participated: Florian Hertz. But what really put you on my radar, and made me want to apply, was a tour I got there with a small group of friends that was set up by Bradford Nordeen and led by Sharp.

If it isn’t clear in the proposal, this project for me personally has two parts to it; there is the work—the illustrations for this book—I want to do inside that magical home and garden you have, but also I see this new series as a launching pad towards making a distinct shift in my priorities to concentrate on making things that give direct joy.

Nicolo Gentile is an artist and educator based in Philadelphia, PA. He holds an MFA in Sculpture from the Tyler School of Art and Architecture at Temple University. His work has been exhibited nationally and internationally in galleries, project spaces, and institutions across the United States and abroad. Gentile is a recipient of the Association of Public Art’s Art on the Parkway Competition and the Velocity Fund, supported by The Andy Warhol Foundation for the Visual Arts, and is currently an Artist in Residence at the Fitler Club. His sculptural and installation-based practice engages queer eroticism, masculinity, and the politics of the body, drawing from kink, fitness culture, and gay subcultural histories. In dialogue with archival practices and figures such as Tom of Finland, his work considers how erotic images, materials, and codes are preserved, inherited, and reactivated across generations shaped by HIV/AIDS and its aftermath. Gentile teaches at the Tyler School of Art and Architecture and has served as a guest lecturer at multiple universities.

My proposed residency project builds from my ongoing body of work, Coming, undone, while shifting its focus toward questions of influence, inheritance, and the transmission of queer erotic knowledge across generations. At the core of this project is an interest in how erotic images, aesthetic codes, and material languages circulate through time—how they are preserved, mythologized, diluted, or reactivated—and how artists inherit not only visual forms, but political urgency, grief, and desire.

Tom of Finland’s work occupies a foundational position within this lineage. His drawings did more than articulate a fantasy of hypermasculinity; they established a visual grammar through which queer desire could be recognized, shared, and survived during periods of profound repression and loss. For my generation—formed in the wake of the AIDS crisis—this inheritance is necessarily mediated: received through reproductions, archives, and cultural echoes rather than lived immediacy. This project considers what it means to work with that distance, and how archival material functions not as static history, but as an active, shaping force.

During the residency, I plan to develop a new series of sculptural and image-based works that operate as “screens” or filters—structures that frame, obscure, and fragment erotic imagery. These works extend my ongoing use of materials drawn from kink, fitness culture, and industrial architecture, positioning them as contemporary vessels for inherited visual codes. Rather than reproducing archival imagery directly, I am interested in how partial visibility, abstraction, and material translation can reflect the instability of memory and transmission. What survives intact? What becomes stylized, fetishized, or sanitized? What disappears altogether?

This project also responds to the Tom of Finland Foundation as both a living archive and a site of care. The Foundation’s role in safeguarding erotic art foregrounds the political labor of preservation itself—particularly for work historically deemed disposable, dangerous, or obscene. My proposal treats the archive not only as a research resource, but as a collaborator, shaping form, pacing, and restraint within the work.

Ultimately, this residency offers an opportunity to slow down and think critically about how queer artists position themselves in relation to their forebears: how desire is inherited without becoming nostalgic, and how erotic art can remain a site of resistance, pleasure, and imagination in the present.

During the residency, I will engage the Foundation’s archives—original drawings, papers, photographs, and ephemera—to study how erotic imagery circulated and survived censorship and crisis. I will examine recurring codes of masculinity, gesture, and display across decades of queer erotic art, using them to inform new sculptural “screens” and image-based works. The archive will act as both source and collaborator, shaping how fragmentation, opacity, and partial visibility operate within the work.

I have long followed the work of the Tom of Finland Foundation and was honored to be a finalist in last year’s Emerging Artist Competition in the Dual Figure category. My work was also included in the 2024 CLAW Benefit Art Show, curated in part by the Foundation, which offered a direct connection to its community and archival practices. This residency presents an opportunity to engage more deeply with the Foundation’s materials, history, and legacy, informing my ongoing exploration of queer eroticism, masculine representation, and the transmission of visual codes across generations.

My work is rooted in sculpture and installation and explores the shifting intersections of queerness, memory, and material culture. I’m particularly interested in how bodies—and the spaces they occupy—hold both historical and personal residue. Drawing from the materials of fitness, kink, and industry, I use steel, leather, aluminum, and archival imagery to investigate power, loss, and survival. Much of my work engages with the legacy of the HIV/AIDS crisis and the aesthetics that emerged in its wake, reframing these histories within the context of contemporary queer identity.

My practice operates in the messy space between presence and absence, strength and subjugation, discipline, grief, and desire. I’m interested in what gets held, what slips through, and how queer identity is constructed, crushed, and reassembled through material. Think: post-bathhouse industrial minimalism with a latex twist. I try to make objects that remember for us when the archive doesn't.

Collage refuses the neatness that assimilation demands. It embraces contradiction, multiplicity, and rupture—all things that queerness thrives in. In my work, collage becomes a way to hold desire and doubt at the same time, to create space for eroticism that doesn’t flatten into marketable identity. When everything queer gets filtered through rainbow logos and dating apps that ask you to pick a torso type, collage lets you say: no, I’m many things—some of them broken, some of them beautiful, some of them just barely holding together. And that’s hot. There’s eroticism in the layers, the cuts, the tension. That friction—that unstable beauty—is where resistance and ecstasy live. And that ecstasy doesn’t have to be polished—it can be raw, ambivalent, and still leave a mark.

I was a kid in the '90s, absorbing fear, shame, and strength in equal parts. Queerness was something whispered or catastrophized, depending on the channel. Coming of age in the shadow of the HIV/AIDS crisis shaped my understanding of community, intimacy, and embodiment. My work is grounded in that inheritance—of grief, resilience, and resistance—but also in the gaps it left behind. I’m interested in the architectures and aesthetics that held queerness during that time: gyms, bars, magazines, leather, metal, screens. By reactivating these materials and sites, I’m not just remembering; I’m not here to live in nostalgia.

I want queer futures that are messier, freer, sexier—where vulnerability isn’t punished and pleasure isn’t policed. I’m imagining alternative futures where queer pleasure and vulnerability are not just allowed but celebrated. I hope queer futures are fluid, communal, and unapologetically complex—where identity isn’t commodified but embodied and continually reimagined.

Collage is both a method and a metaphor. It’s how I understand my body, my history, and probably my personality. It’s a way of working that embraces fragmentation, layering, and contradiction—reflecting how queer lives are often assembled from incomplete histories, coded languages, and ephemeral connections; a manifestation of queer time: non-linear, always in fragments, stitched with care and contradiction.

During my residency at TOM House, I like to document gay fetish with focus on leather and body culture in Los Angeles. Through life-model sessions, personal encounters, and engagement with the Foundation’s archive, I aim to produce a body of work ranging from intimate studies on paper to more complex paintings on canvas. 
This project aligns with the Foundation’s mission, treating erotic art as a living, evolving practice—one that preserves history while actively contributing to contemporary gay culture.

As a London-based artist and costume designer working at the intersection of figurative representation, dress code, and desire, I approach the male form as both subject and collaborator. Over the past twenty years, I have extensively portrayed and documented the gay community, developing a practice rooted in trust, proximity, and sustained engagement. I have found that the most vital art emerges not only from technical skill, but from presence, encounter, and exchange between artist and model. 
Moments of dialogue—spoken or silent—infuse my work with immediacy, empathy, and life, grounding my commitment to work that honours the individual body while situating it within broader cultural and historical contexts.

My training includes a Master’s in Menswear from the Royal College of Art and professional experience as a costume and stage designer for ballet, contemporary dance, and opera. I also designed for leather brand MasterU and often designed and worked with leather myself. These disciplines deepened my understanding of garments as active agents: dress codes shape posture, behaviour, fantasy, and belonging. 
This dual focus on body and attire has led to a sustained investigation into fetish aesthetics and the visual languages through which identity, desire, and solidarity is communicated throughout the gay community.

The contemporary visual grammar of fetish—uniforms, leather, hyper-masculine codes—cannot be separated from the legacy of Tom of Finland. His work did more than depict erotic fantasy: it established a shared visual framework that continues to inform gay self-representation and collective imagination.

Having studied Communication Design at Folkwang University of the Arts, Germany, I received a bursary to continue at the Royal College of Art, London, earning a Master’s in Fashion Design. 
I work since as a visual artist and as a designer of stage and costume for ballet, contemporary dance and opera.
My artwork has been exhibited internationally across the UK, Germany, France, Italy, Greece, Finland, Australia, and the USA, with major exhibitions at Hockney Gallery and Henry Moore Gallery in London, Michael Commerford Gallery in Sydney, and the Kunstmuseum Bonn.
Clients include Rambert Dance Company, Birmingham Royal Ballet, Ballet national de l’Opéra du Rhin, and the Australian Dance Theatre. 
I also lecture at Regent’s University London and for many years have lead workshops at the British Museum, for which I received the Campaign for Drawing’s honorary Trailblazer Award.

I approach archives as a resource for research and critical engagement. At the Foundation, I aim to explore how contemporary gay fetish dress codes continue to be informed by Tom of Finland’s imagery, and how masculinity is constructed through posture, attire, and presence—both with and without sexual narrative. I am particularly interested in non-sexualized portraits and character studies, which allow comparison between historical and contemporary ideals of masculinity. Insights gained from the archive will be placed in dialogue with live encounters in Los Angeles, creating a dynamic interplay between past and present.
In 1999 I read about the Tom of Finland’s Emerging Erotic Artist Contest and participated. Ever since I hoped to find the opportunity to apply for the artist residency program.

Carlos Us Santiago is a Portland emerging artist whose practice explores the tension between intimacy and anonymity. As someone new to photography, his work is driven by curiosity rather than fixed methodology, allowing each project to evolve organically. He focuses on private moments and personal narratives around sexuality, often working with subjects who choose to remain unseen or partially obscured. Through this approach, he examines how vulnerability, desire, and identity can exist without full visibility.

Romantic Strangers 

Cruising is an art form, and dare I say, romantic. My proposed project would depict the art that is cruising, hoping to capture the non-verbal dance between two or many strangers. While cruising is something that primarily happens between people meeting for the first time, cruising can be both, intimate and primal acts of subtle glances and gentle body language that precursor a thrilling chase.

Combined with attention to the setting, like place and lighting, I will approach this project like I began my own photography journey - By walking up to strangers and asking to take their photo - Not showing their face, keeping their anonymity, and remaining strangers. Furthermore, keeping the subject’s identity anonymous helps the person showcase an aspect of themselves that otherwise they may keep a secret. Los Angeles will become my playground, and I will seek spaces and subjects to photograph, hoping for the experience to be as organic as possible.

While the majority of the homo-erotic images we digest focus on the adonis archetype, the focus of my project will be on the act of cruising rather than the physical appearance of the subject(s). I will showcase people of different ages, race, and body types to mirror what actually happens in cruising spaces and what the demographic actually looks like - It’s not just a fantasy.
I heard about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence on an Instagram story.

Yes! This experience, as an artist, is all new to me. I don’t have any formal education or training in the arts. While I’m still learning how to use my camera and editing software, interacting and getting to know other creatives has been beyond rewarding. There is so much I don’t know and there is so much I want to learn. It feels like I’m getting a second chance or an opportunity to redirect my path, and the residency would definitely help steer me in that direction. Having the chance to spend time in Los Angeles would also help me meet more like-minded people that would help my project - both, demographically and sexually. 

My Instagram account got banned and deleted by Instagram, so my current account is fairly new.

Having access to the TOFF archives would serve as a reference for my own work. Cruising is the main undertone of my photography, and having access to work that depicts a big aspect of gay culture, pre-technological advances, would be enriching in terms of stepping back in time and learning from Tom’s craft.

I write criticism, fiction, and creative nonfiction, often blending genres as a sort of queer literary inheritance that disavows traditional boundaries (think: Robert Glück or Gary Indiana.) Over the past decade, I’ve established myself within Chicago’s artistic and literary communities, documenting queer artistic expression and contemporary queer life through my writing. Last year I prioritized both my critical and fiction writing by participating in the Tin House Workshop and ACRE residency and more than doubled my output. The Tom of Finland artist-in-residence program would help me expand my network as both an artist and art critic and allow me to dedicate more time for research, experimentation, and writing.  

During this residency program, I would plan to accomplish two goals: 
1. Produce critical writing responding to queer artists whose work explores sexuality, gender, memory, and embodiment, using Tom of Finland’s archives as a resource for research and inspiration. 
2. Complete a manuscript of my debut novel Eventual Happiness, which follows a young woman who learns she has been romantically betrayed and launches herself into increasingly outlandish sexual encounters, examining the contemporary world of sex and desire with wry, incisive humor. 

I wrote an early draft of the novel in July 2025 while at ACRE, a two-week residency in Wisconsin, and was accepted to a weeklong residency at Gibraltar Point Centre for the Arts in March 2026 where I plan to continue writing. A Tom of Finland residency would provide the time and space for me to complete the manuscript and search for a developmental editor to help me shape the writing and connect me with a literary agent and/or publisher. 

This project will examine contemporary life through sex, love, and betrayal as a woman searches for meaning in the aftermath of separation. Similar to the aloof, disaffected divorcee of Rachel Cusk’s Outline trilogy; the insecure, itinerant poet of Ben Lerner’s Leaving the Atocha Station, or the dislocated narrator of Eliza Barry Callahan’s The Hearing Test, this novel unfolds through comical, often nauseating conversations with men in bars, hotel rooms, and internet DMs. These conversations reveal unlikely truths about the motivations of these men and often diverge into combative discussions about art, infidelity, self mythology, world events, and the possibilities of love.

As a critical and fiction writer, I am inspired by the lineages of queer writers such as Robert Glück, Gary Indiana, David Wojnarowicz, etc., who documented and responded to the art of their friends, enemies, and lovers and captured snapshots of queer communities that otherwise would have been lost to history. I am interested in using the foundation’s archives as a research site to inform my own critical writing, drawing connections between contemporary artists and those represented in the archives, and as a source of inspiration for my creative writing to examine contemporary queer life.

My photography is cinematic and confrontational, built on shadow, intention, and presence.

I use darkness the way others use light. Faces emerge with weight, not softness. Each portrait feels like a frame pulled from a film that refuses to explain itself. The glamour in my work is earned—raw, textured, and unapologetically human.

My 1970s influence lives in confidence and posture. Styling is deliberate. Bodies take up space. Masculinity and vulnerability exist together without compromise. I don’t pose people to be pretty—I frame them to be seen.

There’s a ritual quality in my images: leather, skin, fabric, shadow—armor and invitation at the same time. I photograph people in the act of becoming, not performing.

At its core, my work is about reclaiming power.
Not loud.
Not decorative.
But undeniable.

Charlie received a BFA, with a focus on photography, from Otis Art Institute of Parsons School of Design in Los Angeles in 1989. Photography is his main medium, but he also incorporates sculpture and collage. Constructed environments and playful sets are signatures of his work, which conveys narratives about identity formation, public and private selves, and how we navigate daily life. Charlie is a co-founder of the NY Queer Zine Fair and co-organizer from 2015-2018. He also was part of the Queer Action Figures collective (1994-96) and one half of KNOWSGAY, along with Paul Anthony Moreno. Charlie's projects include short films, collage, photography, objects and zines and he draws inspiration from dirty sailors, fancy desserts and sad pop songs.

"Patroclus in the armor of Achilles," 2022, Artist Book, Edition of 75, 11 × 17 in

The artist book and related exhibition, “Patroclus in the armor of Achilles” pulls from the end of “The Iliad” when Patroclus, in an act of love and defiance, dawns the armor of Achilles (his lover, cousin, or brother; depending on the translation) and goes into battle ‘as’ him, dying in the ensuing fight. The rage Achilles feels turns the tide of the Trojan War but also precipitates his own death. With this queer love story as foundation, I use the armor as metaphor for exchange, connection, touch, and love throughout the artist book and accompanying works.

The exhibition pictured contained the artist book and a series of related works which grew from the project.

--

"Backdrop for a picnic in the Mouth of Hell," 2025, Silkscreen on canvas, 9 × 12 ft

This backdrop draws inspiration from the Sacro Bosco (Garden of Bomarzo) in Italy, where volcanic stone is carved into a landscape populated by mythic beings, monstrous faces, and grotesque figures. I wanted to create a setting for a yet-to-be-realized pornographic film, imagining the actors cruising through this garden of monsters. Referencing the magenta-turn that occurs as film stock decays, the backdrop adopts a pink-hued saturation, aligning the fantasy of the scene with the material afterlife of analog film.

--

"An Architecture of Seduction," 2025, Architectural fragment incorporating vintage 8mm film, 10 × 8 x 1.5 ft

This work reconstructs a fragment of Fire Island’s “TV House,” a home designed by production designer Edwin Wittstein and art director Robert Miller in 1967. Drawing on the architectural language popularized by Horace Gifford, often described as an “architecture of seduction,” the modernist homes of Fire Island were designed to frame, expose, and direct the gaze of desire.

Upon entering the space, the viewer first encounters the mechanical whir of a projector before the image itself comes into view. The structure incorporates a 1980s pornographic film acquired from an anonymous lot of reels purchased on eBay. None of the films included titles, credits, or identified performers. Devoid of digital counterparts, they exist as fragile tethers to a history largely lost. The physical deterioration of the film, (most visible in its magenta tint), mirrors the fragility of the bodies depicted and the precarity of the histories they represent. An excerpt of the film is physically threaded into the architectural fragment, entwining the histories of the home and the film.

--

“Porn Archive” (Ongoing), 2025-Ongoing, Digital scans of 8mm + Super 8mm Film, Variable

Out of a need for my own queer materials, I have begun to salvage and purchase Super 8 and 8mm pornographic films from online stores and private sellers. Over the past year I have started digitizing these works, stabilizing the footage and giving it a renewed digital life. I hope to continue accumulating this collection, sharing it with others, and incorporating it into my art practice. I see the films, in their various states of decay, as intimately connected to the bodies they depict; both celluloid and flesh exist as fragile tethers to histories that are increasingly difficult to recall. By offering the films a measure of temporary stability, I understand their viewing as a kind of provisional revival—a chance for them to be seen once more.
I am a research-based artist working between film, printmaking, sculpture, and installation.

Logan Larsen (b. 1998, Houston, TX) is an interdisciplinary artist and researcher with degrees in Studio Art and Art History from The University of Texas at Austin. His project-based practice spans printmaking, sculpture, publication, installation, and moving image. Larsen’s work examines devotion, intimacy, and longing through the study of queer archives, fan cultures, and visual histories. Treating the archive as both material and guide, his work explores how the materials of queer lives are collected, preserved, and revived across time. Larsen has exhibited with the International Print Center, New York; Northern–Southern Gallery, Austin, TX; the Visual Arts Center at The University of Texas at Austin; Space Gallery at the Pittsburgh Cultural Trust; and the National YoungArts Foundation, New York. He is currently pursuing an MFA at Carnegie Mellon University and is based between Pittsburgh, PA, and Austin, TX.

During this residency at the Tom of Finland Foundation, I plan to develop a series of drawings, collages, and sculptural studies responding to the home itself and the accumulated layers of queer materials embedded within its walls. These studies will serve as groundwork for larger sculptural works in which I will embed my own archival materials, allowing the sculptures to function as conduits and carriers of queer visual history.

I am interested in responding to the home’s accrued visual culture, remnant desire, and traces of past activity, with particular attention to how domestic space becomes a site where fantasy is enacted, preserved, and given material form. The Foundation’s home offers a unique convergence of intimacy, sociality, and care, where erotic life and daily ritual coexist. I approach this project wondering what it might mean to attend a sex party, spend the night, and wake to the smell of coffee in the morning: a space where desire lingers and is followed by prolonged aftercare. This intersection of erotic excess and shared domesticity will guide the development of the work.

My recent work has turned toward queer spaces that carry histories within their architecture. For a recent project, I reconstructed a fragment of a historic Fire Island home and embedded a vintage 8mm pornographic film within its structure, transforming the architecture into a pseudo-projector. The film contained no identifying markers—no publisher or performer names—and had undergone a magenta color shift indicative of its material decay. I understand the fragility of the film body as parallel to the fragility of the bodies on screen. Set on a beach, the footage reads as a possible visualization of the men who once inhabited the Fire Island home.

At the Tom of Finland Foundation, I aim to engage the home as a site of ongoing activation rather than static preservation. I am particularly drawn to spaces carved out for desire (the bedroom, the dungeon, the back room) as architectural records of intimacy and sociality. Conceptually, the project balances two impulses: the archival hand that seeks to preserve queer histories, and the desiring hand that wants to disturb, rearrange, and make a mess. The resulting works will consider how desire survives through material traces, and how archives remain alive through use.

Out of necessity, and in response to the absence of robust queer archives in the places I’ve lived, I have built a personal archive of queer materials shaped by my research interests and desires. During the residency, I plan to consult the Tom of Finland Foundation’s archive for materials beyond my reach, particularly films, drawing references used by Tom, and the Foundation’s extensive collection of queer printed matter. I will also treat the rooms of the house themselves as archival sites, closely observing, handling, and responding to the materials embedded within them.

I first learned about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence program through scholar Andy Campbell during a lecture promoting his book “Bound Together.” In discussing Tom of Finland’s artistic career and reception, Campbell also spoke about former resident P. Staff’s work developed in response to their time at the Foundation. His discussion of Staff’s inhabiting of the space and their intermingling of archival practice and desire helped shape my understanding of Tom’s legacy and motivated my interest in engaging the Foundation and its home as a site for artistic research.
No, nothing to share at this time.

As I enter the final year of my MFA at Carnegie Mellon University, opportunities for sustained and focused engagement are especially formative to my research and practice. A residency at the Tom of Finland Foundation would meaningfully inform the development of my forthcoming thesis work, allowing me to spend extended time with the Foundation’s archive and to inhabit a queer space uniquely aligned with the questions driving my practice. I am deeply interested in how this experience could shape the direction of my work beyond the residency itself. Thank you for your time and consideration.

Diego Cajas Parra is a Chilean-born artist and fashion designer based in New York, working under his eponymous label Diego Cajas. His practice treats fashion as art, using corsetry as an erotic language—one that shapes, restrains, and amplifies the body as a site of restriction and power. Moving between excess and control, his work moves through contradiction, mixing the masculine with the feminine, the extreme with the soft, the dark with the sacred. In this tension, the body becomes both exposed and protected, disciplined and desired. Influenced by his religious childhood, ballroom culture, underground erotica, and lived experiences of displacement as Latinx immigrant, Diego Cajas creates garments and performances that question gender, politics, normativity, and visibility. His work has been exhibited and featured in artistic and cultural spaces, including the Museum of Sex in New York. Situated within a lineage of queer erotic art, his practice insists on intimacy, guilt, pleasure and craft as acts of authorship, resistance, and self-definition.

“The Religious Belief That Pain Purifies the Spirit”

This project explores the relationship between pain, forgiveness, and purification through the body, examining self-inflicted bodily suffering as a ritual of punishment, devotion, and release from guilt. Drawing from religious practices in which pain is understood as a path toward spiritual elevation, the work treats endurance as a means of cleansing, resetting, and transcending the self through physical sacrifice.

The project is fundamentally framed by a rupture of gender. It displaces the corset—a historically feminized undergarment associated with restraint, intimacy, and the disciplining of female bodies—onto masculine, Latin, thug-coded figures traditionally read as virile, aggressive, and resistant to vulnerability. In this collision, femininity is not softened or erased but imposed and endured, destabilizing masculinity through force, submission, and bodily transformation.

The work unfolds as a visual presentation of extreme, impossible corsetry—corsets that cannot be worn, cannot be closed, and actively resist functionality. These sculptural objects are activated through a performative act of torture: the prolonged, violent process of putting on and cinching an extreme corset until the back finally meets and closes. What is traditionally hidden, intimate, and coded as feminine is exposed, strained, and forced onto bodies that are not meant to contain it.

Using corsetry as a tool, the project engages tight lacing, force, and tension to radically alter the human body—compressing muscle, shifting internal structures, burning skin through friction, and restricting breath to the edge of collapse. Within this intensity, pain becomes focus; endurance becomes meditation. The corset feminizes the body while simultaneously brutalizing it, producing an image that exists between devotion and humiliation, beauty and violence, submission and resistance.

Daily cinching and lacing function as a repetitive ritual, demanding discipline, controlled breathing, and psychological surrender in the pursuit of an inhuman, impossible form. The act echoes religious penance while also mirroring the social violence imposed on gender-nonconforming bodies—where transformation is achieved only through suffering.

Ultimately, the work presents corsetry as both torture device and sacred object. Pain is framed not as spectacle but as devotion—an intimate, cyclical act in which the body becomes altar and offering. Caught between masculinity and femininity, punishment and grace, collapse and transcendence, the project insists that beauty, holiness, and redemption can emerge precisely from the violent rupture of gendered expectations.

I will be working directly with the Tom of Finland Foundation archives to study how leather, fetish garments, and hyper-masculine bodies have been built visually as sources of power, desire, and restraint. I am especially interested in archival material that links masculinity with restraint, ritual, and discipline. This research will inform both the formal construction of my corsets and the performative framework of the project, as a means of recontextualizing archival codes of queer masculinity through gender rupture, endurance, and bodily transformation.

I am a photographer working primarily with analogue photography. My practice is rooted in the tradition of autobiographical and humanistic documentary photography. I am most interested in living and creating works that explore relatable intimacy, emotion, and lived experience through ordinary and diverse life.

I received my MFA from the Academy of Fine Arts, Uniarts Helsinki, and completed additional exchange studies at Beaux-Arts de Paris. I have held solo exhibitions at Villa Noailles (France), Malva – Museum of Visual Arts (Finland), Galerie Le Lieu (France), and Iittala & Arabia Design Gallery (Finland), and have distributed photography publications widely across Europe and Asia.

My work has been featured in i-D, AnOther, Dazed, Replica Man, British Journal of Photography, and Helsingin Sanomat, and I have collaborated with Iittala, Comme des Garçons, and Nike. I am currently developing a trilogy of autobiographical photobooks exploring my cross-cultural relationship with my Japanese partner. The first volume, Mountain (山), was published in 2025 and sold out its 1,000-copy print run within a few months, with distribution in over thirty art bookstores worldwide.

Dear Application Committee,

I am currently finishing the second photography book in a trilogy of autobiographical books. The first book, Mountain (山), was about love, desire, admiration, and the strong emotional impact of falling in love. It focused on the feeling of seeing another person from that perspective, and how it changes the way we see and feel in general.

The second book, Middle (中), is about being in between. It looks at a relationship as a space that is always moving, not fixed. There is no clear center, only the space between two people and constant change. The book also deals with time — how emotions are tied to time, how we cannot control it, and how life moves forward whether we want it to or not. For me, this reflects impermanence, freedom, but also sadness.

The photographs for this second book were taken in places connected to my own life and to European history, such as France, Italy, Greece, and Finland. Through these places, I am trying to understand what being European means today — what it means to exist in between so many countries, cultures, and histories. I am also asking how we see European history and culture from our present life, through personal experience rather than big theoretical ideas.

During the residency, I want to finish editing the second book, take care of printing it, and prepare its launch with an exhibition in Los Angeles. Through this, I would be very interested in meeting people who are interested in both erotic art and Finnish culture, and hopefully building relationships with curators, galleries, institutions, and most importantly, peer artists, that I can grow with in the near and long-term future.

While staying at the house, I would also like to start a new set of photographs with my partner. These would be simple still-life images of the body, inspired by Touko’s drawings, and mixed with elements of the natural world and its forms: earth, sand, mud, milk, honey, and other natural elements. I imagine making these images using the house, the garden, and the light around it, because I feel that this place could offer creative freedom and a relaxed environment for creating nude images, which has not been available to us before.

First of all, I want to say that I really love Touko’s work and the way he represents desire, lust, and worship through the body. I am interested in studying how his work creates space for lust, tenderness, and curiosity at the same time. By reflecting on and studying the archive, I want to develop a small photographic series of around ten images that express similar emotions through a different visual language, while still being inspired by his drawings.

This new work, made in dialogue with Touko’s drawings, would explore how masculine desire can be shown today through photography, with natural elements incorporated into the images.

responses.answer: I have known Tom of Finland’s work since I was 16 and started studying in art school, so I would describe it as part of my education as Finnish homosexual. I did not know about the residency before Jaakko Nousiainen, the director of the Finnish Cultural Institute in Tokyo, referred me to Richard Villani and recommended that I apply.
I have no special requirements regarding accessibility. I have autoimmune condition which requires me to not eat grains containing gluten and oats.

Because I currently live and work very closely with my boyfriend, I hope that we could stay together in the small room. I applied for the small room because, at the moment, I am mainly responsible for our financial means. I cover most of our expenses, including food, possible car rental, and flights.

I do have strong grant support for my artistic work from the Kone Foundation, but since I support both of us, keeping accommodation costs low would make a real difference. Having the small room would allow me to use more of my resources for basic living needs like groceries, as well as for artistic costs such as film, developing, darkroom materials, and the production of the book.

Regarding room size, we have both lived for the past two years in very small apartments and usually travel with only our backpacks, so we truly need very little space.

If given the opportunity for the residency, I hope it would be possible to consider letting us both stay in the small room so we could choose the more affordable option. Thank you very much for your consideration.

I am a composer and artist exploring sound, site, and sexuality. My creative works queer the everyday, allowing us to experience things from new perspectives. My practice is situated between music, visual art, performance, and multimedia, and works have been performed and exhibited in 18 countries and 25 US States. My kink life is embedded in many works, often disarming people by offering vulnerability. My passions for science, travel, and storytelling have led to a Fulbright in Iceland, the McKnight Visiting Composer Fellowship, a project award from FEMA, and a New Music USA grant, among other residencies and awards. 
I hold my Doctorate of Musical Arts from CU Boulder and teach music composition at the University of Denver. I am currently an artist in residence at RedLine Contemporary Art Center and live in Denver with my husband Andy.
Through Tom’s work originally; then reading recently Nayland Blake’s older writing on Tom sparked my interest in looking up the Foundation’s website again, and I checked out the application dates.

So much of my work lies in being on-site, shifting organically based on personal stories and resources. Its transformative nature needs time to unfold rather than packing an immediate punch. Many pieces of mine are like a kink scene: sometimes a revelation happens once we have the ability to sit and soak in the moment.

I would like to use the archives directly as source material for lyrics, as well as images, objects, and scenes in photographs as jumping off points. I want to create a work that has historical accuracy and is based on real experiences and objects. 
The domestic life of the house itself will also be an inspiration- I believe the archive is also the mix of art and culture in a large city, the architecture of the building and the community around it. The stories and scenes from this “everyday” will be woven into my work.

I will create a kink performance piece for voice and electronics, directly drawn from Tom’s images, letters, ephemera, and home. In a classical sense it is a song cycle, and in the experimental sense it is a theater piece, with potential gear and sling/furniture. I’m asking: what queer experimental work can I create, borne out of Tom’s archive? And how (and why) does my leather identity integrate with my training as a classical composer and artist? 
I will make work that queers both “classical” music and BDSM practices. Our fantasies, our bondage and leather, and the erotic sounds from our toys and our bodies, are ripe for Art-making. Lyrics may imagine a life without discrimination or violence, the sadness in losing play partners, the mundanity of a leatherman paying taxes and taking out trash. 
Using electronics as accompaniment pays tribute to LGBTQ dance and fetish clubs, and to porn soundtracks of Patrick Cowley. The classical baritone voice has a respect for tradition and beauty, a powerful masculinity, grace and sensuality. 

I see connections between Tom’s work and my own. Tom loved finding ways to express his fantasies, to create archetypes of men. I envision utopian spaces within music and installation, where time is suspended and people are their most erotic selves without judgement. As queer men, we both advocate(d) for a life lived freely and proudly, despite censorship. Much of my work is self-produced as institutions shy away from it–while at the same time are quite titillated by it. Tom refused to work without passion or arousal–one of my career goals! Tom was surrounded by creative, intelligent, perverted minds. I would love to be in a community of supporters who understand my work and its purpose.

Much of my practice is suited to working in an archive, taking photos, journaling, and sketching. A musical instrument on hand is helpful, but not necessary. My work blends solitary time and social time; the Echo Park creative and kink community is part of the work. Researching Tom’s work makes him come even more alive. It would be a pleasure to premiere the piece through, or to send documentation back to, the Foundation once completed, as I know my work will have a long tail of benefits beyond my stay.

Daniel Rajcsanyi (1992*) fuses sculpture, fashion, intimacy and the body to create performances, photographs, and music. All these different mediums lead to the cosy world of production that is where his audience is taken. Daniel's practice usually comprises the body, either his or of friends and welding these to explore what this means when reconstructing the scene as well as analyzing what bodies mean in different environments, by his own journey of these portrayals Daniel examines the functions that different characters look from a social perspective. "Mocking life as a one-hit-wonder and nothing has any matter. The selfie breaks the rules of self-expression. A mock on the self-expression of these days. I want to defeat. Defeat the vision of the self and break this ideology of beauty. Passing out is my goal of high. An orgasm with every usage.“

My proposed project would be site-specific and developed in direct response to the location. My artistic practice is strongly dependent on place, and I am interested in exploring which materials, tools, and formal strategies become available or meaningful within a given context. Rather than arriving with a fixed concept, I work through observation, experimentation, and engagement with the surroundings.

My works often draw on local culture, spatial conditions, and the “language” of tools and materials that are specific to a place. I am particularly interested in how materials carry cultural memory and how making processes can reflect local histories, labor, and codes.

Conceptually, I would orient myself toward the essence of Tom of Finland—not in a literal or illustrative way, but as an attitude: confidence, desire, stylization, masculinity, and freedom of self-representation. This essence would serve as a loose framework rather than a predetermined outcome.

At this stage, I cannot give a definitive description of the final project. Much of my work emerges through play, chance encounters, and situational developments during the residency. I value this openness, as it allows the project to grow organically from the place itself and from lived experience within it.

Izabela Cookson (they/them) is an Brazilian American artist, photographer, and curator working between Miami, FL and Baltimore, MD. Their work is best described as a love letter, exploring the complicated notions of love through historical methods of photography, installation, stop motion animation, and artist books.

Their work has been exhibited across the US in museums, galleries, and fairs in group shows at ICA Miami (2021, 2022), New Artists Dealers Association Miami (2023), Wilgus and Main Zero Gallery in Baltimore (2025- 2022), and Vox Populi Gallery in Philadelphia (2025). Most recently, Cookson received a YoungArts Microgrant to fund their project Death Drop, about drag culture and its intersection with American funerary rituals as a way to probe and make comments about identity and the language we use to describe it and was granted a solo show to exhibit the work at The Great White Wall Gallery in November of 2025.
