During my time at the residency, I plan on continuing to work on my series "Death Drop", body of work that uses the language and aesthetics of drag culture to understand the making of queer identity. While the term “death drop” refers to the dance move, the term stemming from fans of RuPaul's Drag Race but originating from Ballroom culture, I understand it to serve as a figure of speech for when genderqueer, gender-nonconforming, and nonbinary people shed the identity and its expectations imposed on them at birth. It represents the bold and brave revelation of self; an identity they have consciously and confidently constructed, reflecting their personal interpretations of gender and selfhood. Examining the fluid boundaries between portraiture, character study, and caricature, Drag, in its exaltation and deconstruction of gender expectations, becomes a space where identity becomes a performance and extension of self, complicating the traditional definitions of photographic representation. The act of drag transformation blurs the distinction between what is "real" and what is "performed", challenging the rigidity of photographic labels. By capturing these personas, I explore the fluidity of identity, the interplay of performance and self-revelation, and the ways in which drag serves as both a form of empowerment and a means of self discovery. 

This body of work is born out of my love of drag and the profound role it has played in my understanding of gender as a nonbinary person. Drag has shown me the joy that comes with living freely and boldly, wether that be in a wig and full face of makeup, or in the absence of adornment. The word death drop recontextualizes the dance move to describe that self-realization.

Through the residency, I plan on continuing to expand the series and making more photographs that more explicitly incorporate death rituals and funerary traditions into my collaborations with drag performers. While these elements have always been central to the conceptual framework of Death Drop, their visual representation has been limited by time, resources, and access. The residency would provide the space and support necessary to realize this aspect of the project more fully, due to its location, allowing me to connect with a broader network of drag performers, as well as with open-minded funeral homes and death professionals willing to engage with the work and allow me to photograph within their spaces—opportunities that were not available to me in Baltimore. These environments are essential to the project’s exploration of transformation, self-construction, and mortality, and would allow the series to more deeply examine drag as a catalyst and example of transformation, change, and visual deviation from rigid labels.

My background in photography and art history is rooted in a desire to engage critically with visual lineages and to build upon the work of those who came before me. The Tom of Finland Foundation archives offer vital context for Death Drop, particularly through representations of self-fashioning, resistance, and unapologetic visibility. By studying drawings and photographs in the archive, I can push my work toward a more direct, embodied, and non-passive representation of queerness. Engaging with the archive allows historical self-determination and community to be reactivated and carried forward through contemporary photographic form.

answer: I am available all year with a preference for the fall / winter.

responses.answer: Interdisciplinary arts - Photography, video, sound, text, and archival research.

Archive of Almosts 1950 to 1985 examines the mid century erotic print systems that circulated queer desire before liberation and before the AIDS crisis. My recent work already uses historical queer images as living material rather than closed documents, treating vintage erotica, devotional motifs, and vernacular print culture as mechanisms of erotic transmission. This positions me to activate the Foundation’s archive rather than simply consult it, extending an ongoing body of work rather than initiating a new one.

A recent moving image work, LOVEBEAT, extends this research into contemporary vernacular erotics by recontextualizing color-treated amateur athletic footage alongside archival sporting material. Undressing, exercise, and physical demonstration become sites of erotic pedagogy in which desire circulates through technique, imitation, and gaze. The residency would deepen this moving image strand through direct engagement with the Foundation’s archive.

From 1950 to 1985, queer erotic culture relied on amateur physique photographs, mail-order catalogs, small-run magazines, explicit classifieds, and anonymous submissions that rarely entered art history. These materials did more than represent desire. They modeled techniques, gestures, and postures through which erotic knowledge was transmitted, a form of queer erotic pedagogy in which desire circulated through instruction, imitation, and repetition. They also functioned as shared signals through which queer lives recognized themselves across distance, risk, and coded circulation. The contributors who made and distributed this material, including models, photographers, designers, printers, and hobbyists, were rarely credited. Their work forms a field of almosts: almost artists, almost publishers, almost historians, almost communities.

Working within the Foundation’s archive, I will scan and study selected materials and use their visual and editorial structures as the basis for new photographs, video, and sound. During the residency, I plan to complete a moving image work and a focused series of photographic interventions that can be shown in dialogue with archival material. A hybrid exhibition at Tom House will stage archival fragments alongside new work. Video introduces temporalities of circulation, including delay, anticipation, and repetition, while sound allows intimacy and longing to register beyond the visible.

Archive of Almosts reframes mid century erotic print culture not as static evidence of the past but as deferred proposals for queer futures interrupted by censorship and the AIDS epidemic. By activating these materials within contemporary practice, the project positions Tom House not only as a custodian of history but also as a transmitter of future erotic imaginaries.

Paul Beaudoin is a queer interdisciplinary artist and composer working across photography, video, sound, and text. His practice examines how queer desire, intimacy, and memory persist across time, with particular attention to minor visual histories through which erotic culture circulates and survives. Drawing from archival fragments, devotional imagery, and vernacular print ephemera, Beaudoin treats historical queer images as living material for contemporary inheritance rather than closed documentation of the past. His work has been exhibited internationally in museums, festivals, and galleries, including recent presentations at the Pride Museum in Brussels and KinoMural in Prague, and is represented within contemporary queer art networks through Newman Gallery in Berlin and The Black Box Gallery in London. Born in the United States and now based in Tallinn, Estonia, he holds a PhD in music composition and theory. His current projects focus on intergenerational erotic transmission through a hybrid lens-based installation.
I do not eat any seafood.

The Foundation’s archive holds exactly the materials this project needs: vintage physique photographs, small-run magazines, explicit classifieds, mail-order catalogs, and anonymous submissions that sustained queer erotic culture outside official institutions. During the residency, I will review and scan selected materials and use their visual and editorial logics as source material for new photographs, video, and sound, with close attention to the ethics of working with anonymous and vulnerable contributors. This work generates new artistic output and brings renewed attention to people who were never credited in their own time.

The materials preserved at Tom House emerged from a period when queer erotic culture survived through private circulation, coded distribution, and informal networks of transmission. That history has renewed relevance today as censorship, book and image bans, and legislative targeting threaten queer visibility in the United States and elsewhere. Archive of Almosts approaches mid-century erotic print culture not as static evidence of the past but as deferred proposals for queer futures. The residency enables their activation through new artistic work, extending the Foundation’s legacy into intergenerational sharing, contemporary erotic production, and cultural survival. 

I would also welcome the opportunity to share my work through an artist talk or public walkthrough at Tom House during the residency.

I learned about the residency through queer art networks, Foundation programming, and conversations around Tom of Finland’s cultural legacy. As my current work directly engages mid-century erotic print culture, the residency aligns with an emerging direction in my practice, making this the right moment to apply.

responses.answer:
I was born in Naples, Italy, in 1995. I studied Fine Arts at the Academy of Fine Arts in Naples, earning my BFA and Master’s.

I later moved to London, where I completed postgraduate studies in Museum Cultures at Birkbeck, writing my dissertation, "Do Men Have to Be Dressed Up to Get Into Museums?", on the representation of male nudity in museum contexts. I studied Art and Business at Sotheby’s Institute of Art. I am based in London.

My work has been shown internationally in exhibitions, cultural events, and philanthropic projects. I’ve been invited to create my works live as a performance, developing my depictions of naked men in real-time before an audience at art events.

My pieces have been exhibited across Europe, the United Kingdom, and the United States.

I learned about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program through Volker Morlock, a collector of my work and a long-time supporter of the Foundation. I also spoke with artist Rinaldo Hopf, who shared his positive experience as a former resident and encouraged me to apply.

My practice centers on drawing nude male figures situated in unexpected environments. Their nudity is deliberate and defiant, placing the body, freed of inhibitions, where it is not usually permitted and unsettling the social codes attached to public space. These nonconforming figures exist in a state of exposure that reflects both desire and unease: pleasure is present, but so is friction. As a queer artist, I am interested in how the nude body can function as a form of quiet rebellion, one that is inviting dialogue rather than limited to spectacle.

The project I propose would be developed in close relation to the Tom of Finland House, beginning with the back garden area inspired by Tom’s story: Pleasure Park. I approach Pleasure Park as both a physical site and a conceptual framework - a state of mind. It is a protected space where pleasure is cultivated, shared, and defended, and also where the erotic is understood as a serious and generative force.

Working from this environment, I would develop a new series of drawings in which nude male figures carry the psychological space of Pleasure Park beyond the house and into Los Angeles. The figures are placed within recognizable urban and natural settings, but they remain marked by the confidence and freedom rooted in the house itself. In this way, the work maintains a clear point of origin while allowing that sensibility to expand outward.

Time spent at the house (drawing, observing, and engaging with its rhythms) would be central to the process. I am interested in participating in the Foundation’s monthly drawing sessions and in exchanges with the other residents, allowing shared presence and conversation to shape the work, like a polysymphonic Pleasure Park, as much as solitary studio time. I am also open to moments of live drawing, where the act of making becomes visible and participatory.

The resulting works of art would form a body of drawings inseparable from Tom of Finland House and Pleasure Park: shaped by its spaces, its archive, and its role as a nurturing site for queer pleasure, creativity, and community.

My engagement with queer imagery began in Naples, where I first encountered Tom of Finland through a book. That experience shaped my approach to the male nude. My practice is enriched by direct encounters with artworks and archival materials, as reflected in my dissertation in Museum Cultures on the male figure. At the Foundation, I would study Tom’s original drawings, photographs, and preparatory works to gain insight into his creative process. I am also inspired by photographs of Herb Ritts, Daniel Perry, and Aaron Cobbett, etc., making the Foundation’s wider archive an active presence during my time at Tom House.

My research stems from a desire to analyze the darkness of the night, the connections and bonds formed between the various personalities who inhabit it, and the shadows of my own soul. I would like to draw upon the archival material in your possession to create new graphic and sculptural works on handmade paper, which I would produce directly on-site (if possible). Into the paper pulp, I intend to incorporate cigar ash, fragments of flyers, and promotional material from the Los Angeles nightlife and the LGBTQIA+ community.
I aim to create artist's books made of paper and tar membrane, bound with bondage ropes and knots. The pages will feature an alternation of thoughts, drawings, and ideas conceived during the residency. I plan to produce 28 large-scale volumes (41 cm x 27 cm x 7 cm), echoing the format of the first edition of Diderot and d’Alembert’s Encyclopédie, published in the second half of the 18th century. My goal is to draw a parallel between Enlightenment Knowledge and street nightlife—life lived on the edge, between pleasure and pain, expectations and illusions that dissolve at dawn. The 'light' of culture in the form and scale of the books; the 'shadows' of desire in the content. The choice of tar membrane is a reference to the asphalt of the streets, symbolizing both the journey taken so far and the long battles that have yet to be faced.
I belong to the Leather community, and this would be an opportunity to closely study and experience the places that created and influenced the gay-leather collective imagination.
I became aware of this opportunity through the 2024 Tom of Finland Art & Culture Festival held at Berghain in Berlin; while visiting the website, I saw that it was possible to apply for a residency.

I would very much like to participate in this residency. Unfortunately, as I teach at an Art High School, I am only available during the academic break between June and August. I realize this is a narrow window, but I truly hope to be able to have this experience. My website is currently offline for renovation; however, I expect to have it back up and running within a few days. I apologize for any inconvenience.

responses.answer: analogue photography, curatorial considerations, performance art as flaneur

Born in New York to Colombian parents and based in Berlin since 2011, Nicholé Velásquez is a migrant mythographer and flâneur, expanding analogue photography into a speculative image‑language of migration and queer time. These photographs do not simply document time; they model a kind of polynomial time, in which industrial processes, diasporic trajectories and intimate emotions fold into one another. Walking becomes both method and metaphor, as city, body and camera synchronise in an ongoing lexicon of flânerie. Across exhibitions and residencies worldwide, Velásquez’s work invites viewers to inhabit displacement as a poetic, critical lens on the human condition today.

For the residency, I propose an archival engagement (40% time) that dives into Circus drawings, not only from Tom’s 1975 portfolio but also considering any other sketches, correspondence, ephemera. 

I will select highlights to share with audiences featuring a body of circa 12 works by the end of my residency. I am interested in oral histories around the archives as well. If possible, I would be glad to host a performance talk which engages audiences with the archives. This could be a presentation of selected artwork with short recording for social media.

In 2024 I met Richard Villani who invited me to artistically participate in the 40th anniversary of the Tom of Finland Foundation’s Arts & Culture Festival. Learning about my ongoing curatorial work, Richard also asked me to work on production for the festival at Halle am Berghain. It was a great inspiration to meet everyone connected to ToFF and work under his artistic direction. Durk Dehner encouraged the artist residency as a welcoming and inclusive place which made me curious to now apply.

responses.answer:
My interest in an Ecology of Migrating Identities stems already from my Colombian heritage as an American living and working in Northern Europe. Activities across languages (Spanish, German, English, with some French and Swedish), have shaped my transcultural practice. Primarily, my engagement has been image making which has brought me back to extended stays in Christiania, Stockholm and settling in Berlin since 2011. 

Since 2011, I have regularly participated with artists aged 80s-20s on ongoing intergenerational exchanges, curating different positions together with Trevor Lloyd Morgan in 2023. Curating these themes of exchanges during my curatorial fellowship (Otte1 2023-2024) in Northern Germany, I invited Astrid Proll to share her photography as a living memory as a gay woman, after extradition from the UK and incarceration in Germany. My Otte1 fellowship focused on a broader landscape of international curatorial practices by selecting and working with artists fellowships across literature, new music, sculpture and visual research. This informed my work with wearevillage, curating Instinct #16, #17 and the first solo presentation of Jasper J Mauer whilst leading the Queer Art Bridges programme. In 2024, I did production for Tom of Finland Foundation’s 40th anniversary Arts and Culture Festival held at Halle am Berghain; which included over a hundred artists under the curatorial direction of Richard Villani. This past year, 2025, I exhibited a community of about 50 artists whom I personally know as part of ‘At Least we have each other’ at Sonntags Club as a reflection on changing global political attitudes. Simultaneously this past decade, I regularly participated in a diaspora of positions at The 4th Wall in Berlin. 

All of these experiences has deepened my practice on empathetic listening, collaborating across complex environments and sparked an interest in archival/provenance research and academic writing. My project uses a critical autobiographical, life‑history approach in dialogue with the institutional archive of Brooks International Fellowship in conjunction with Fellowship in Tate Modern Curatorial and Delfina Foundation. This application is unusual, as it treats my research’s own artistic and professional trajectory as one archival trace among others in order to examine how art‑institutional archives like the Tate’s shape, preserve, and silence particular narratives.

Decades of art practice led me into research on images, photography, and to consider a shift of consciousness, uniquely expressed by a changing human condition from industrial revolutions, inclusive now, during our current so called 4th Industrial Revolution.

responses.answer:
Proposed is to explore Tom’s Circus themes as a site where I will engage my own ongoing artistic production as well as curatorial mapping that explores Tom’s resistance or engagement of Aristotelian dramaturgy (fluid identities in non-narrative acts disrupting unified plot, conventional realism, per reading Aristotle's Poetics). 

This is a residency as an immersion into the archives at TOM House, into his letters, catalogue and oral histories by those who knew him. My archival engagement would be connected to sharing ongoing encounters with Tom’s artworks inviting a wider community into talks, workshops, highlights and a new artistic production in my style.

This residency explores and traces how Tom's homoerotic acrobats might connect to early 20th-century avant-gardes (Futurism, Dada, Bauhaus, Russian theatre) that mined circus aesthetics as a "treasure trove" for radical form. Cezanne, Seurat, Picasso, and Matisse used the theme of circus performers as metaphors for marginal, mobile artistic identities testing movement, color, and bodies in extremes.

How did Tom digest his themes for the Circus? How can my ongoing art making in portraits draw inspiration from Tom’s archive as an American with Colombian Heritage? My residency positions harlequin portraits as contemporary interlocutors: experimenting with bodies, risk, care, and horizontal creativity. I am particularly inspired by mid-1970s visual upheavals where Tom’s circus and erotic play met cultural rupture.

For the residency, I propose an archival engagement (40% time) that dives into Circus drawings, sketches, correspondence, ephemera. I will select charged highlights to share with audiences featuring Tom’s tension, humor, raw queer visibility.

Planned is to dedicate about ten percent of my time for events like meetings or talks and writing could be an additional ten percent of my time at Tom House. Community Outputs (20% time) would result in talks, curated encounters or writing possible exhibition proposals spotlighting Tom's resistance to linear narrative. Shared meals and workshops would engage the Circus themes, inviting TOM House audiences and residents into dialogues together.

For New Production (40% time):  I propose works that document fresh approaches to Tom's legacy (e.g., letter exchanges), drawing from archive themes and offered to the Foundation. Specifically on my experimental portraits, I would collaborate with longtime penpal Tommy Norris (Las Vegas). 

This project fuels intergenerational listening with local LA stories inaccessible from Berlin. A small reception transforming Circus theme into living queer ecologies of erotic performance and kinship can be explored.

Liam Mackenzie (they/he)(b. 1997) is a lens-based artist who creates using photography, videography, and physical fabrication. Their work follows the act of ‘performing for the camera’ to personally, politically, and theoretically examine queerness, gender expression, bodies, disability, and sex. Using themselves as the performer and using repeating elements of exaggeration, maximalism, humor, and kink, Mackenzie embodies the varying forms people assume to subvert perceptions.

I am proposing to explore disability aesthetics and disability and how they intertwine with kink and sexuality. My perspective of disability is rooted in Disability Justice, which has connected me to disability aesthetics and influenced my perspective on my queerness. Disability aesthetics invites people to reframe disability as not an object that we removed ourselves from, but rather an element that we understand and connect to. For example, would the sculpture of Venus de Milo still be an iconic image of beauty if she had her arms? Tom of Finland offers a rich history of queer artifacts and portrayal of queer sex, which at face value, does not connect itself to disability. But through reframing the lens on the work through disability aesthetics, we can highlight the connection between Tom of Finland's work and disability. For the purpose of this project, I am focusing solely on the drawings and photographs in the archives. This project would happen in two phases: the first one would be collecting and selecting pieces from the archives, and writing a short essay to highlight the disability aesthetics and Disability Justice in the work. The 4th-6th work samples that is a part of my package reflects my experience working with and researching archival materials, and how I used those materials to make new work. The second phrase would be artistically recreating the selected work with queer disabled people as models through photography. The models would be found through social media, local Los Angeles community-based organizations, and word of mouth. Through moodboarding and planning, I aim to create a series of 10 images. The image recreations will have similar elements of the original referencial piece such as tableaus, but I will be using creative editing such as solarization edits and image manipluation. The work samples 1-3 reflect the aesthetic direction I plan to take with the recreation images. Tom of Finland's work is iconic in the realm of sex, sexuality, and queerness, and my proposed plan would connect an iconic platform to disability, a part of humanity that is underreprsented. By bridging the gap between disability and sexuality through the iconic platform, we can include disabled people in conversations about sexuality. I strongly believe that by including disability into sexuality, this would cause a new wave of sex and kink, and I hope to be a catalyst for that wave.

The archives would be fundamental for my proposed project. It would offer me a rich and deep collection of work to highlight my thesis and the references for the recreation photographs. My first instinct is to select a wide range of dates from the archives, to highlight disability aesthetics throughout history, but that is subject to change. For the subject matter of this work, I strongly believe in peer reviewing and sharing the archival materials with my disabled peers. My experience with disability is not a monolith and I need external input to inform my work.

responses.answer:
One of the main principles of Disability Justice created by the group Sins Invalid is "recognizing wholeness,” which means understanding how every aspect of a person can aid in Disability Justice. Felix Gonzalez-Torres’ Untitled (Portrait of Ross) (1991) is a time-based piece that takes the form of candy piles in corners, and audiences are encouraged to take candies to mirror Gonzalez-Torres’ partner, Ross’ weight loss in his experience with AIDS. This piece is such an iconic piece in queer history, as it encapsulates the grief people experienced during the AIDS crisis. However, there is a divide between disability and queerness, as this piece is only deemed as a queer history artifact. This piece should be a documentation of queer and disabled history, which highlights the need to reframe important works to include disability. There are a lot of parallels of the plight for justice for the LGBTQIA+ and disabled community, such as restriction on visitation rights in hospitals/institutions and police brutality. It is also important to note race and gender play a massive factor in those plights, as Black and brown communities were significantly affected by those actions. There has been a process in recognizing said wholeness, like people who are HIV+ are able to qualify for disability benefits in Canada. To continue the process of minimizing the gap between queerness and disability, there has to be a reframing of work such as Untitled (Portrait of Ross) to bring disability into queer history and spaces.

Bob Flanagan’s Visible Man (1992) is an extreme example of disability aesthetics, and it requires future examine to understand how it plays into everyday practices. Flanagan has an impressive collection of macabre, masochist, and filthy work that explores his body and cystic fibrosis, but Visible Man puts the branch of the aesthetics of human disqualification into practice. Toby Siebers argues that there is a line that is socially constructed to push people with differences, and if they have a certain list of qualities, they are rendered non-human. This label allows non-disabled people to ignore and remove themselves from disabled narratives, as it is a non-human issue. It belongs in disability aesthetics because it confronts who makes that line, and asks if that line can be moved. Visible Man is a video of Flanagan using a children's toy called The Visible Man, a translucent figurine that shows the inner workings of the human body. This toy illustrates a non-disabled body, how that body functions and operates. Flanagan uses the toy to illustrate what his disabled body goes through, such as mimicking mucus dripping from his nose and his bowel movements being unpredictable and a certain texture. Flanagan’s piece confronts the line by using an image of a “healthy” body, and turns it into a body that meets the requirements of human disqualification. The voluntary act of going past the line and rendering himself non-human confronts the systems of human disqualification, as it removes the power it has to assign that label to someone.

Fashion illustration and drawing (pen, watercolor, marker and charcoal), with research-driven couture design studies

Kadeem Alphanso Fyffe, born 1991 in Queens, New York, is a Jamaican-American, Black, queer multidisciplinary artist, fashion designer, and author based in Brooklyn, New York. A Parsons School of Design graduate, his practice explores the body as a site of power, discipline, desire, and self-sovereignty, with a focused commitment to empowering female, femme, and queer bodies through dress and visual language. Working across fashion, drawing, and mixed-media studies, he examines how Black and queer identities negotiate visibility, erotic authority, and control through posture, ritual, and self-presentation.

Fyffe has presented work during New York Fashion Week and at the Kennedy Center, with projects spanning couture and ready-to-wear fashion, as well as juried gallery exhibitions. Fyffe is represented by JABberwocky Literary Agency - he is the author of Threading the Needle (2023), a #1 New Release in Commercial Fashion (Amazon), and a frequent lecturer on fashion, identity, and creative autonomy. His work stands in dialogue with Tom of Finland’s legacy, asserting queer self-possession, freedom, and embodiment through clarity, restraint, and presence.

(MISTR)ESS is an erotic couture study that positions fashion illustration as a site of queer authority, desire, and bodily control. During the residency, I will challenge myself to produce a focused body/collection of drawings and sketches—using marker, charcoal, and black-and-white and color techniques—that depict structured luxury couture garments on gay male and queer bodies. Rooted in couture traditions, the work centers materials and symbols foundational to my aesthics and Tom of Finland’s legacy: leather, lace, hankies, buckles, harnesses, exposure, restraint, and erotic power.

As a commercial fashion designer, my practice is often shaped by expectations of propriety and marketability, which limits how directly I can engage sexuality, risk, and the exposed body. Illustration offers a necessary freedom—to reveal, exaggerate, and eroticize the body as a site of queer investigation without fear of censorship or institutional critique. This project allows me to work without compromise, using the body as both subject and structure.

(MISTR)ESS marks a deliberate return to my queer and leather roots, creating space for identities long present in my practice to take full visual precedence. Where my career as a couturier has largely been devoted to dressing classy ladies and wives, (MISTR)ESS asserts the mistress—and/or the mister—as figures of control, desire, and self-possession. The work embraces eroticism not as spectacle, but as discipline, authorship, and clarity.

Sexuality, identity, Blackness, and queerness are inseparable from my creative practice due to my intersectional identity. Many artists seek the Tom of Finland Foundation residency because their current environments do not permit full erotic expression - this rings ture for me. (MISTR)ESS uses this space to create an erotic couture collection without compromise—honoring Tom of Finland’s legacy not through imitation, but through freedom, rigor, and unapologetic queer self-authorship.

I plan to engage the Tom of Finland Foundation archives as a space for close looking, reflection, and research-driven creation. I am interested in studying original artworks, photographs, letters, and ephemera to understand how queer erotic power is communicated through posture, proportion, material, and restraint. Research is central to my practice, and I intentionally reflect archival study back into my collections and visual studies. The archives will help ensure the work developed during the residency is clear, intentional, and well researched, informing both the symbolic language and compositional discipline of the final body of work.

I learned about the Tom of Finland Foundation Artist-in-Residence Program through social media and conversations within my creative and queer communities in New York and Los Angeles, particularly among artists involved in the leather and BDSM scene. I was also encouraged to apply by David Aldea, Interim Executive Director of the Foundation, who is a long-time friend and mentor, and felt the residency strongly aligned with my practice.

Yes! Tom of Finland’s drawings were among the first images I encountered as a young gay person, living in Durham(NC), that allowed me to recognize myself without shame. Before I had language for my sexuality, I could point to those images and understand that my identity existed, that desire could be powerful rather than hidden. That early encounter shaped my journey into sexual self-awareness, liberation and autonomy, then eventually my involvement in the leather community in New York City.

Engaging with this legacy now, as a Black artist and fashion designer, feels both meaningful and necessary - especially in this current political climate, where artisitc rebellion is critical. I am excited to bring a perspective that is informed by Blackness, queerness, and couture traditions—expanding the visual conversation around erotic power, representation, and authorship. Approaching this work through a fashion/couture lens allows me to contribute a distinct, contemporary voice to a lineage that has deeply informed my personal and creative evolution. In the future, I can envision myself ast the Creative Director of Tom of Finland - this project is the launching point!

Robert Andy Coombs is an American photographer and artist whose work confronts disability, sexuality, and visibility through an unapologetically intimate lens. A gay power wheelchair user, Coombs photographs from within his lived experience, creating images that challenge cultural assumptions about desirability, access, and autonomy. His practice blends street photography, portraiture, and Polaroid to document queer life, intimacy, and public space from a vantage point rarely seen yet deeply felt.

Coombs holds a BFA from Kendall College of Art and Design and an MFA from the Yale School of Art. He has taught at the University of Miami, lectured at Harvard University, and received the United States Artist Award. His work has been exhibited internationally and is included in the Getty Museum publication and exhibition *Queer Lens*. Through projects such as *CripFag* and *Traversing Pride*, Coombs creates work that is sensual, political, and fiercely human, insisting on disabled queer bodies not as symbols, but as subjects with agency, desire, and power.

My proposed residency project expands my long-term body of work CripFag into a site-specific investigation of queer disabled desire within the historic Tom House. Tom of Finland established one of the most powerful visual languages of queer erotic fantasy, shaping how generations understand masculinity, sex, and visibility. Yet the bodies that formed this canon were overwhelmingly able bodied. This project asks what it means for disabled queer bodies to claim that erotic lineage not as guests, but as inheritors.

As a gay power wheelchair user, my practice is grounded in lived embodiment. I photograph from the height and orientation of my own body, creating images that are intimate, confrontational, and deeply aware of access, vulnerability, and desire. At Tom House, I will produce a new body of Polaroids and large format photographic prints centered on myself and a constellation of queer, masculine, disabled, non-disabled, and gender fluid subjects I find compelling. These works will depict participants inhabiting the house as erotic protagonists, reimagining Tom’s visual mythology through contemporary bodies, identities, and embodiments.

The residency will culminate in a site-responsive exhibition composed primarily of Polaroids and large format prints. The project will remain intentionally open to new and unforeseen forms that may emerge through sustained engagement with the Tom of Finland Foundation archive, the historic architecture of Tom House, and the creative and erotic energy of greater Los Angeles. This openness allows the residency to function not only as a production period, but as a generative space where new visual languages can organically develop in response to place, history, and community.

The Tom of Finland Foundation has long championed my work, has included it in their permanent archive, and has consistently shared my images as part of their public commitment to representing queer disability. The Artist in Residence program now presents a critical opportunity to extend that relationship beyond representation into sustained institutional action. While the Foundation has meaningfully supported trans and gender expansive artists, disabled queer artists have not yet been centered within the AiR canon. This residency would formally place disabled queer desire into the living lineage of artists who shape Tom House, expanding its canon in a way that reflects the full complexity of contemporary queer life.

This project is not a request for symbolic inclusion. It is a call for structural presence. I am ready to produce work that honors the Foundation’s legacy while extending it forward. The time is not approaching. It is here.

I plan to engage the Foundation’s archive through both direct study and personal exchange with the people who knew Tom and who continue to steward his legacy. Beyond closely examining his original drawings, photographs, letters, and ephemera, I am especially interested in learning from those who hold embodied knowledge of his life, working methods, and artistic nuances. These conversations, alongside sustained immersion in Tom’s imagery, will inform how I translate his visual language, compositional codes, and erotic mythologies into new photographic work rooted in contemporary queer embodiment.

I was first introduced to the Tom of Finland Foundation while I was studying at the Yale School of Art, when the Foundation reached out to include my work in their annual exhibition. That initial invitation began an ongoing relationship, leading to my inclusion in their permanent archive and continued engagement with my practice over the years. The Artist in Residence program feels like a natural continuation of that long standing connection and shared commitment to advancing bold, uncensored queer art.

I am a power wheelchair user and will be traveling with my roommate, who is also my primary full time caregiver. Funding to support their presence throughout the residency will be necessary. I will also require a photography assistant, and I have several candidates based in the Los Angeles area who would be well suited to support the production of the project.

I am enthusiastic about working collaboratively with the Foundation as part of a pilot framework to help develop more accessible residency practices for future artists who use wheelchairs. The residency does not need to be perfectly accessible. What is essential is access that allows me to live and work comfortably and independently.

Ideally, this would include accessible living quarters for my caregiver and myself, preferably in separate rooms located near Tom House, access to the main floor of Tom House to engage with the archives, access to the backyard and studio spaces for photographic production, and, if possible, an accessible rental van to support offsite shoots and outings.

These access considerations are fundamental to my ability to fully participate in and complete the proposed project while also contributing to the Foundation’s long term accessibility development.

Miguel Argenta (2001) explores the overproduction of images and the ways in which they shape our perception of the body, desire, and memory. Rather than offering a closed—utopian or dystopian—reading of visual contemporaneity, his practice appropriates the excesses and residues of visual culture, transforming them into poetic and critical material. Since 2019, his work has focused on queer memory and processes of symbolic resignification from an affective, dissident perspective.
Working through intervened photography, collage, installation, performance, and drawing, Argenta constructs spaces of ambiguity in which hybridity and monstrosity operate as forms of resistance. His works require slow and attentive observation, revealing ghostly apparitions and blurred figures that destabilize fixed identities. These visual strategies question the boundaries between the human and the non-human, as well as between the intimate and the political. At the core of his practice lies a desire to challenge binary conceptions of gender, proposing the body as a territory in constant transformation.

This residency project expands my ongoing research on pornography through a new body of work developed in dialogue with the Tom of Finland Foundation. Using a personal archive of queer pornographic magazines (1970–1996), I will intervene, transfer, and recompose their images onto rigid, fragmented surfaces. These publications functioned not only as erotic material but as infrastructures of queer sociability, visibility, and resistance before the internet. By transforming disposable prints into archaeological, fractured objects, the project reframes pornography as cultural heritage and affective pedagogy, activating queer memory through material tension, erosion, and visual resilience.

Access to the Tom of Finland Foundation’s archive is essential to my project. Its unique collection of artworks, documents, and queer erotic ephemera will situate my research within a broader genealogy of queer representation, informing my investigation of pornography as cultural archive and political infrastructure while shaping the conceptual, material, and aesthetic dimensions of the work.

I learned about the Tom of Finland Artist-in-Residence Program through Instagram, where the Foundation shared information about the open call. Following the Tom of Finland Foundation on social media has allowed me to stay connected to its activities, exhibitions, and residency opportunities, and to better understand its commitment to preserving and activating queer erotic archives. Encountering the residency through this platform felt coherent with my own research interests, as social media continues to function as a contemporary space for the circulation of images, community-building, and the dissemination of queer cultural practices.
I do not have any special requirements or needs during the residency. I have no mobility, accessibility, or dietary restrictions.

In addition to my proposed project, I would like to highlight my broader interest in portraiture as a critical tool for exploring processes of appropriation, de-iconization, and symbolic re-signification within contemporary queer imaginaries. My work examines how visual symbols traditionally associated with masculinity—such as gestures, postures, or iconographic markers—are destabilized when embodied by non-normative bodies, generating new symbolic tensions charged with political meaning.

Drawing on feminist historiography and theories of de-iconization, particularly those developed in the 1980s and by Estrella de Diego, my practice investigates how symbols lose their hegemonic authority when displaced from the center to the margins. Rather than destroying iconography, my work seeks to transform it through re-appropriation. I am especially interested in practices such as cross-dressing, where dominant symbols are reincarnated in dissident bodies, producing new forms of reading. Ultimately, my work understands re-signification as a continuous, cyclical process that bridges cultural memory and present queer identities.

Simón Malvaez’s artistic journey is deeply influenced by the rich cultural landscapes of the places he has lived, including his formative years in Tijuana, time spent in Mexico City, and five years in San Francisco, before settling in Brooklyn. 

His work is an intersection of design and art, rooted in the Bauhaus philosophy, which emphasizes the harmonious integration of form and function.

Simón's art reflects his vibrant Latino heritage and his identity within the LGBTQ+ community. Using basic materials and familiar geometric shapes, he works with primary colors, pastels, and metallics to create pieces that are bold and dynamic. 

His approach celebrates bodies and personalities that have shaped his experiences, offering a visual narrative that speaks to identity, diversity, and inclusion.

Sea Dax is an interdisciplinary artist and maker who currently lives and works in Cincinnati, Ohio. He acquired his BFA at the Art Academy of Cincinnati in 2012. Dax has exhibited in local galleries including The Carnegie, Wavepool Gallery, the Contemporary Arts Center, and has exhibited internationally at Artemesia Gallery in Windsor, VIC, Australia. He is in the process of acquiring his MFA from the University of Cincinnati DAAP School of Art. His current body of work explores queer and trans objects, and the function of archive in relation to trans representation within art historical contexts.

My practice is built on a lifelong fear of forgetting and a fascination with the
unreliable nature of memory. I see remembering as both a powerful act of agency and sometimes also a mess to make sense of. Within my practice I draw from art history, queer and trans archives, folk art traditions, zine/diy culture, personal ephemera, and
spirituality to emphasize what is missing and what could be imagined, with a heavy
focus on observational painting and object collection.
My current body of work engages art history as a resource and an obstacle,
focusing on the covert ways trans lives have been recorded (and often omitted) within
art historical and broader cultural contexts. I look to the archives to attempt to locate myself within history, community, and what I call “transcestry.” I reimagine these archives through inserting what I call “trans objects” (sharps containers, HRT supplies, binders, intracommunally understood trans objects, etc.) and other queer coded items into still life settings. It’s important to me to respond to the absence of overt transness available within the art historical archive, and allow myself to reinvent, speculate, and fabricate what I think a still life that made space for trans life would look like. Through my work I hope to empower trans people to see themselves as active participants in the making of future histories, and to claim space for our presence across time. 
I have long been a fan of Tom’s work – as a trans man, a leatherman, and as an artist. With my time at the Tom of Finland AIR, I would be very interested in exploring the archives. If possible, I’d like to create still lives that includes items from the archive within my paintings, and/or do studies of objects that exist within the archive, if this is possible. Infusing historical objects from queer history into my works is very important to me, and this would be so special to me to be able to work directly with objects from the collection. I also would hope to get lots of time with the archives, as I am a big queer history buff, and so much of my research is focused on queer and trans contributions to art. I am also drawn to the idea of sharing space with other queer artists, as I live in the Midwest and queer specific arts spaces are few and far between.

I would like to include aspects of the archives within still life works I create, as described above. I have an ever-expanding project of queer and trans still life paintings I am currently working on, and being able to spend time with Tom's archive, and include some of these historically significant objects in some of my paintings would be an absolute honor.
A friend sent it to me on Instagram.

If it is not possible for me to work directly with objects from the archive, I am adaptable. If that means drawing only, or documenting objects in the archive to reference outside of the archive, etc. That is also okay. Working directly with these objects is the most appealing, but getting to spend time with them at all is such a privilege and I can work with you however needed.

ALSO - I can make any of the Lawton's Lookout time slots that I selected work, but June-August would be most preferred. Thanks so much for considering me!

I am a Berlin-based artist with a BFA from the Gerrit Rietveld Academy. Working across multimedia practices including painting, photography, installation, and poetry, my work focuses on feminism, queer spaces, cruising areas, neglected societies, and lands. My practice is research-based, often examining cruising areas as political and social sites. Today, I work as both an artist and a curator.

Working title: "Public Desires, Private Archives". 

For the Tom of Finland Residency in Los Angeles, I propose a research-based, multimedia project that examines gay cruising areas as political, social, and erotic sites, placing them in dialogue with the Tom of Finland Archive. The project explores how cruising spaces—parks, paths, beaches, and marginal urban zones—function as informal queer architectures shaped by desire, risk, resistance, and community.

Rooted in my ongoing practice, this project treats cruising not merely as sexual behavior, but as a method of occupying space under conditions of exclusion, and as a form of queer knowledge production. By engaging the Tom of Finland Foundation’s archive, I aim to trace how erotic imagery, fantasy, and masculinity intersect with lived spatial practices of cruising across different historical and geographical contexts.

Through this residency, I seek to ask:
How do cruising areas function as feminist and queer counter-spaces?
How does erotic representation shape our understanding of public space?
In what ways can archives of erotic art illuminate overlooked social histories embedded in landscapes?

The Tom of Finland Archive will serve as a conceptual and visual anchor for the project. I plan to study:

Representations of outdoor, liminal, and subcultural spaces in the archive

The relationship between masculinity, anonymity, and power

How erotic fantasy reclaims bodies and spaces historically marked by shame or danger

Rather than illustrating the archive directly, I will use it as a parallel structure, a historical counterpoint to contemporary cruising sites, particularly in and around Los Angeles.

I would like to use my voice and body in new ways that engage the public and that are charged by erotic exploration both solo and collaborative. Figuring out how to be of service to others with that art and make it into rituals about releasing and receiving is of most interest to me. 

I'd like to immerse myself in the foundation, its network, and queer Los Angeles sex, art, and entertainment. Filtered into late night/early morning meditation and songwriting, beds of tracks will be made to improvise on with meditatively repeated (sung) phrases. "The past won't get better" (for example) might be used to discover new music and evoke feelings within audiences/visitors.

I'll sing, improvise, and repeat those phrases and/or additional song structures for a block of hours without leaving my place. I'll lie down while singing, vulnerably in meditation as a naked altar. Audiences will write down prayers, confessions, and things they want to release. They'd lay them on my body and seal them by dripping wax on me from burning candles. 

That is just one idea for performance art exploration as ritual. Incorporating elements like shibari, being painted/written on, and elements of intimacy with strangers and other artists all intrigue me. The workshopping, research, and performance of these would be captured through photograph, film and voice notes. I'd love to make a documentary about the performances and my time in L.A. Interviewing artists and new friends/hookups/muses about why they think eroticism is often not looked at as art and the roles of religion and shame is great research and material for the documentary. It would be great to work with the foundation and other artists to help make that happen, but I will use my phone, field recorder and laptop to capture things. 

I also see the opportunity to perform with a full band or through cabaret performances. I'd introduce my new musical discoveries and other original music through rock/dance shows in unconventional spaces like queer kink and fetish clubs. Audiences would be free to explore intimacy and sexual exploration while an erotically charged punk performance is taking place and sharing live performance and sexual energy in that way excites me.

Yves Klein's live paintings intrigue me too but with through live intimacy another subject and paint. This would be set up as a ritual too. A work(s) of art are made and for sale (painting, film, photography)... But a live performance and the shared time and space of events like that have the power to open worlds for artist and audiences alike. 

Queer sex is holy.


Madonna from 1990-1996ish is also a huge inspiration for these ideas, as are artists 
Marina Abramović, Grace Jones, Robert Mapplethorpe, Joseph Beuys, and of course... Prince.

I will absorb as much as I can, but I am very intrigued by the archives of photography, magazines, videos and anything that may have a relationship to iconoclasm, esotericism and spirituality. Sexuality as spiritual liberation and as a healing force/power through artistic expression are of great interests to me as a survivor of sexual trauma. My music, performance and visuals have allowed me to find authentic sexual power and that discovery is perpetual. Your archives seem like pretty fun places to continue my discovery and to awaken more to my sexual powers and their potency.

As a Black and gender-fluid artist it took me a long time to find myself in gay erotica. But I have been able to see so much new and uncovered representation over the past few years. It has helped me let go of limiting beliefs about the beauty and worth of my big lips, dreadlocks, black skin and dick. When I think of Tom of Finland, now, there is a resonance with an energy of liberation that is very pure and touching. There is a light in what is revealed by Tom's work that makes queer life, especially in the Midwest, a lot less dark. I saw the call on IG today, it aligned, and your foundation is a perfect fit/support for freaks like me!
I'm allergic to mangos and everybody tells me that sucks. No mangoes for me and no special requirements.

While this opportunity does seem perfect for me, I do gig work and have been creating art full time for over two years. Any assistance to offset the costs of the residency or pay them in full would be such a blessing. I'd also be grateful for any help with getting to and back home from LA (maybe I'll just stay... get me out of Missouri...). Financial assistance  is not a requirement, but a scholarship would be a huge help to me. If there is a way to offer mutual aid in some way through any work you might have to cover costs it'd be great to intern/work study too.

responses.answer: I am a multidisciplinary artist, working in media such as sculpture, architecture, design and performance.

Omar Miranda is a Cuban architect, artist, and designer based in Madrid. His practice moves fluidly between these disciplines, combining critical analysis with a strong sensitivity to materiality. He has been recognized with some Spanish Architecture Award, and has collaborated on collections awarded with Mercedes-Benz Fashion Talent.
His work investigates the relationship between body, space, and object from a poetic and contemporary perspective, exploring how these elements are mutually activated. In Madrid, he developed GUARIDA: a series of architectural projects conceived for queer people, also Glory Hole: sculptural furniture for domestic spaces activated by the body, exploring sex and pleasure.
He has designed objects for fashion brands and developed projects such as Sweat Lamp in collaboration with Rubén Gómez, where he explores the materiality of bodily fluids. His research extends to sculptural works presented in the exhibition Accustomed to Contact. In 2025, he presented the performance Fitting Room at Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week Madrid.

Latin Lover: flashing lights, bodies that touch each other
This project is conceived as a video work accompanied by a series of sculptures. As a Caribbean artist living far from my country, I am interested in exploring the development of the eroticism of the Caribbean body in other contexts, such as Europe and North America. I am also interested in how this eroticism fluctuates across gender gradients, and in ideas of masculinity and femininity within a collective or within a body read as masculine, like my own—how it is projected outward and how it coexists socially in different ways across the Caribbean, Europe, and North America.
I am interested in exploring these variations of masculinity and femininity as tools of resistance, but also of survival, and in investigating how music acts as a catalyst for shared erotic spaces, as a stage for seduction and bodily communication. I aim to materialize a project in which the body becomes a transcriber of erotic languages, communication, and care for a community that increasingly needs to remain united around shared values.
During the residency, the idea is to find a space and, within it, investigate movement and costume, and through music develop a choreography that can be recorded as a video art piece of my body in that space. Through choreography, layers of costume are gradually revealed, one after another: from a superficial layer, such as the suit of an ultra-masculine man, to leather pieces referencing the leather scene or feminine lingerie. These layers are unveiled over time through choreography and performance.
As another outcome to accompany the video, I would like to work on a series of sculptures resulting from an ongoing investigation into how the movements generated by the dancing body produce organic, erotic, and meaning-laden objects. The materiality is yet to be discovered and should be evoked by the space and the work within it; it may include the fabrics from the costumes or residual materials from the site (soil, glass, plastics, plants).
As a queer person born in the 1990s in Cuba, I was subjected during my childhood to a militarization and masculinization of my gestures. With this project, I would like to radically investigate how music and a queer context expand the body toward other forms of movement and closeness to others—something I have been developing for years but have not yet been able to fully materialize as an artistic project.

The classic image of a Latin Lover immediately conjures up images of muscular men with ambiguous sexuality. I believe your Foundation is the ideal place for me to immerse myself in a world where I can explore, through your archive, forms and practices of queerness, from movements and clothing, gestures, mannerisms, and physical intimacy. I am also interested in the stories of queer childhoods, like mine, that have been re-educated by political and social contexts, as well as stories of people who have reclaimed their bodies within a radical artistic practice

I have several friends and acquaintances who have collaborated with the Foundation at different times, such as Antoni Hervás, Clara López Menéndez, Luis Venegas, Carlos Enfedaque (…) and I have also been following the Foundation's work for years because of its importance and the work they do internationally in our community
I would need some workspace to be able to work on the sculptures.

Hey, I’m Pat, and I’m an artist. I’ve been making art since I was around seven, and it has been a part of my life ever since. Throughout primary school, I took numerous art classes. Eventually, in my senior year, I began taking art more seriously and spent more time in the art rooms, refining my craft and honing my skills as an artist.
After a couple of years of traveling and not creating art, I am now working on a series of portraits of leather men in my area. I’ve been creating these portraits mainly for self-exploration, and it has become a personal interest of mine. I upload the finished works to my website’s blog ("Series") and share the process there. I also mainly post my series on my social media accounts, Instagram and Facebook. I am almost done with my series. I have one more portrait left, and then the next chapter of my erotic artwork will commence.

I have a style that's very unique to me where I have some of the piece in black and white, and the rest is in full color. I've recently finished a piece of a friend of mine and his current partner kissing. The couple is in full color, and the leather is in black and white. I made this piece really to show their love for each other, but (to me) what ended up happening it showed the real person behind the leather. So I want to work on more leather men and women in the area with this style of mixing color realism and black and gray. 

The ink drawings that I've recently been working on are a new fascination. I recently studied anatomy and have been practicing everyday. While showing my progress on social media. I've then gotten into dip pens and inks, so I've added the two together, and I am now able to draw from my head and my own erotic fantasies. Their very simple drawings. This is something I would like to improve on. With more anatomy studies at the Tom house, and utilize the archive for as well. 

There is also another project that's erotic, but also is a "public service announcement." It talks about drug abuse and addiction. Showing a model, me, overdosed on the toilet. Now this sounds very gruesome, but the message of drug abuse and addiction would hit the viewer hard and very in your face. Sort of facing one's fears. This is a piece that I'm making regardless of getting into the residency. That's how good the idea is.

Another smaller project that I can work on is my Tom of Finland Outfit. Now, please don't sue me. I've been working on this jacket since December, and I love Tom so much. I'm good friends with Cockeye Kink(Clair), and I can't afford any his Tom of Finland Gear. I'm sorry, but I'm not spending $800 on a jacket. So I bought a white leather jacket, some leather paint and varnish, and started painting different scenes from Tom's pen and ink drawings. Basically, collaging different scenes from his comics and combining them all into a leather jacket and pants(just need to order the pants). This'll be iconic as I don't know anyone who is doing this with leather or even leather paint.

My engagement with the archive will be learning more about the queer art history. As that will inspire the fire within me to keep going. That's what Tom did for me two years ago. Also I would love to sit at one of the live drawing sessions that the Tom house hosts. As that will trendily help me with learning the male form, and improve my drawing from life ability. I don't normally draw from life and I would love to sit and draw a muscle man and study their muscles. 

Also donating time to my craft will help me as well. As  I work a fulltime warehouse job. Making art prints for other artists. We print on paper canvas and metal.

I've been staring at Tom's work since I was thirteen years old, and now at the old age of 28 years old. Tom's work is more inspirational if anything allowing me to be me. I've fell back into art with Tom's work, and when I looked more into his work I found this website and the archive. Which then lead me to the residency. I've also have seen the posts on Instagram and Facebook about the applications and I had many friends tell me to apply. So I applied, and every year I'm applying. Even if i get in once I'm still applying.

The only special requirement is water. I mainly drink water. I shouldn't drink soda or any acidic drinks due to a kidney disease. I also cannot smoke, of any kind, or drunk any alcohol or liquor. I'm making it sound worse than it is. It has gotten better since I was diagnosed about five years ago. I'm on three medications for just my blood pressure and kidney.

Other than maybe connecting me to models, I've told you everything that I have planned. 

Although... If I get picked, I want Durk Dehner to call me, and I want him to tell me that I got picked for the Artist-in-Residency. My number is 610-613-7801.
Some of my process reels are on my website, but mainly on my Instagram.
My writing samples are found in my blog on my website. Check the Series tab and each portrait I talk about how I met the model, the interview, photoshoot, and also the process.

responses.answer: Cyanotype photography, installation, scent-based elements, archival research, writing, and socially engaged artistic practice.

I am a Texas-based artist, curator, and writer working at the intersection of queer history, sensory memory, and material culture. My practice centers on cyanotype photography, scent, and archival inquiry as tools for exploring pleasure, intimacy, and cultural survival within queer and Latinx communities. I approach making as a form of research—blending photographic processes, plant-based materials, and writing to trace how desire and domestic ritual are preserved across time.

I am the founder of Barrio Pop Studio, an art and apothecary project rooted in olfactory storytelling, and the curator of Scent Fair Austin, an exhibition platform exploring scent as contemporary art. My work is informed by institutional training in museums and archives, alongside community-centered approaches to care, touch, and embodiment. Through my practice, I seek to activate archives as living sites—spaces where erotic memory, personal ephemera, and pleasure function as radical forms of knowledge.

My proposed project builds upon a cyanotype diptych series initiated during my first visit to Tom of Finland House in October 2025, following an introduction to artist-in-residence Onur Hüstürk. Together, we created cyanotypes in the garden that responded to the site as a contemporary “pleasure park”—a living landscape shaped by queer presence, intimacy, and communal care.

During the residency, I propose to expand this series into a larger body of work that engages the house, gardens, and archives as interconnected spaces of erotic memory. Using cyanotype—a process historically linked to early photography, domestic science, and botanical documentation—I will produce site-responsive works that incorporate shadows, gestures, plant matter, and found forms drawn from the house and grounds. These images will function as quiet records of queer leisure and desire, emphasizing slowness, touch, and presence rather than spectacle.

The project is conceptually anchored in the idea of the “pleasure park” as both fantasy and archive: a place where erotic expression unfolds through repetition, ritual, and collective imagination. I am particularly interested in how Tom of Finland’s world-building—his attention to environment, posture, costume, and gaze—can be translated into non-figurative, atmospheric images that honor erotic lineage without direct representation.

In addition to visual work, I plan to produce a short written reflection or artist text responding to the residency, situating the cyanotypes within broader conversations around queer archives, ephemera, and embodied knowledge. The residency would allow me to deepen this inquiry through sustained time, access to archival materials, and engagement with the house as both historical site and living community space.

I plan to engage the archives as a generative research tool, focusing on photographs, personal ephemera, and environmental details that reflect how pleasure, leisure, and domestic space operate within Tom of Finland’s world. Rather than direct reproduction, I will use the archives to inform compositional choices, gestures, and atmospheres within my cyanotypes—translating archival intimacy into material abstraction. The archives will also inform my written reflections, helping situate the work within a lineage of queer erotic art that privileges care, fantasy, and collective memory.

I first learned about the residency through my visit to Tom of Finland House in October 2025, during a tour that introduced me to the Foundation’s mission and ongoing artist residency program. That visit—and my subsequent collaboration with the then artist-in-residence—deepened my interest in the house as a site of active artistic exchange rather than solely an archive. Conversations with artists connected to the Foundation, along with my broader curatorial and archival research practice, reinforced the residency as a meaningful context for expanding my work.
No special requirements at this time.

This project grows from a genuine, prior relationship with the house and its community. Returning to expand this work feels like a continuation rather than a beginning—an opportunity to deepen trust, research, and material experimentation within a space that already holds personal and creative significance for me.

Graphite, ink, colored pencil, and digital illustration focused on queer masculinity, uniform culture, and erotic subcultures.

My name is Touko Laaksonen, and I’m a Finnish-born queer artist exploring the intersections of masculinity, desire, and fantasy. My work often plays with archetypes of strength and authority, reframing them through tenderness and humor. I’m interested in how queer communities use imagery to create spaces of freedom, recognition, and erotic imagination. Through drawing and digital illustration, I seek to build worlds that honor queer resilience and pleasure, while challenging limiting notions of who gets to embody power or beauty.

I plan to use the archives as a way to study the evolution of queer visual language — from intimate sketches to staged reference photographs to fully realized drawings. I’m particularly interested in the materials that reveal process: how composition, gesture, and stylization emerge from observation and desire.

I propose to develop a new drawing series that examines how queer masculinities have been coded, staged, and mythologized over time. Using the Foundation’s archive as a starting point, I will study photographs, sketches, and personal materials to understand how gesture, posture, and costume functioned within queer subcultures. My project will reinterpret these gestures through contemporary subjects, creating drawings that bridge historical erotic languages with today’s diverse queer bodies and communities.
